    # Login to a registry docker login # Docker Hub (prompts for credentials) docker login myregistry.azurecr.io # Custom registry # Tag an image for pushing docker tag my-app:latest myregistry.io/my-app:v1.0.0 # Push image to registry docker push myregistry.io/my-app:v1.0.0 # Pull image from registry docker pull myregistry.io/my-app:v1.0.0 # Search Docker Hub for images docker search nginx # Logout from registry docker logout myregistry.azurecr.io

    Image Security Scanning:

    Modern registries provide vulnerability scanning—analyzing images for known security issues (CVEs). Docker Hub, ECR, GCR, and ACR all offer automated scanning. Always review scan results before deploying to production.

    🔒 Registry Security Best Practices
    • Use private registries for proprietary applications
    • Enable vulnerability scanning on all images
    • Implement image signing and verification (Docker Content Trust)
    • Use least-privilege access controls (IAM roles, service accounts)
    • Regularly update base images to patch vulnerabilities
    • Scan images in CI pipeline before deployment

    2.8 Docker Best Practices & Performance Optimization

    Building containers is easy. Building production-ready, secure, performant containers requires discipline and best practices. This section covers essential guidelines that separate hobbyist Docker usage from professional DevOps engineering.

    Dockerfile Optimization Strategies:

    Production Dockerfile Best Practices

    1. Use Official Base Images

    Start with official images (python:3.11, node:20, nginx:1.25) from Docker Hub's "library" namespace. They're maintained by dedicated teams, regularly updated for security patches, and optimized for size and performance. Avoid random community images.

    2. Minimize Layer Count

    Each RUN, COPY, ADD instruction creates a new layer. Combine related commands into single RUN statements using && to reduce layer count. Example: RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y package1 package2 && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/*

    3. Order Instructions by Changeability

    Place instructions that rarely change (base image, system dependencies) at the top. Place frequently changing instructions (COPY application code) at the bottom. This maximizes Docker's layer caching, making builds faster by reusing unchanged layers.

    4. Use .dockerignore

    Create a .dockerignore file to exclude unnecessary files from build context (node_modules, .git, logs, test files). Smaller build context = faster builds and smaller images. Works like .gitignore but for Docker.

    5. Don't Run as Root

    Containers run as root by default—a security risk. Create a non-root user in your Dockerfile and switch to it with USER instruction. If compromised, attacker has limited privileges instead of root access to container.

    6. Use Multi-Stage Builds (Advanced Preview)

    Build your application in one stage (with all build tools), then copy only the compiled artifact to a minimal runtime stage. Result: tiny production images without build dependencies. We'll explore this deeply in Week 3.

    Example: Optimized Python Application Dockerfile

    # Use official Python slim image (smaller than full Python) FROM python:3.11-slim # Set working directory WORKDIR /app # Install system dependencies (rarely change, cache this layer) RUN apt-get update && apt-get install -y \ gcc \ && rm -rf /var/lib/apt/lists/* # Copy requirements first (changes less often than code) COPY requirements.txt . # Install Python dependencies (cached if requirements.txt unchanged) RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt # Copy application code (changes frequently, goes last) COPY . . # Create non-root user and switch to it RUN useradd -m -u 1000 appuser && chown -R appuser:appuser /app USER appuser # Expose port EXPOSE 8000 # Run application CMD ["python", "app.py"]

    Container Resource Management:

    # Limit CPU and memory usage docker run -d \ --name myapp \ --cpus="1.5" \ # Limit to 1.5 CPU cores --memory="512m" \ # Limit to 512MB RAM --memory-swap="1g" \ # Total memory + swap = 1GB myapp:latest # Set CPU priority (relative weight) docker run -d \ --name high-priority-app \ --cpu-shares=1024 \ # Higher = more CPU time when contention myapp:latest # Monitor container resource usage docker stats # Real-time stats for all containers docker stats myapp # Stats for specific container
    ⚡ Performance Tips
    • Always set resource limits in production to prevent runaway containers
    • Use alpine-based images for minimal footprint (python:3.11-alpine vs python:3.11)
    • Clean up package manager caches in same RUN command that installs packages
    • Avoid installing "recommended" packages: apt-get install --no-install-recommends
    • Use specific version tags for reproducible builds
    • Regularly update base images for security patches and performance improvements

    Hands-On Labs

    Now it's time to apply what you've learned! These five labs build progressively from basic container operations to multi-container applications. Complete each lab in order, as later labs build on earlier concepts.

    ⚠️ Lab Prerequisites

    Before starting, ensure Docker Desktop is installed and running. Verify with docker --version and docker ps. You'll need a stable internet connection for pulling images from Docker Hub. Budget 4-6 hours to complete all five labs.

    Lab 1: Your First Docker Container

    Objective: Pull an official nginx image, run it as a container, access the web server, and understand basic container lifecycle commands.

    Learning Goals:

    • Understand image pull and container run workflow
    • Practice port mapping for accessing containerized services
    • Learn basic container management commands
    Navigate to your lab working directory:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course/week-02-docker-fundamentals/lab-01-first-container

    Every file you create in this lab goes into this directory. Stay here for all remaining steps unless a step explicitly tells you to change directory.

    Search for nginx image on Docker Hub:
    docker search nginx # Identify the official nginx image (should be first result)
    Pull the official nginx image:
    docker pull nginx:latest # This downloads all layers of the nginx image

    Verify: Run docker images to see the downloaded image with its size and ID.

    Run nginx container with port mapping:
    docker run -d --name my-nginx -p 8080:80 nginx:latest # -d: Run in background (detached mode) # --name: Friendly name for the container # -p 8080:80: Map host port 8080 to container port 80
    Verify container is running:
    docker ps # Should show my-nginx container with STATUS "Up"
    Access the web server:

    Open your browser and navigate to http://localhost:8080. You should see the nginx welcome page.

    Alternatively, use curl: curl http://localhost:8080

    Inspect container logs:
    docker logs my-nginx # Shows nginx access logs and error logs
    Execute commands inside the running container:
    docker exec -it my-nginx bash # Opens an interactive bash shell inside the container # Inside the container, explore: ls /usr/share/nginx/html # Default nginx web root cat /etc/nginx/nginx.conf # Nginx configuration exit # Exit the container shell
    Stop and remove the container:
    docker stop my-nginx # Gracefully stop the container docker ps -a # Show all containers (including stopped) docker rm my-nginx # Remove the stopped container docker ps -a # Verify it's gone
    ✅ Lab Success Criteria

    You've completed this lab when: (1) You can access nginx welcome page at localhost:8080, (2) You can view container logs, (3) You can execute commands inside the running container, (4) You successfully stop and remove the container.

    Lab 2: Building Your First Custom Image

    Objective: Create a Dockerfile for a simple Python web application, build a custom Docker image, and run it as a container.

    Learning Goals:

    • Write a production-ready Dockerfile
    • Understand the image build process and layer caching
    • Practice image tagging and versioning
    Navigate to your lab working directory:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course/week-02-docker-fundamentals/lab-02-custom-image

    Every file you create in this lab goes into this directory. Stay here for all remaining steps unless a step explicitly tells you to change directory.

    Create project directory and application code:
    mkdir ~/docker-lab-app && cd ~/docker-lab-app # Create a simple Flask application cat > app.py << 'EOF' from flask import Flask import socket app=Flask(__name__) @app.route('/') def hello(): hostname=socket.gethostname() return f"

    Hello from Docker!

    Container hostname: {hostname}

    " if __name__ == '__main__': app.run(host='0.0.0.0', port=5000) EOF
    Create requirements.txt:
    cat > requirements.txt << 'EOF' flask==3.0.0 EOF
    Create a Dockerfile:
    cat > Dockerfile << 'EOF' # Use official Python slim image FROM python:3.11-slim # Set working directory WORKDIR /app # Copy requirements and install dependencies COPY requirements.txt . RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt # Copy application code COPY app.py . # Expose port EXPOSE 5000 # Run the application CMD ["python", "app.py" ] EOF
    Build the Docker image:
    docker build -t my-flask-app:v1.0 . # -t: Tag the image with name and version # .: Build context (current directory) # Watch the build process—each step creates a layer
    Verify the image was created:
    docker images | grep my-flask-app # Should show my-flask-app with tag v1.0
    Run a container from your custom image:
    docker run -d --name flask-container -p 5000:5000 my-flask-app:v1.0
    Test the application:
    curl http://localhost:5000 # Should display HTML with "Hello from Docker!" and container hostname

    Or open http://localhost:5000 in your browser.

    Rebuild to demonstrate layer caching:
    # Modify app.py slightly (change the HTML message) sed -i 's/Hello from Docker/Hello from Docker v2/g' app.py # Rebuild the image docker build -t my-flask-app:v1.1 . # Notice: Requirements layer is cached (USING CACHE), only COPY app.py layer rebuilds
    Clean up:
    docker stop flask-container docker rm flask-container docker rmi my-flask-app:v1.0 my-flask-app:v1.1
    ✅ Lab Success Criteria

    You've completed this lab when: (1) You can build a custom image from a Dockerfile, (2) Your containerized Flask app is accessible at localhost:5000, (3) You understand how layer caching speeds up rebuilds, (4) You can create multiple tagged versions of an image.

    Lab 3: Container Networking & Communication

    Objective: Create a custom Docker network, run multiple containers on that network, and demonstrate container-to-container communication using DNS.

    Learning Goals:

    • Create and manage custom Docker networks
    • Understand container DNS resolution
    • Practice multi-container communication patterns
    Navigate to your lab working directory:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course/week-02-docker-fundamentals/lab-03-networking

    Every file you create in this lab goes into this directory. Stay here for all remaining steps unless a step explicitly tells you to change directory.

    Create a custom bridge network:
    docker network create my-app-network
    Inspect the network:
    docker network inspect my-app-network # Shows network configuration, subnet, gateway
    Run a nginx container on the custom network:
    docker run -d --name web-server --network my-app-network nginx:latest
    Run a second container (alpine with networking tools) on the same network:
    docker run -it --name client --network my-app-network alpine sh # Inside the alpine container, install curl: apk add --no-cache curl # Test DNS resolution - container name resolves to IP: ping -c 3 web-server # Access nginx using container name as hostname: curl http://web-server # Should return nginx welcome page HTML exit
    Verify network isolation:
    # Run a container on the default bridge network docker run -d --name isolated-nginx nginx:latest # Try to access it from the client on my-app-network docker exec client ping -c 3 isolated-nginx # Should fail - containers on different networks can't communicate
    Connect a container to multiple networks:
    # Connect isolated-nginx to my-app-network docker network connect my-app-network isolated-nginx # Now client can reach it docker exec client curl http://isolated-nginx # Success! Container is now on both networks
    Clean up:
    docker stop web-server client isolated-nginx docker rm web-server client isolated-nginx docker network rm my-app-network
    ✅ Lab Success Criteria

    You've completed this lab when: (1) You can create custom networks, (2) Containers on the same network can communicate using container names as hostnames, (3) You understand network isolation (containers on different networks can't communicate by default), (4) You can connect a container to multiple networks.

    Lab 4: Data Persistence with Volumes

    Objective: Demonstrate container filesystem ephemerality, create named volumes, and persist data across container recreation.

    Learning Goals:

    • Understand why volumes are necessary for stateful applications
    • Create and manage named volumes
    • Practice data persistence patterns
    Navigate to your lab working directory:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course/week-02-docker-fundamentals/lab-04-volumes

    Every file you create in this lab goes into this directory. Stay here for all remaining steps unless a step explicitly tells you to change directory.

    Demonstrate ephemeral filesystem (no volume):
    # Run a container and write data to its filesystem docker run --name test-ephemeral ubuntu:22.04 \ bash -c "echo 'This data will be lost' > /data.txt && cat /data.txt" # Remove the container docker rm test-ephemeral # Run a new container with same image - data is gone docker run --name test-ephemeral-2 ubuntu:22.04 cat /data.txt # Error: No such file or directory (as expected) docker rm test-ephemeral-2
    Create a named volume:
    docker volume create my-persistent-data
    Run container with volume mounted:
    # Write data to the volume docker run --name test-persistent -v my-persistent-data:/data ubuntu:22.04 \ bash -c "echo 'This data persists!' > /data/persistent.txt && cat /data/persistent.txt"
    Remove container and verify data persists:
    docker rm test-persistent # Run a NEW container with the SAME volume docker run --name test-persistent-2 -v my-persistent-data:/data ubuntu:22.04 \ cat /data/persistent.txt # Success! Data persisted across container recreation
    Practical example - MySQL database with persistent storage:
    # Create volume for MySQL data docker volume create mysql-data # Run MySQL with volume for database files docker run -d \ --name mysql-db \ -e MYSQL_ROOT_PASSWORD=secret \ -v mysql-data:/var/lib/mysql \ mysql:8.0 # Wait for MySQL to initialize (20-30 seconds) sleep 30 # Create a test database docker exec mysql-db mysql -uroot -psecret \ -e "CREATE DATABASE testdb; USE testdb; CREATE TABLE users (id INT, name VARCHAR(50)); INSERT INTO users VALUES (1, 'Alice');" # Query the data docker exec mysql-db mysql -uroot -psecret \ -e "SELECT * FROM testdb.users;"
    Simulate container failure and recovery:
    # Stop and remove MySQL container docker stop mysql-db docker rm mysql-db # Start a NEW MySQL container with the SAME volume docker run -d \ --name mysql-db-recovered \ -e MYSQL_ROOT_PASSWORD=secret \ -v mysql-data:/var/lib/mysql \ mysql:8.0 # Wait for startup sleep 20 # Query data - it's still there! docker exec mysql-db-recovered mysql -uroot -psecret \ -e "SELECT * FROM testdb.users;" # Returns: Alice (data survived container recreation)
    Clean up:
    docker stop mysql-db-recovered docker rm mysql-db-recovered test-persistent-2 docker volume rm my-persistent-data mysql-data
    ✅ Lab Success Criteria

    You've completed this lab when: (1) You understand that container filesystems are ephemeral by default, (2) You can create named volumes, (3) Data written to volumes survives container deletion and recreation, (4) You can run a stateful application (MySQL) with persistent storage.

    Lab 5: Multi-Container Application (Web + Database)

    Objective: Deploy a complete application stack with separate containers for web frontend and database backend, connected via custom network and using volumes for data persistence.

    Learning Goals:

    • Integrate networking and volume concepts
    • Understand multi-container application architecture
    • Practice real-world deployment patterns
    Navigate to your lab working directory:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course/week-02-docker-fundamentals/lab-05-multi-container

    Every file you create in this lab goes into this directory. Stay here for all remaining steps unless a step explicitly tells you to change directory.

    Create project infrastructure:
    # Create custom network for the application docker network create app-network # Create volume for database persistence docker volume create postgres-data
    Deploy PostgreSQL database:
    docker run -d \ --name app-database \ --network app-network \ -v postgres-data:/var/lib/postgresql/data \ -e POSTGRES_PASSWORD=mypassword \ -e POSTGRES_DB=appdb \ postgres:15
    Create a simple web application that connects to the database:
    mkdir ~/multi-container-app && cd ~/multi-container-app cat > app.py << 'EOF' from flask import Flask import psycopg2 import os app=Flask(__name__) def get_db_connection(): return psycopg2.connect( host='app-database' , # Container name as hostname database='appdb' , user='postgres' , password='mypassword' ) @app.route('/') def index(): try: conn=get_db_connection() cur=conn.cursor() cur.execute('SELECT version();') db_version=cur.fetchone()[0] cur.close() conn.close() return f"

    Connected to Database!

    PostgreSQL version: {db_version}

    " except Exception as e: return f"

    Database connection failed

    {str(e)}

    " if __name__ == '__main__': app.run(host='0.0.0.0', port=5000) EOF cat > requirements.txt << 'EOF' flask==3.0.0 psycopg2-binary==2.9.9 EOF cat> Dockerfile << 'EOF' FROM python:3.11-slim WORKDIR /app COPY requirements.txt . RUN pip install --no-cache-dir -r requirements.txt COPY app.py . EXPOSE 5000 CMD ["python", "app.py" ] EOF
    Build and deploy the web application:
    # Build the image docker build -t my-web-app:v1 . # Run the web container on the same network docker run -d \ --name app-web \ --network app-network \ -p 5000:5000 \ my-web-app:v1
    Test the application:
    # Wait a few seconds for containers to be fully ready sleep 10 # Access the web application curl http://localhost:5000 # Should display "Connected to Database!" with PostgreSQL version # Or open http://localhost:5000 in your browser
    Verify data persistence:
    # Create a test table and insert data docker exec app-database psql -U postgres -d appdb \ -c "CREATE TABLE visitors (id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, visit_time TIMESTAMP DEFAULT NOW());" docker exec app-database psql -U postgres -d appdb \ -c "INSERT INTO visitors DEFAULT VALUES;" # Query the data docker exec app-database psql -U postgres -d appdb \ -c "SELECT * FROM visitors;" # Stop and remove database container docker stop app-database docker rm app-database # Start new database container with same volume docker run -d \ --name app-database \ --network app-network \ -v postgres-data:/var/lib/postgresql/data \ -e POSTGRES_PASSWORD=mypassword \ -e POSTGRES_DB=appdb \ postgres:15 sleep 10 # Data still exists! docker exec app-database psql -U postgres -d appdb \ -c "SELECT * FROM visitors;"
    View logs and monitor the application:
    # View web application logs docker logs app-web # View database logs docker logs app-database # Monitor resource usage docker stats app-web app-database # Press Ctrl+C to exit
    Clean up:
    docker stop app-web app-database docker rm app-web app-database docker rmi my-web-app:v1 docker network rm app-network docker volume rm postgres-data
    ✅ Lab Success Criteria

    You've completed this lab when: (1) Web and database containers run on the same custom network, (2) Web container can connect to database using container name as hostname, (3) Database data persists across container recreation, (4) You can access the web application at localhost:5000 and see successful database connection message.

    🎯 Labs Completed - What's Next?

    Congratulations! You've completed all five Docker fundamentals labs. You now have hands-on experience with containers, images, networking, volumes, and multi-container applications. Next, review the lab solutions and troubleshooting guides below to deepen your understanding. Then complete the practice questions, quiz, test, and week-ending assignment to solidify your knowledge.

    3

    Advanced Docker & Multi-Container Applications

    Docker Compose, Multi-Stage Builds, Security, and Production Deployment Patterns

    Building on Week 2's Docker fundamentals, this week elevates your containerization skills to production-grade expertise. You'll master Docker Compose for declarative multi-service orchestration, implement multi-stage builds that dramatically reduce image sizes, secure containers with vulnerability scanning and best practices, configure advanced networking patterns, and deploy production-ready architectures. By week's end, you'll orchestrate complex applications with the same tools used by Fortune 500 companies, transforming the manual docker run commands from Week 2 into elegant, version-controlled infrastructure definitions.

    Mentorship Guidance

    Mentorship students receive structured support for Advanced Docker & Multi-Container Applications: live Q&A, assignment feedback, and office hours focused on this week's goals.

    Share blockers in Google Classroom and schedule a review session once your lab and assignment work are ready.

    Weekly Delivery Contract

    Week 3 should clearly show iteration on the Week 2 project rather than a disconnected reset. Mentors need to see how the learner evolved an earlier artefact into a more production-ready system using Compose, multi-stage builds, security checks, and stronger documentation.

    • Primary GitHub update: maintain the same course repository and add a clearly reviewable week-03-advanced-docker/ workspace with links back to the Week 2 baseline that this week improves.
    • Required artefacts: docker-compose.yml, updated Dockerfiles, security scan evidence, environment examples, screenshots of multi-container execution, and a README explaining architecture, services, and recovery decisions.
    • Portfolio expectation: the weekly project should show progression, not just completion. Learners should document what changed from Week 2, why it changed, and how the new approach is more production-ready.
    • Google Classroom package: submit the course repo URL, the exact week-03-advanced-docker/ path, any external deployment or registry links, and a reflection on blockers, debugging steps, and production tradeoffs.
    • Completion standard: a mentor should be able to inspect the repo and understand the architecture, security posture, execution steps, and learner reasoning without requesting missing context.

    Learning Objectives

    By the end of this week, you will be able to demonstrate advanced Docker competencies across all Bloom's Taxonomy levels:

    Remember

    Define Docker Compose, multi-stage builds, and container security concepts. List Docker Compose commands and YAML structure.

    Understand

    Explain how Docker Compose simplifies multi-container orchestration. Describe multi-stage build optimization strategies.

    Apply

    Write docker-compose.yml files for multi-service applications. Implement multi-stage Dockerfiles to reduce image sizes by 70%+.

    Analyze

    Compare Docker Compose vs manual container orchestration. Assess container vulnerabilities using security scanning tools.

    Evaluate

    Critique docker-compose.yml configurations for production readiness. Recommend security hardening strategies for containerized applications.

    Create

    Design complete multi-tier application stacks with Docker Compose. Architect production deployment strategies with health checks and rollback capabilities.

    Pre-Class Exercises

    Complete these exercises before studying the main content. They prepare you for advanced Docker concepts and activate prior knowledge from Week 2. Budget 2-3 hours for this work.

    ⚙ Week 03 Workspace Setup — Complete This First

    Create your working directory for this week inside the course repository. Every pre-class note, lab file, screenshot, and project deliverable belongs under this folder tree — nothing goes anywhere else.

    1. Navigate to your course repository root:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course
    2. Create this week's folder and all sub-directories in one command:
    mkdir -p week-03-advanced-docker/{preclass-notes,lab-01-compose-production,lab-02-multi-stage-builds,lab-03-container-security,lab-04-production-ready,lab-05-env-configs,weekly-project}

    This single command creates the whole week's workspace tree. You will stay within this tree for pre-class work, every lab, and the weekly project.

    3. Verify the structure was created correctly:
    find week-03-advanced-docker -type d | sort

    You should see 7 directories (preclass-notes + 5 lab folders + weekly-project).

    4. Create your pre-class notes file:
    touch week-03-advanced-docker/preclass-notes/week-03-notes.md

    All research, written answers, and reflections for this week's pre-class exercises go into this Markdown file. Keep it open as you work through the exercises below.

    5. Commit the empty scaffold to your GitHub portfolio:
    git add week-03-advanced-docker/ git commit -m "chore(week-03-advanced-docker): scaffold week 03 working directory" git push

    Pushing the empty scaffold creates a timestamped entry on your GitHub profile showing when you started this week — visible evidence for your portfolio and mentorship reviews.

    📁 Week 03 Folder Structure
    ~/devops-engineering-course/ └── week-03-advanced-docker/ ├── preclass-notes/ ├── lab-01-compose-production/ ├── lab-02-multi-stage-builds/ ├── lab-03-container-security/ ├── lab-04-production-ready/ ├── lab-05-env-configs/ └── weekly-project/

    1Week 2 Multi-Container Reflection

    Review your Week 2 assignment (Task Management API with PostgreSQL + Redis). Write 200-300 words reflecting on:

    • How many docker commands did you need to remember?
    • What happens if you restart your computer? Do you need to recreate networks and volumes manually?
    • If you wanted to share your project with a teammate, how would they replicate your exact setup?
    • What pain points did you experience with manual container orchestration?
    💡 Reflection Goal

    This reflection helps you understand why Docker Compose exists. The friction you experienced is exactly what Compose solves.

    2Docker Compose Quick Start

    Visit docs.docker.com/compose and read the "Overview" and "Get Started" pages (spend 20-30 minutes). Don't code yet—just familiarize yourself with:

    • What problem does Docker Compose solve?
    • What is a docker-compose.yml file?
    • What are the basic commands: docker-compose up, docker-compose down?
    • How does Compose handle networks and volumes?

    Take notes on any confusing terminology—we'll clarify in teaching notes.

    3Multi-Stage Build Research

    Search for "Docker multi-stage builds" and study 2-3 blog posts or tutorials. Look for examples showing before/after image sizes. Answer:

    • What is a multi-stage build?
    • Why do images become smaller with multi-stage builds?
    • What kinds of files/dependencies get excluded from final images?
    • When would you not use multi-stage builds?
    ⚠️ Don't Worry About Syntax Yet

    Focus on understanding the concept. We'll master the syntax together in labs.

    4Container Security Awareness

    Read about container security vulnerabilities. Search for "Docker security best practices" and identify at least 5 security concerns when running containers in production. Consider:

    • Running containers as root user
    • Using images from untrusted sources
    • Exposing unnecessary ports
    • Outdated base images with known CVEs (Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures)
    • Secrets in environment variables or image layers

    Write a brief summary (150-200 words) of what you learned about container security risks.

    5Production Deployment Patterns

    Research how companies deploy Docker containers to production. Look for information about:

    • Health checks: How do systems know if a container is healthy?
    • Restart policies: What happens when a container crashes?
    • Resource limits: How do you prevent one container from consuming all host resources?
    • Logging strategies: Where do container logs go in production?

    Don't try to implement anything yet—just build awareness of production concerns beyond basic docker run.

    📚 Recommended Reading

    Optional but valuable: Browse the official Docker Compose documentation (docs.docker.com/compose/compose-file) to see the full range of configuration options. Don't memorize—just get a sense of what's possible. We'll reference this extensively during labs.

    Detailed Teaching Notes

    3.1 Docker Compose Introduction: Simplifying Multi-Container Orchestration

    In Week 2's assignment, you experienced the pain of manually orchestrating multiple containers. Remember writing long docker run commands with dozens of flags? Creating networks and volumes separately? Managing container startup order? Docker Compose eliminates all of that complexity.

    🎯 What is Docker Compose?

    Docker Compose is a tool for defining and running multi-container Docker applications using a simple YAML configuration file. Instead of typing multiple docker commands, you declare your entire application stack in docker-compose.yml and bring it up with a single command: docker-compose up.

    The Problem Compose Solves:

    Think back to your Week 2 Task Management API assignment. To run it, you had to:

    # Step 1: Create a custom network docker network create task-api-network # Step 2: Create volumes docker volume create postgres-data docker volume create redis-data # Step 3: Start PostgreSQL docker run -d \ --name task-db \ --network task-api-network \ -e POSTGRES_USER=taskuser \ -e POSTGRES_PASSWORD=taskpass \ -e POSTGRES_DB=taskdb \ -v postgres-data:/var/lib/postgresql/data \ -p 5432:5432 \ postgres:15-alpine # Step 4: Wait for database to be ready... # Step 5: Start Redis docker run -d \ --name task-cache \ --network task-api-network \ -v redis-data:/data \ -p 6379:6379 \ redis:7-alpine # Step 6: Start the API application docker run -d \ --name task-api \ --network task-api-network \ -e DATABASE_URL=postgresql://taskuser:taskpass@task-db:5432/taskdb \ -e REDIS_URL=redis://task-cache:6379 \ -p 3000:3000 \ --depends-on task-db task-cache \ my-task-api:latest

    That's 50+ lines of imperative commands that must be run in the correct order. If you restart your computer, you have to remember all of this. If a colleague joins your team, they need documentation. If you deploy to staging, you manually recreate everything. This doesn't scale.

    With Docker Compose, this entire stack becomes:

    version: '3.8' services: task-db: image: postgres:15-alpine environment: POSTGRES_USER: taskuser POSTGRES_PASSWORD: taskpass POSTGRES_DB: taskdb volumes: - postgres-data:/var/lib/postgresql/data networks: - task-api-network ports: - "5432:5432" task-cache: image: redis:7-alpine volumes: - redis-data:/data networks: - task-api-network ports: - "6379:6379" task-api: image: my-task-api:latest environment: DATABASE_URL: postgresql://taskuser:taskpass@task-db:5432/taskdb REDIS_URL: redis://task-cache:6379 depends_on: - task-db - task-cache networks: - task-api-network ports: - "3000:3000" volumes: postgres-data: redis-data: networks: task-api-network:

    Now, to start the entire application: docker-compose up. To stop it: docker-compose down. To rebuild and restart: docker-compose up --build. One command instead of six complex commands.

    💡 Key Benefits of Docker Compose
    • Declarative Configuration: Describe what you want, not how to achieve it
    • Single Source of Truth: Your entire stack is documented in one file
    • Version Controlled: Commit docker-compose.yml to Git with your application code
    • Environment Parity: Dev, staging, and production use identical configurations
    • Automatic Networking: Compose creates networks and DNS resolution automatically
    • Dependency Management: Control startup order with depends_on
    • Single-Command Lifecycle: Start, stop, rebuild entire stacks with one command

    When to Use Docker Compose:

    • Local Development: Run your entire application stack on your laptop
    • Testing Environments: Spin up temporary environments for testing branches
    • CI/CD Pipelines: Integration tests that require multiple services
    • Single-Host Deployments: Small projects on a single server (not production-scale)
    • Documentation: Even if you don't use it, docker-compose.yml documents your architecture
    ⚠️ When NOT to Use Docker Compose

    Docker Compose is NOT a production orchestrator for multi-host, scaled applications. For production, you need Kubernetes (covered in Weeks 5-9). Compose works on a single Docker host—it doesn't distribute containers across multiple servers, doesn't provide high availability, and doesn't auto-scale. Think of Compose as "development/testing tool" and Kubernetes as "production orchestrator."

    3.2 Docker Compose File Structure: YAML Anatomy

    Docker Compose uses YAML (YAML Ain't Markup Language), a human-readable data serialization format. If you've never used YAML, don't worry—it's simpler than JSON with less punctuation.

    📖 YAML Basics

    YAML uses indentation (spaces, not tabs!) to show structure. Key-value pairs are key: value. Lists use - dashes. Nested structures use more indentation. That's 90% of what you need to know.

    Quick YAML Syntax Reference:

    # Comments start with # key: value # String value number: 42 # Number value boolean: true # Boolean value # List/Array fruits: - apple - banana - orange # Nested object database: host: localhost port: 5432 credentials: username: admin password: secret # Multi-line string (pipe preserves newlines) script: | echo "Line 1" echo "Line 2" echo "Line 3"

    Docker Compose File Structure:

    A docker-compose.yml file has four main top-level sections:

    version: '3.8' # Compose file format version services: # Define your containers here web: image: nginx api: build: . database: image: postgres volumes: # Define named volumes db-data: cache-data: networks: # Define custom networks frontend: backend: configs: # Optional: external configuration files app-config:

    Compose File Top-Level Keys

    version

    Specifies the Compose file format version. Use '3.8' (latest stable as of Docker 20.10+). Different versions support different features. Always quote the version number.

    services

    The heart of your compose file. Each service is a container. Service names become DNS hostnames (other containers can reach web by name). Contains all your application components.

    volumes

    Define named volumes that persist data. Compose creates these automatically. Services reference volumes by name. Volumes survive docker-compose down (unless you add -v flag).

    networks

    Define custom networks. By default, Compose creates one network for all services. Custom networks let you isolate services (frontend can't access database directly). Services join networks by name.

    Service Configuration Options:

    Within the services section, each service (container) can have many configuration options. Here are the most important:

    services: web: # Image to use (from Docker Hub or registry) image: nginx:alpine # OR build from Dockerfile in current directory build: . # OR build with specific context and Dockerfile build: context: ./web dockerfile: Dockerfile.production # Container name (optional, Compose auto-generates if omitted) container_name: my-web-server # Port mapping (HOST:CONTAINER) ports: - "8080:80" # Map host 8080 to container 80 - "443:443" # Environment variables environment: NODE_ENV: production DATABASE_URL: postgres://db:5432/myapp API_KEY: ${API_KEY} # From .env file or host environment # OR load environment from file env_file: - .env - .env.production # Volume mounts volumes: - ./app:/usr/share/nginx/html # Bind mount (local folder) - web-logs:/var/log/nginx # Named volume - /etc/nginx/nginx.conf:/etc/nginx/nginx.conf:ro # Read-only mount # Networks this service joins networks: - frontend - backend # Service dependencies (startup order) depends_on: - database - cache # Restart policy restart: unless-stopped # Options: no, always, on-failure, unless-stopped # Resource limits deploy: resources: limits: cpus: '0.50' # Max 50% of one CPU core memory: 512M # Max 512MB RAM reservations: cpus: '0.25' # Guaranteed 25% CPU memory: 256M # Guaranteed 256MB RAM # Health check healthcheck: test: ["CMD", "curl", "-f", "http://localhost/health"] interval: 30s timeout: 10s retries: 3 start_period: 40s # Command override (replaces CMD in Dockerfile) command: npm start # Entrypoint override entrypoint: /app/start.sh # Working directory working_dir: /app # Expose ports (documentation only, doesn't publish) expose: - "3000"
    ⚠️ Common YAML Mistakes
    • Using Tabs: YAML requires spaces for indentation, not tabs. Use 2-space indentation consistently.
    • Incorrect Indentation: Misaligned keys break parsing. Use a YAML linter or editor with YAML support.
    • Missing Colons: key:value (no space) is valid, but key: value (space after colon) is more readable.
    • Quoting Issues: Version must be quoted: version: '3.8' not version: 3.8
    • Port Mapping Quotes: Quote port mappings: "8080:80" prevents YAML parsing 8080:80 as time.

    3.3 Multi-Service Orchestration: Building Complete Stacks

    The real power of Docker Compose emerges when orchestrating multiple interdependent services. Let's build a realistic three-tier application stack: a React frontend, Node.js API backend, PostgreSQL database, Redis cache, and nginx reverse proxy.

    Example: Complete E-Commerce Application Stack

    version: '3.8' services: # Frontend: React SPA frontend: build: context: ./frontend dockerfile: Dockerfile ports: - "3000:80" depends_on: - api networks: - frontend-network environment: REACT_APP_API_URL: http://localhost:4000/api restart: unless-stopped # Backend API: Node.js + Express api: build: context: ./backend dockerfile: Dockerfile ports: - "4000:4000" environment: NODE_ENV: production DATABASE_URL: postgresql://ecommerce:secret123@database:5432/ecommerce_db REDIS_URL: redis://cache:6379 JWT_SECRET: ${JWT_SECRET} depends_on: - database - cache networks: - frontend-network - backend-network volumes: - ./backend/logs:/app/logs restart: unless-stopped healthcheck: test: ["CMD", "curl", "-f", "http://localhost:4000/health"] interval: 30s timeout: 10s retries: 3 # Database: PostgreSQL database: image: postgres:15-alpine environment: POSTGRES_USER: ecommerce POSTGRES_PASSWORD: secret123 POSTGRES_DB: ecommerce_db volumes: - postgres-data:/var/lib/postgresql/data - ./backend/database/init.sql:/docker-entrypoint-initdb.d/init.sql networks: - backend-network restart: unless-stopped healthcheck: test: ["CMD-SHELL", "pg_isready -U ecommerce"] interval: 10s timeout: 5s retries: 5 # Cache: Redis cache: image: redis:7-alpine command: redis-server --appendonly yes volumes: - redis-data:/data networks: - backend-network restart: unless-stopped healthcheck: test: ["CMD", "redis-cli", "ping"] interval: 10s timeout: 5s retries: 5 # Reverse Proxy: Nginx proxy: image: nginx:alpine ports: - "80:80" - "443:443" volumes: - ./nginx/nginx.conf:/etc/nginx/nginx.conf:ro - ./nginx/ssl:/etc/nginx/ssl:ro depends_on: - frontend - api networks: - frontend-network restart: unless-stopped volumes: postgres-data: driver: local redis-data: driver: local networks: frontend-network: driver: bridge backend-network: driver: bridge

    Key Orchestration Concepts in This Example:

    🔗 Service Discovery & DNS

    Notice how the API service connects to database:5432 and cache:6379. Compose automatically creates DNS entries where service names become hostnames. The API container can reach the database using database as the hostname—no IP addresses needed. This works because they're on the same network (backend-network).

    🔐 Network Segmentation

    We created two networks: frontend-network (for frontend ↔ API communication) and backend-network (for API ↔ database ↔ cache). This security best practice ensures the frontend can't directly access the database—all requests must go through the API. The API joins both networks, acting as a gateway.

    ⏱️ Dependency Management with depends_on

    The depends_on key controls startup order. Compose starts database and cache before api, and api before frontend. However, depends_on only waits for container start, not readiness. Use health checks to ensure services are actually ready to accept connections.

    ⚠️ depends_on Limitations

    depends_on starts containers in order but doesn't wait for them to be ready. PostgreSQL might take 10 seconds to initialize, but the API container starts immediately and tries to connect, causing errors. Solutions: (1) Add health checks, (2) Build retry logic into your application, (3) Use wait-for-it scripts, (4) Use depends_on with condition: service_healthy (Compose v3.4+ with health checks).

    Environment Variable Substitution:

    Notice JWT_SECRET: ${JWT_SECRET} in the API service. Compose can substitute variables from:

    • .env file: Create .env in the same directory as docker-compose.yml
    • Host environment: Export variables in your shell before running Compose
    • Shell export: export JWT_SECRET=mysecret && docker-compose up

    Example .env file:

    # .env file (never commit to Git!) JWT_SECRET=super-secret-jwt-key-change-in-production DATABASE_PASSWORD=strong-database-password REDIS_PASSWORD=redis-password-here # You can also set Compose-specific variables COMPOSE_PROJECT_NAME=ecommerce COMPOSE_FILE=docker-compose.yml:docker-compose.override.yml

    3.4 Docker Compose Commands & Workflows

    Docker Compose provides a rich CLI for managing multi-container applications. Let's master the essential commands and common workflows.

    Essential Commands:

    # Start all services (creates networks/volumes if needed) docker-compose up # Start in detached mode (background) docker-compose up -d # Build images before starting (if using build: directive) docker-compose up --build # Force recreate containers even if config unchanged docker-compose up --force-recreate # Scale a service to multiple instances docker-compose up --scale api=3 # Start specific services only docker-compose up frontend api # Stop all services (containers remain) docker-compose stop # Start stopped services docker-compose start # Stop and remove containers, networks (volumes persist) docker-compose down # Stop and remove containers, networks, AND volumes docker-compose down -v # View running services docker-compose ps # View logs from all services docker-compose logs # Follow logs in real-time docker-compose logs -f # View logs for specific service docker-compose logs -f api # Tail last 100 lines docker-compose logs --tail=100 api # Execute command in running service docker-compose exec api bash docker-compose exec database psql -U ecommerce ecommerce_db # Run one-off command (creates new container) docker-compose run api npm test # Build or rebuild services docker-compose build # Build without cache docker-compose build --no-cache # Pull latest images for all services docker-compose pull # Validate compose file syntax docker-compose config # View actual configuration (with variable substitution) docker-compose config # List volumes docker volume ls # List networks docker network ls # View resource usage docker-compose top # Restart services docker-compose restart # Restart specific service docker-compose restart api # Pause all services (freeze, don't stop) docker-compose pause # Unpause services docker-compose unpause

    Development Workflow:

    Typical Development Day with Compose

    Morning: Start your stack
    cd my-project docker-compose up -d # Start in background docker-compose logs -f api # Watch API logs
    Code changes: Your editor saves files to bind-mounted volumes

    If using hot-reload (nodemon, webpack dev server), changes appear immediately. No container restart needed.

    Dockerfile changes: Rebuild the service
    docker-compose up -d --build api # Rebuild only API service
    Debugging: Execute into running container
    docker-compose exec api bash # Now you're inside the container ls -la cat /app/logs/error.log env | grep DATABASE # Check environment variables
    Database operations: Connect to database
    docker-compose exec database psql -U ecommerce ecommerce_db # Run SQL queries, inspect schema
    Check service health
    docker-compose ps # See status, ports, health docker-compose top # See running processes
    View logs from multiple services
    docker-compose logs -f api database # Watch multiple services
    Evening: Clean shutdown
    docker-compose down # Stop and remove containers (data persists in volumes)

    Production Deployment Workflow:

    ⚠️ Compose in Production

    While Docker Compose can be used for small production deployments on a single server, it's not designed for production-scale orchestration. For real production environments with multiple hosts, high availability, auto-scaling, and rolling updates, use Kubernetes (Weeks 5-9) or Docker Swarm. However, for small projects, internal tools, or single-server deployments, Compose works fine.

    If you do use Compose for production, follow these practices:

    ✅ Production Best Practices
    • Use specific image tags: postgres:15.2-alpine not postgres:latest
    • Set restart policies: restart: unless-stopped for automatic recovery
    • Implement health checks: Ensure services are ready before accepting traffic
    • Use .env files: Never hardcode secrets in docker-compose.yml
    • Set resource limits: Prevent services from consuming all host resources
    • Use named volumes: Never bind-mount local directories in production
    • Configure logging: Use proper log drivers (journald, syslog, ELK)
    • Network segmentation: Isolate services with multiple networks
    • Backup volumes: Automate backups of persistent data
    • Monitor everything: Use Prometheus, Grafana, or cloud monitoring

    Compose Override Files:

    You can create environment-specific configurations without duplicating your entire compose file:

    # docker-compose.yml (base configuration) version: '3.8' services: api: image: my-api:latest environment: NODE_ENV: production # docker-compose.override.yml (automatically loaded, for development) version: '3.8' services: api: build: . # Override: build locally instead of pulling image volumes: - ./src:/app/src # Override: mount source for hot-reload environment: NODE_ENV: development DEBUG: true # docker-compose.prod.yml (for production, explicit load) version: '3.8' services: api: deploy: replicas: 3 resources: limits: memory: 1G

    To use specific override files:

    # Development (automatically loads docker-compose.yml + docker-compose.override.yml) docker-compose up # Production (explicitly specify files) docker-compose -f docker-compose.yml -f docker-compose.prod.yml up -d # Testing (use test-specific config) docker-compose -f docker-compose.yml -f docker-compose.test.yml run api npm test
    💡 Pro Tip: Project Organization

    Store your compose files in your project repository alongside your application code. This ensures environment parity—developers, CI/CD pipelines, and production all use identical service definitions. Version control your docker-compose.yml, but NEVER commit .env files with secrets. Use .env.example as a template.

    3.5 Multi-Stage Builds: Optimizing Image Size & Security

    One of the most powerful Docker features for production deployments is multi-stage builds. This technique dramatically reduces image size (often by 70-90%), improves security by excluding build tools from runtime images, and keeps Dockerfiles clean and maintainable.

    🎯 What are Multi-Stage Builds?

    Multi-stage builds use multiple FROM statements in a single Dockerfile. Each FROM instruction starts a new build stage. You can selectively copy artifacts from one stage to another, leaving behind everything you don't need in the final image.

    The Problem: Bloated Images

    Traditional Dockerfiles include everything needed to build the application: compilers, build tools, development dependencies, source code, intermediate files. For example, a Node.js app might need:

    • Node.js + npm (needed at runtime)
    • Build tools (node-gyp, python, make, gcc) for native dependencies
    • All devDependencies from package.json (testing, linting tools)
    • Source TypeScript files (if using TypeScript)
    • Git for version control during build

    This creates massive images. A simple Node.js API might balloon from 150MB (minimal runtime) to 1.5GB (with all build tools). Larger images mean:

    • Slower image pulls (longer deployment times)
    • More storage costs
    • Larger attack surface (more packages = more vulnerabilities)
    • Slower container startup

    Example: Traditional Single-Stage Build (BAD)

    # Traditional approach - everything in one stage FROM node:18 WORKDIR /app # Install ALL dependencies (including devDependencies) COPY package*.json ./ RUN npm install # Copy source code COPY . . # Build application (if TypeScript/bundling needed) RUN npm run build # Expose port and start EXPOSE 3000 CMD ["node", "dist/index.js"] # Result: Image size ~1.2GB (includes build tools, devDependencies, source files)

    Solution: Multi-Stage Build (GOOD)

    # Stage 1: Build stage (named "builder") FROM node:18 AS builder WORKDIR /app # Install ALL dependencies (including devDependencies needed for build) COPY package*.json ./ RUN npm install # Copy source code COPY . . # Build the application RUN npm run build # Stage 2: Production stage FROM node:18-alpine WORKDIR /app # Copy only production dependencies COPY package*.json ./ RUN npm install --production # Copy ONLY the built artifacts from builder stage COPY --from=builder /app/dist ./dist # Create non-root user RUN addgroup -g 1001 -S nodejs && \ adduser -S nodejs -u 1001 USER nodejs EXPOSE 3000 CMD ["node", "dist/index.js"] # Result: Image size ~150MB (90% reduction!)

    How Multi-Stage Builds Work:

    Multi-Stage Build Anatomy

    Stage 1: Builder

    Uses full-featured base image (e.g., node:18, not alpine). Installs all dependencies including devDependencies. Compiles/builds the application. Contains compilers, build tools, source code—everything needed for building but not needed at runtime.

    Stage 2: Production

    Uses minimal base image (e.g., node:18-alpine, distroless). Installs only production dependencies (npm install --production). Copies built artifacts from builder stage using COPY --from=builder. Result: tiny image with only what's needed to run.

    Key Insight

    Docker only keeps the final stage in the resulting image. All previous stages are discarded (though cached for faster rebuilds). This means builder stage with all its bloat doesn't end up in production image.

    Real-World Example: Go Application

    Go applications benefit tremendously from multi-stage builds because compiled Go binaries are statically linked—they need almost nothing at runtime:

    # Stage 1: Build stage FROM golang:1.21-alpine AS builder WORKDIR /app # Copy dependency files COPY go.mod go.sum ./ RUN go mod download # Copy source code COPY . . # Build static binary with optimizations RUN CGO_ENABLED=0 GOOS=linux go build -a -installsuffix cgo -o main . # Stage 2: Runtime stage (scratch = empty image!) FROM scratch # Copy CA certificates for HTTPS COPY --from=builder /etc/ssl/certs/ca-certificates.crt /etc/ssl/certs/ # Copy only the binary COPY --from=builder /app/main /main EXPOSE 8080 ENTRYPOINT ["/main"] # Result: Image size ~10MB (from potentially 500MB+ with builder stage)

    Using scratch (completely empty base image) with Go creates images measured in megabytes instead of gigabytes. The application binary and CA certificates are the only contents.

    ✅ Multi-Stage Build Best Practices
    • Name your stages: Use FROM image AS stage-name for clarity
    • Order stages by frequency of change: Put stable stages first for better caching
    • Use alpine or distroless for final stage: Minimal base images reduce attack surface
    • Copy only necessary artifacts: Don't copy entire directories, be selective
    • Create non-root user: Never run containers as root in production
    • Combine stages for different environments: Use --target flag to build specific stages

    3.6 Container Security: Scanning, Hardening & Best Practices

    Security is not optional in production deployments. Every container image you run is a potential attack vector. This section covers practical security measures every DevOps engineer must implement.

    ⚠️ Container Security Threats

    Containers share the host kernel. A container escape vulnerability allows attackers to break out of the container and compromise the host system and all other containers. Running vulnerable or malicious containers is extremely dangerous.

    Security Principle #1: Minimal Base Images

    Smaller images have fewer packages, which means fewer vulnerabilities. Use minimal base images:

    # Bad: Full OS with unnecessary packages FROM ubuntu:22.04 # ~77MB base, hundreds of packages # Better: Alpine Linux (minimal) FROM node:18-alpine # ~40MB base, minimal packages # Best: Google Distroless (no shell, no package manager) FROM gcr.io/distroless/nodejs18-debian11 # ~50MB, extremely minimal # Ultimate: Scratch (empty, for static binaries only) FROM scratch # 0MB, literally nothing except your binary
    🔐 Distroless Images

    Google's distroless images contain only your application and runtime dependencies. No shell, no package manager, no debugging tools. This makes exploitation extremely difficult even if your application is compromised. Distroless images are production-hardened by default.

    Security Principle #2: Never Run as Root

    By default, containers run as root (UID 0). If an attacker exploits your application, they have root privileges inside the container. Always create a non-root user:

    FROM node:18-alpine WORKDIR /app # Install dependencies COPY package*.json ./ RUN npm install --production # Copy application COPY . . # Create non-root user RUN addgroup -g 1001 -S nodejs && \ adduser -S nodejs -u 1001 && \ chown -R nodejs:nodejs /app # Switch to non-root user USER nodejs EXPOSE 3000 CMD ["node", "index.js"]

    Security Principle #3: Scan Images for Vulnerabilities

    Use vulnerability scanners to identify known CVEs (Common Vulnerabilities and Exposures) in your images:

    # Scan with Docker Scout (built into Docker Desktop) docker scout cves my-app:latest # Scan with Trivy (open-source, comprehensive) trivy image my-app:latest # Scan with Snyk (commercial, integrates with CI/CD) snyk container test my-app:latest # Scan with Anchore (enterprise-grade) anchore-cli image scan my-app:latest

    Example Trivy Scan Output:

    my-app:latest (alpine 3.18.0) ================================== Total: 23 (UNKNOWN: 0, LOW: 8, MEDIUM: 12, HIGH: 3, CRITICAL: 0) ┌──────────────┬────────────────┬──────────┬───────────────────┬───────────────┬──────────────────────────────────────┐ │ Library │ Vulnerability │ Severity │ Installed Version │ Fixed Version │ Title │ ├──────────────┼────────────────┼──────────┼───────────────────┼───────────────┼──────────────────────────────────────┤ │ openssl │ CVE-2023-12345 │ HIGH │ 3.0.8-r0 │ 3.0.9-r0 │ OpenSSL: buffer overflow in... │ │ libcurl │ CVE-2023-67890 │ HIGH │ 8.1.2-r0 │ 8.2.0-r0 │ curl: use-after-free in... │ └──────────────┴────────────────┴──────────┴───────────────────┴───────────────┴──────────────────────────────────────┘ Action: Update base image or rebuild to get patched versions

    Security Principle #4: Use Secrets Management

    Never hardcode secrets in Dockerfiles or images. Never commit secrets to Git. Use proper secrets management:

    ⚠️ NEVER Do This
    # BAD: Secrets in Dockerfile FROM node:18-alpine ENV DATABASE_PASSWORD=supersecret123 ❌ ENV API_KEY=abc123xyz ❌ # BAD: Secrets in docker-compose.yml committed to Git services: api: environment: DB_PASSWORD: supersecret123 ❌
    ✅ Proper Secrets Management
    # Option 1: Environment variables from host (development) docker run -e DATABASE_PASSWORD=$DATABASE_PASSWORD my-app # Option 2: .env file (never commit to Git!) # docker-compose.yml services: api: env_file: - .env # .env in .gitignore # Option 3: Docker Secrets (Swarm mode) docker secret create db_password ./db_password.txt docker service create --secret db_password my-app # Option 4: External secrets managers (production) # - AWS Secrets Manager # - HashiCorp Vault # - Azure Key Vault # - Google Secret Manager

    Security Principle #5: Read-Only Root Filesystem

    Make the container filesystem read-only to prevent malware from writing files:

    # docker-compose.yml services: api: image: my-api:latest read_only: true # Root filesystem read-only tmpfs: - /tmp # Allow writes to tmp (volatile) - /app/logs # Allow writes to logs

    Security Checklist Summary:

    Container Security Checklist

    • ✓ Use minimal base images (Alpine, Distroless, or Scratch)
    • ✓ Scan images for vulnerabilities regularly (Trivy, Snyk, Docker Scout)
    • ✓ Never run containers as root (create non-root user)
    • ✓ Use multi-stage builds to exclude build tools from runtime
    • ✓ Keep base images updated (rebuild regularly for security patches)
    • ✓ Never embed secrets in images or commit them to Git
    • ✓ Use secrets management (env vars, Docker secrets, Vault)
    • ✓ Enable read-only root filesystem when possible
    • ✓ Limit container capabilities (drop unnecessary Linux capabilities)
    • ✓ Set resource limits (prevent DoS via resource exhaustion)
    • ✓ Use specific image tags, never :latest in production
    • ✓ Sign and verify images (Docker Content Trust)
    • ✓ Run containers with --security-opt=no-new-privileges
    • ✓ Network segmentation (isolate services with custom networks)
    • ✓ Log everything (stdout/stderr for centralized logging)

    3.7 Production Patterns: Health Checks, Logging & Resource Management

    Production deployments require more than just "run the container." You need health monitoring, proper logging, resource constraints, and restart policies to build resilient systems.

    Health Checks: Knowing When Containers Are Really Ready

    A container might be "running" but not ready to accept traffic. Database might be initializing, application might be loading configurations. Health checks verify readiness:

    # Dockerfile health check FROM node:18-alpine WORKDIR /app COPY . . RUN npm install # Health check: curl /health endpoint every 30s HEALTHCHECK --interval=30s --timeout=10s --retries=3 --start-period=40s \ CMD curl -f http://localhost:3000/health || exit 1 CMD ["node", "index.js"]

    docker-compose.yml health checks:

    version: '3.8' services: api: image: my-api:latest healthcheck: test: ["CMD", "curl", "-f", "http://localhost:3000/health"] interval: 30s # Check every 30 seconds timeout: 10s # Fail if check takes >10s retries: 3 # 3 failed checks = unhealthy start_period: 40s # Grace period during startup depends_on: database: condition: service_healthy # Wait for database health database: image: postgres:15-alpine healthcheck: test: ["CMD-SHELL", "pg_isready -U postgres"] interval: 10s timeout: 5s retries: 5

    Your Application Must Implement Health Endpoints:

    // Node.js Express health endpoint app.get('/health', async (req, res) => { try { // Check database connection await db.query('SELECT 1'); // Check cache connection await redis.ping(); // All checks passed res.status(200).json({ status: 'healthy', timestamp: new Date().toISOString(), uptime: process.uptime() }); } catch (error) { // Something is wrong res.status(503).json({ status: 'unhealthy', error: error.message }); } });

    Logging Best Practices

    Containers are ephemeral—when they die, their logs die with them (unless you persist logs properly). Follow the 12-Factor App logging principle: write all logs to stdout/stderr, let infrastructure collect them.

    ✅ Container Logging Best Practices
    • Write to stdout/stderr: Never write logs to files inside containers
    • Use structured logging: JSON logs are easier to parse and search
    • Include context: Request ID, user ID, timestamp, severity level
    • Configure log drivers: Use Docker logging drivers (json-file, syslog, fluentd)
    • Centralize logs: ELK Stack, Splunk, CloudWatch, Datadog
    • Set log rotation: Prevent disk space exhaustion
    # Configure Docker logging driver docker run \ --log-driver=json-file \ --log-opt max-size=10m \ --log-opt max-file=3 \ my-app # docker-compose.yml logging configuration services: api: image: my-api:latest logging: driver: "json-file" options: max-size: "10m" max-file: "3"

    Resource Limits: Preventing Runaway Containers

    Without limits, a single container can consume all host CPU/memory, crashing other containers. Always set resource limits:

    # docker run with resource limits docker run \ --memory="512m" \ --memory-swap="512m" \ --cpus="0.5" \ --pids-limit=100 \ my-app # docker-compose.yml resource limits services: api: image: my-api:latest deploy: resources: limits: cpus: '0.5' # Max 50% of one CPU memory: 512M # Max 512MB RAM reservations: cpus: '0.25' # Guaranteed 25% CPU memory: 256M # Guaranteed 256MB RAM

    Restart Policies: Automatic Recovery

    Containers should restart automatically when they fail:

    # Restart policies docker run --restart=no my-app # Never restart (default) docker run --restart=on-failure my-app # Restart only if exit code != 0 docker run --restart=always my-app # Always restart (even after host reboot) docker run --restart=unless-stopped my-app # Restart unless manually stopped (recommended) # docker-compose.yml services: api: image: my-api:latest restart: unless-stopped # Recommended for production

    Hands-On Labs

    Lab 1Convert Week 2 Task API to Docker Compose

    Objective: Experience the power of Docker Compose by converting your Week 2 manual multi-container setup to a simple YAML file.

    Scenario: Remember your Week 2 assignment where you manually ran multiple docker commands? Let's eliminate all that pain with Compose.

    Navigate to your lab working directory:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course/week-03-advanced-docker/lab-01-compose-production

    Every file you create in this lab goes into this directory. Stay here for all remaining steps unless a step explicitly tells you to change directory.

    Create project structure
    mkdir week3-compose-lab && cd week3-compose-lab mkdir app database
    Create a simple Node.js API (app/index.js)
    // app/index.js const express = require('express'); const { Pool } = require('pg'); const redis = require('redis'); const app = express(); const port = 3000; // PostgreSQL connection const pool = new Pool({ host: process.env.DB_HOST || 'database', database: process.env.DB_NAME || 'taskdb', user: process.env.DB_USER || 'taskuser', password: process.env.DB_PASSWORD || 'taskpass', port: 5432 }); // Redis connection const redisClient = redis.createClient({ url: `redis://${process.env.REDIS_HOST || 'cache'}:6379` }); redisClient.connect(); app.get('/health', async (req, res) => { try { await pool.query('SELECT 1'); await redisClient.ping(); res.json({ status: 'healthy', timestamp: new Date() }); } catch (error) { res.status(503).json({ status: 'unhealthy', error: error.message }); } }); app.get('/tasks', async (req, res) => { const result = await pool.query('SELECT * FROM tasks'); res.json(result.rows); }); app.listen(port, () => { console.log(`Task API running on port ${port}`); });
    Create package.json (app/package.json)
    { "name": "task-api", "version": "1.0.0", "main": "index.js", "dependencies": { "express": "^4.18.0", "pg": "^8.11.0", "redis": "^4.6.0" }, "scripts": { "start": "node index.js" } }
    Create Dockerfile (app/Dockerfile)
    FROM node:18-alpine WORKDIR /app COPY package*.json ./ RUN npm install --production COPY . . # Create non-root user RUN addgroup -g 1001 nodejs && \ adduser -S nodejs -u 1001 USER nodejs EXPOSE 3000 CMD ["npm", "start"]
    Create database initialization script (database/init.sql)
    CREATE TABLE IF NOT EXISTS tasks ( id SERIAL PRIMARY KEY, title VARCHAR(255) NOT NULL, completed BOOLEAN DEFAULT false, created_at TIMESTAMP DEFAULT CURRENT_TIMESTAMP ); INSERT INTO tasks (title, completed) VALUES ('Learn Docker Compose', true), ('Build multi-service app', false), ('Deploy to production', false);
    Create docker-compose.yml
    version: '3.8' services: api: build: ./app ports: - "3000:3000" environment: DB_HOST: database DB_NAME: taskdb DB_USER: taskuser DB_PASSWORD: taskpass REDIS_HOST: cache depends_on: database: condition: service_healthy cache: condition: service_healthy networks: - frontend - backend restart: unless-stopped database: image: postgres:15-alpine environment: POSTGRES_DB: taskdb POSTGRES_USER: taskuser POSTGRES_PASSWORD: taskpass volumes: - postgres-data:/var/lib/postgresql/data - ./database/init.sql:/docker-entrypoint-initdb.d/init.sql networks: - backend healthcheck: test: ["CMD-SHELL", "pg_isready -U taskuser"] interval: 5s timeout: 3s retries: 5 restart: unless-stopped cache: image: redis:7-alpine volumes: - redis-data:/data networks: - backend healthcheck: test: ["CMD", "redis-cli", "ping"] interval: 5s timeout: 3s retries: 5 restart: unless-stopped volumes: postgres-data: redis-data: networks: frontend: backend:
    Start the entire stack with ONE command
    docker-compose up -d
    Verify services are running
    docker-compose ps docker-compose logs -f api
    Test the application
    curl http://localhost:3000/health curl http://localhost:3000/tasks
    Clean up
    docker-compose down # Remove containers/networks (volumes persist) docker-compose down -v # Remove everything including volumes

    Compare: Week 2 required 6+ docker commands run manually in sequence. Week 3: docker-compose up. This is the power of declarative infrastructure.

    Lab 2Multi-Stage Build: Node.js Application

    Objective: Implement a multi-stage build that reduces image size by 70%+.

    Navigate to your lab working directory:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course/week-03-advanced-docker/lab-02-multi-stage-builds

    Every file you create in this lab goes into this directory. Stay here for all remaining steps unless a step explicitly tells you to change directory.

    Create project with TypeScript
    mkdir multistage-lab && cd multistage-lab npm init -y npm install express npm install --save-dev typescript @types/node @types/express
    Create TypeScript source (src/index.ts)
    import express from 'express'; const app = express(); const port = 3000; app.get('/', (req, res) => { res.json({ message: 'Multi-stage build success!', size: 'optimized' }); }); app.listen(port, () => { console.log(`Server running on port ${port}`); });
    Create tsconfig.json
    { "compilerOptions": { "target": "ES2020", "module": "commonjs", "outDir": "./dist", "rootDir": "./src", "strict": true, "esModuleInterop": true } }
    Add build script to package.json
    "scripts": { "build": "tsc", "start": "node dist/index.js" }
    Create multi-stage Dockerfile
    # Stage 1: Build FROM node:18 AS builder WORKDIR /app COPY package*.json ./ RUN npm install COPY . . RUN npm run build # Stage 2: Production FROM node:18-alpine WORKDIR /app COPY package*.json ./ RUN npm install --production COPY --from=builder /app/dist ./dist RUN addgroup -g 1001 nodejs && \ adduser -S nodejs -u 1001 && \ chown -R nodejs:nodejs /app USER nodejs EXPOSE 3000 CMD ["npm", "start"]
    Build and compare image sizes
    # Build single-stage (comment out second stage, use only builder) docker build -t app-single-stage . docker images app-single-stage # Build multi-stage docker build -t app-multi-stage . docker images app-multi-stage # Compare sizes - you should see 60-80% reduction!
    Run and test
    docker run -p 3000:3000 app-multi-stage curl http://localhost:3000

    Lab 3Container Security Scanning

    Objective: Scan container images for vulnerabilities and fix them.

    Navigate to your lab working directory:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course/week-03-advanced-docker/lab-03-container-security

    Every file you create in this lab goes into this directory. Stay here for all remaining steps unless a step explicitly tells you to change directory.

    Install Trivy (security scanner)
    # macOS brew install trivy # Linux wget -qO - https://aquasecurity.github.io/trivy-repo/deb/public.key | sudo apt-key add - echo "deb https://aquasecurity.github.io/trivy-repo/deb $(lsb_release -sc) main" | sudo tee -a /etc/apt/sources.list.d/trivy.list sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get install trivy
    Scan a vulnerable image
    # Scan an older image (likely has vulnerabilities) trivy image node:16 # Scan with severity filter trivy image --severity HIGH,CRITICAL node:16 # Save report trivy image -f json -o report.json node:16
    Compare with updated image
    # Scan latest version trivy image node:18-alpine # Notice fewer vulnerabilities in newer, minimal images
    Scan your own images
    # Build an image from previous labs docker build -t my-app:v1 . # Scan it trivy image my-app:v1 # Fix vulnerabilities by updating base image and dependencies # Rebuild and scan again
    Integrate into CI/CD (preview)
    # Fail build if HIGH/CRITICAL vulnerabilities found trivy image --exit-code 1 --severity HIGH,CRITICAL my-app:v1 # Exit code 1 = vulnerabilities found (fails CI pipeline) # Exit code 0 = clean image (passes CI pipeline)

    Lab 4Production-Ready Configuration

    Objective: Add health checks, logging, and resource limits to a Compose stack.

    Navigate to your lab working directory:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course/week-03-advanced-docker/lab-04-production-ready

    Every file you create in this lab goes into this directory. Stay here for all remaining steps unless a step explicitly tells you to change directory.

    Extend Lab 1 with production features

    Take your docker-compose.yml from Lab 1 and enhance it with production patterns.

    Add comprehensive health checks
    services: api: # ... existing config ... healthcheck: test: ["CMD", "curl", "-f", "http://localhost:3000/health"] interval: 30s timeout: 10s retries: 3 start_period: 40s
    Configure logging
    services: api: # ... existing config ... logging: driver: "json-file" options: max-size: "10m" max-file: "3"
    Add resource limits
    services: api: # ... existing config ... deploy: resources: limits: cpus: '0.5' memory: 512M reservations: cpus: '0.25' memory: 256M
    Add restart policies
    services: api: restart: unless-stopped database: restart: unless-stopped cache: restart: unless-stopped
    Test failure scenarios
    # Kill a container and watch it restart docker-compose ps docker kill week3-compose-lab_api_1 docker-compose ps # Should show restarting # Monitor health status watch -n 1 docker-compose ps # View logs during restart docker-compose logs -f api
    Monitor resource usage
    # Real-time resource monitoring docker stats # Verify limits are enforced docker-compose top

    Lab 5Environment-Specific Configurations

    Objective: Use Compose override files for dev/staging/production environments.

    Navigate to your lab working directory:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course/week-03-advanced-docker/lab-05-env-configs

    Every file you create in this lab goes into this directory. Stay here for all remaining steps unless a step explicitly tells you to change directory.

    Create base configuration (docker-compose.yml)
    version: '3.8' services: app: image: my-app:latest ports: - "3000:3000" environment: NODE_ENV: production
    Create development override (docker-compose.override.yml)
    version: '3.8' services: app: build: . # Build locally instead of using image volumes: - ./src:/app/src # Hot reload environment: NODE_ENV: development DEBUG: true command: npm run dev
    Create production override (docker-compose.prod.yml)
    version: '3.8' services: app: deploy: replicas: 3 resources: limits: memory: 1G restart: unless-stopped logging: driver: "json-file" options: max-size: "50m" max-file: "5"
    Run in different environments
    # Development (auto-loads docker-compose.override.yml) docker-compose up # Production docker-compose -f docker-compose.yml -f docker-compose.prod.yml up -d # Staging (create docker-compose.staging.yml) docker-compose -f docker-compose.yml -f docker-compose.staging.yml up -d
    Verify configuration merging
    # View merged configuration docker-compose config # View production merged config docker-compose -f docker-compose.yml -f docker-compose.prod.yml config

    Weekly Quiz

    Complete this 10-question multiple-choice quiz to assess your understanding. This quiz is auto-gradable and formatted for Google Classroom. Each question has one correct answer.

    Week 3 Quiz - Advanced Docker & Multi-Container Applications

    1. What is the primary purpose of Docker Compose?

    • A) To compile Docker images from source code
    • B) To provide container security scanning
    • C) To define and run multi-container applications with a single configuration file
    • D) To monitor container performance in production

    2. In a Dockerfile, what is the purpose of using multiple FROM statements?

    • A) To create container replicas for high availability
    • B) To implement multi-stage builds that reduce final image size
    • C) To specify fallback base images if the primary is unavailable
    • D) To enable multi-architecture image builds

    3. Which command shows you the merged Docker Compose configuration without starting containers?

    • A) docker-compose validate
    • B) docker-compose ps
    • C) docker-compose config
    • D) docker-compose inspect

    4. What is the security risk of running containers as the root user?

    • A) Root containers consume more CPU resources
    • B) Root containers cannot access network services
    • C) Root containers can potentially escalate privileges and compromise the host system
    • D) Root containers are incompatible with health checks

    5. In Docker Compose, what does depends_on with condition: service_healthy ensure?

    • A) The dependent service has a valid image
    • B) The dependent service is the correct version
    • C) The dependent service's health check passes before starting the dependent container
    • D) The dependent service has network connectivity

    6. Which tool is commonly used for container vulnerability scanning?

    • A) Docker Compose
    • B) Docker Swarm
    • C) Trivy
    • D) Dockerfile

    7. What is the purpose of setting resource limits (deploy.resources.limits) in Docker Compose?

    • A) To improve container startup time
    • B) To prevent containers from consuming excessive CPU/memory and affecting other services
    • C) To enable automatic horizontal scaling
    • D) To specify minimum hardware requirements for the host

    8. What does docker-compose down -v do that docker-compose down doesn't?

    • A) Validates the compose file syntax
    • B) Creates verbose output logs
    • C) Removes named volumes, deleting all persistent data
    • D) Forces removal even if containers are running

    9. Which restart policy ensures a container restarts unless explicitly stopped by the user?

    • A) no
    • B) always
    • C) unless-stopped
    • D) on-failure

    10. What is the primary benefit of using distroless or alpine base images?

    • A) Faster network connectivity
    • B) Better CPU performance
    • C) Smaller image size and reduced attack surface
    • D) Automatic container orchestration

    Scoring: 10 correct = Mastery (100%), 8-9 correct = Proficient (80-90%), 6-7 correct = Developing (60-70%), Below 6 = Needs Review (review teaching notes and labs)

    Weekly Test

    Complete these 5 scenario-based questions that require applied problem-solving. These questions assess your ability to analyze situations and apply Week 3 concepts to real-world challenges.

    • Scenario 1: Your Docker Compose application has three services: web, api, and database. When you run docker-compose up, the API service starts immediately and crashes with "connection refused" errors when trying to connect to the database. The database service is starting slowly (takes 30 seconds to initialize). How would you fix this startup timing issue? Provide the specific Compose configuration changes.

      Explanation: The health check verifies PostgreSQL is ready to accept connections, not just that the container is running. start_period: 30s gives the database time to initialize without marking it unhealthy. depends_on with condition: service_healthy ensures API waits until the database health check passes. This eliminates race conditions and connection errors.

  • Scenario 2: Your Node.js application Docker image is 1.8GB, causing slow deployments and high storage costs. The image uses FROM node:18 and includes TypeScript source files, devDependencies, and build tools. Redesign the Dockerfile using multi-stage builds to reduce the image size. Show your complete Dockerfile.

    Explanation: Builder stage includes TypeScript compiler and devDependencies (400MB+). Production stage uses alpine (smaller base), installs only production dependencies, copies compiled JavaScript from builder, runs as non-root user. Expected size reduction: 1.8GB → 200-300MB (~85% reduction). Security improvements: alpine base, non-root user, no source code or build tools.

  • Scenario 3: You run trivy image myapp:latest and discover 15 HIGH and 3 CRITICAL vulnerabilities. The base image is node:14. Your CI/CD pipeline should fail if any HIGH or CRITICAL vulnerabilities exist. What specific steps would you take to remediate these vulnerabilities and configure Trivy to fail the build?

    Step 2 - Update system packages:
    RUN apk update && apk upgrade --no-cache
    Step 3 - CI/CD integration with exit codes:
    # In CI/CD pipeline (GitHub Actions, GitLab CI, etc.) trivy image --exit-code 1 --severity HIGH,CRITICAL myapp:latest # Or with detailed output trivy image \ --exit-code 1 \ --severity HIGH,CRITICAL \ --format json \ --output trivy-report.json \ myapp:latest
    Explanation: Node 14 is EOL (end-of-life) with known unpatched vulnerabilities. Node 20 is the current LTS with security patches. Alpine adds minimal attack surface. --exit-code 1 fails the pipeline if vulnerabilities found. Regular scanning (weekly) catches new CVEs. Automate with scheduled CI jobs.
