  • Scenario 4: Your production docker-compose.yml works perfectly in development but needs production-specific configuration: replicas=3 for the web service, stricter resource limits (512MB memory max), production logging driver (json-file with rotation), and image pulls from Docker Hub instead of local builds. Design the file structure and show the docker-compose.prod.yml override file.

    docker-compose.prod.yml:
    version: '3.8' services: web: image: myregistry/myapp:${VERSION} deploy: replicas: 3 resources: limits: cpus: '1' memory: 512M reservations: cpus: '0.5' memory: 256M restart_policy: condition: unless-stopped logging: driver: "json-file" options: max-size: "10m" max-file: "3" environment: NODE_ENV: production
    Deployment command:
    docker-compose -f docker-compose.yml -f docker-compose.prod.yml up -d
    Explanation: Base file contains common config. Prod override specifies registry image (no build), scales to 3 replicas, enforces memory limits, configures log rotation to prevent disk filling, sets production environment. This pattern maintains consistency while environment-specific.

  • Scenario 5: You're troubleshooting a container that keeps restarting every 2-3 minutes. docker-compose logs api shows no errors. docker-compose ps shows the container status as "Restarting (137)" every few minutes. What does exit code 137 indicate? What are three possible root causes, and how would you diagnose each?

    Root Cause 1 - Memory Limit Exceeded:
    # Check resource limits docker-compose config | grep -A 5 "deploy:" # Monitor actual usage docker stats # Solution: Increase memory limit or fix memory leak
    Root Cause 2 - Memory Leak in Application:
    # Inspect memory usage before crash docker stats --no-stream # Profile application memory docker-compose exec api node --inspect app.js # Solution: Fix leak (unreleased connections, growing arrays, etc.)
    Root Cause 3 - Insufficient Host Memory:
    # Check host memory free -h # Check kernel logs for OOM dmesg | grep -i oom # Solution: Add host memory or reduce container count
    Explanation: Exit 137 = memory issue 95% of time. docker stats shows real-time usage. Memory limits may be too restrictive or application may have leak. If no explicit limit, host OOM killer killed container. Fix by increasing limits, fixing leaks, or scaling host resources.

    • Weekly Assignment

    Project Folder Source of Truth (Week 03)

    Put the final weekly assignment deliverables inside ~/devops-engineering-course/week-03-advanced-docker/weekly-project/. This folder is the grading source of truth for Google Classroom and mentorship review.

    • Main submission document: create or update week-03-advanced-docker/weekly-project/README.md as the assignment brief, summary, and runbook.
    • Supporting artefacts: keep screenshots, diagrams, exported evidence, configs, and code samples in clearly named files under week-03-advanced-docker/weekly-project/.
    • Lab handoff rule: if the weekly project builds on a lab, copy or reference the relevant files from the lab folder into weekly-project/ rather than leaving your final submission scattered across lab directories.
    • Submission path: when you submit to Google Classroom, include the repository URL and the exact path week-03-advanced-docker/weekly-project/.
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course/week-03-advanced-docker/weekly-project git add . git commit -m "feat(week-03-advanced-docker): submit week 03 weekly project artefacts" git push

    Assignment Title: Production-Ready Multi-Service Application Deployment

    Assignment Overview

    Design and implement a complete production-ready multi-service application using all Week 3 concepts: Docker Compose orchestration, multi-stage builds, container security, health checks, resource management, and environment-specific configurations. This assignment integrates all weekly learning objectives.

    Requirements

    Part 1: Application Architecture (20 points)

    Build a Task Management System with the following services:

    • Frontend: React application (port 3000)
    • API: Node.js/Express REST API (port 8080)
    • Database: PostgreSQL 15 (port 5432)
    • Cache: Redis 7 (port 6379)
    • Proxy: Nginx reverse proxy (port 80)
    Part 2: Docker Compose Configuration (25 points)

    Create three Compose files:

    • docker-compose.yml: Base configuration shared across environments
    • docker-compose.override.yml: Development-specific (volume mounts for hot reload, debug ports)
    • docker-compose.prod.yml: Production configuration (replicas, strict limits, security hardening)

    Must include: Service dependencies with health conditions, custom networks (frontend/backend separation), named volumes for persistence, environment variable substitution from .env file

    Part 3: Multi-Stage Dockerfiles (20 points)

    Create optimized multi-stage Dockerfiles for frontend and API:

    • Builder stage for compiling/transpiling code
    • Production stage with alpine base image
    • Non-root user for running applications
    • Document before/after image sizes showing at least 60% reduction
    Part 4: Security Implementation (15 points)
    • All images scanned with Trivy showing zero CRITICAL/HIGH vulnerabilities
    • All containers run as non-root users
    • Secrets managed through .env files (not hardcoded)
    • Read-only root filesystems where possible
    • Network segmentation (frontend/backend networks)
    Part 5: Production Readiness (15 points)
    • Health checks for all services (HTTP or command-based)
    • Resource limits (CPU and memory) for all containers
    • Restart policy: unless-stopped
    • Logging configuration: json-file driver with rotation (10MB max, 3 files)
    • Nginx configured with reverse proxy and static file serving
    Part 6: Documentation (5 points)

    Create comprehensive README.md including:

    • Architecture diagram (ASCII art or image)
    • Setup instructions for development and production
    • Environment variables documentation
    • Troubleshooting guide with common issues
    • Image size comparisons (before/after multi-stage)
    • Trivy scan reports showing security compliance

    Deliverables

    • GitHub repository with all code, Dockerfiles, and Compose files
    • README.md with complete documentation
    • Screenshots showing:
      • docker-compose ps with all services healthy
      • docker images showing image sizes
      • Trivy scan results (zero CRITICAL/HIGH)
      • Working application in browser
      • docker stats showing resource usage
    • Recorded demo (2-3 minutes) showing:
      • Development startup: docker-compose up
      • Production startup with prod config
      • Application functionality (CRUD operations)
      • Automatic restart after killing a container

    Submission

    • Deadline: 7 days from assignment date
    • Format: GitHub repository URL submitted via Google Classroom
    • Repository must include: All source code, Dockerfiles, docker-compose files, .env.example, README.md, screenshots/ folder, demo video (or link)

    Assignment Rubric (100 points total)

    Criteria Exemplary (90-100%) Proficient (75-89%) Developing (60-74%) Needs Improvement (<60%)< /th>
    Architecture
    (20 pts)    		 All 5 services implemented, working integration, proper separation of concerns, excellent design decisions All services present, mostly working, minor integration issues, adequate design Missing 1 service or significant integration problems, basic design Multiple services missing or non-functional architecture
    Compose Config
    (25 pts)    		 Three files perfect, all features (health checks, networks, volumes, env vars), flawless merging, production-grade Three files present, most features working, minor config issues, functional Files incomplete, missing key features, configuration errors, partially working Compose files missing or fundamentally broken
    Multi-Stage Builds
    (20 pts)    		 Optimized Dockerfiles, >60% size reduction, perfect separation, comprehensive documentation Multi-stage implemented, 40-60% reduction, functional, basic documentation Multi-stage attempted, <40% reduction, some issues, minimal documentation No multi-stage or completely ineffective
    Security
    (15 pts)    		 Zero CRITICAL/HIGH vulns, all non-root, secrets managed, network segmented, read-only FS, comprehensive security Low vuln count, mostly non-root, secrets handled, some security measures Some vulns present, inconsistent security, basic measures High vuln count, root users, hardcoded secrets, insecure
    Production Readiness
    (15 pts)    		 All health checks, proper resource limits, logging configured, restart policies, production-grade Most features present, adequate limits, basic logging, functional Some features missing, incomplete configuration, partially production-ready Missing critical production features, not deployment-ready
    Documentation
    (5 pts)    		 Comprehensive README, diagrams, troubleshooting, all screenshots, professional quality Good README, key information present, most screenshots, adequate Basic README, missing information, few screenshots, minimal Poor or missing documentation
    💡 Success Tips
    • Start early—this is a comprehensive assignment requiring 8-12 hours
    • Test development environment first, then tackle production configuration
    • Run Trivy scans early to avoid last-minute vulnerability issues
    • Document as you go—don't leave documentation until the end
    • Test failure scenarios: kill containers, exceed memory limits, verify auto-restart
    • Use docker-compose config to debug configuration merging
    • Commit frequently to GitHub with meaningful messages

    Use Week 3 to evaluate whether the learner can communicate engineering progression. The strongest submissions show a clear before-and-after narrative from Week 2 to Week 3, with explicit reasoning for Compose adoption, optimization choices, and security hardening.

    • Check that Compose and multi-stage builds are present as files, not only described in prose.
    • Require evidence of debugging, scans, or validation output instead of accepting unsupported claims.
    • Assess whether README sections support mentor reproduction and recruiter-style portfolio review.
    • Prioritize reproducibility, documentation quality, and architectural reasoning alongside technical correctness.
    Mentorship Review for Week 3

    Mentorship students: your Week 3 work (Advanced Docker & Multi-Container Applications) receives expert feedback on:

    • Core concepts: accuracy and depth of the week's technical objectives
    • Implementation quality: correctness, reliability, and operational readiness
    • Security and risk: safeguards, least privilege, and failure handling
    • Architecture decisions: tradeoffs, scalability, and maintainability
    • Documentation: clarity, reproducibility, and deployment notes

    Submit via Google Classroom and schedule a review call for feedback and portfolio guidance.

    4

    Container CI/CD & Automation

    Building Automated Pipelines for Containerized Applications

    This week bridges the gap between container technology and production deployment automation. You've mastered Docker and multi-container orchestration—now you'll automate the entire lifecycle from code commit to production deployment. We'll build comprehensive CI/CD pipelines that automatically build images, run tests, scan for vulnerabilities, and deploy to staging and production environments. By week's end, you'll have implemented a complete automated workflow that mirrors industry practices at companies like Netflix, Spotify, and Airbnb, where every code commit triggers automated quality gates and deployments happen dozens of times per day with confidence.

    Mentorship Guidance

    Mentorship students receive structured support for Container CI/CD & Automation: live Q&A, assignment feedback, and office hours focused on this week's goals.

    Share blockers in Google Classroom and schedule a review session once your lab and assignment work are ready.

    Learning Objectives

    By the end of this week, you will be able to demonstrate mastery across all levels of Bloom's Taxonomy:

    Remember

    Define CI/CD pipeline stages, artifacts, and key terminology. Recall the purpose of each pipeline stage (build, test, scan, deploy).

    Understand

    Explain how container CI/CD differs from traditional application CI/CD. Describe the automated workflow from git push to production deployment.

    Apply

    Implement GitHub Actions workflows for automated Docker builds. Configure multi-stage pipelines with quality gates and approvals.

    Analyze

    Troubleshoot failed pipeline executions. Compare different CI/CD platforms (GitHub Actions vs GitLab CI vs Jenkins) for container workflows.

    Evaluate

    Assess pipeline efficiency and identify bottlenecks. Critique security practices in container CI/CD workflows and recommend improvements.

    Create

    Design end-to-end CI/CD pipelines for multi-service applications. Build custom GitHub Actions for specialized container workflows.

    Pre-Class Exercises

    Complete these exercises before studying the main content. These activities prepare you for advanced CI/CD concepts by connecting your Week 1-3 knowledge to automated workflows. Budget 2-3 hours for this preparatory work.

    ⚙ Week 04 Workspace Setup — Complete This First

    Create your working directory for this week inside the course repository. Every pre-class note, lab file, screenshot, and project deliverable belongs under this folder tree — nothing goes anywhere else.

    1. Navigate to your course repository root:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course
    2. Create this week's folder and all sub-directories in one command:
    mkdir -p week-04-container-cicd/{preclass-notes,lab-01-github-actions-basics,lab-02-multi-stage-pipeline,lab-03-automated-deployment,lab-04-production-deployment,lab-05-complete-pipeline,weekly-project}

    This single command creates the whole week's workspace tree. You will stay within this tree for pre-class work, every lab, and the weekly project.

    3. Verify the structure was created correctly:
    find week-04-container-cicd -type d | sort

    You should see 7 directories (preclass-notes + 5 lab folders + weekly-project).

    4. Create your pre-class notes file:
    touch week-04-container-cicd/preclass-notes/week-04-notes.md

    All research, written answers, and reflections for this week's pre-class exercises go into this Markdown file. Keep it open as you work through the exercises below.

    5. Commit the empty scaffold to your GitHub portfolio:
    git add week-04-container-cicd/ git commit -m "chore(week-04-container-cicd): scaffold week 04 working directory" git push

    Pushing the empty scaffold creates a timestamped entry on your GitHub profile showing when you started this week — visible evidence for your portfolio and mentorship reviews.

    📁 Week 04 Folder Structure
    ~/devops-engineering-course/ └── week-04-container-cicd/ ├── preclass-notes/ ├── lab-01-github-actions-basics/ ├── lab-02-multi-stage-pipeline/ ├── lab-03-automated-deployment/ ├── lab-04-production-deployment/ ├── lab-05-complete-pipeline/ └── weekly-project/

    1Manual Deployment Pain Points Analysis

    Reflect on your Week 3 assignment where you manually built images, ran tests, and deployed containers. Document 5-7 specific pain points or inefficiencies you experienced. For each pain point, estimate how much time it consumed and how prone to human error it was. Example pain points: forgetting to tag images correctly, manually copying environment variables, waiting for builds to complete while watching terminal output, manually verifying deployment success.

    💡 Reflection Focus

    This exercise helps you appreciate WHY automation matters. Every manual step you identify is a candidate for automation. Keep this list—you'll automate these exact pain points this week.

    2GitHub Actions Exploration

    Visit github.com/features/actions and explore the GitHub Actions documentation. Read the "Understanding GitHub Actions" quickstart guide. Then, browse the GitHub Actions Marketplace and identify 5 pre-built actions that relate to Docker or containers. For each action, note:

    • Action name and what it does
    • Who maintains it (official vs community)
    • Number of stars/usage (popularity indicator)
    • One potential use case in your projects

    Popular actions to look for: docker/build-push-action, docker/login-action, aquasecurity/trivy-action, actions/checkout, actions/cache

    3CI/CD Pipeline Diagram Study

    Search for "container CI/CD pipeline diagram" or "Docker CI/CD workflow" images. Study 3-5 different pipeline visualizations and identify common stages. Create your own diagram (hand-drawn or digital) showing what you think a complete container CI/CD pipeline should include. Include at minimum: code repository, build stage, test stage, image registry, and deployment stage. Add any other stages you think are necessary (security scanning, approval gates, notifications, etc.).

    📊 Diagram Template

    Your diagram doesn't need to be perfect. The goal is activating prior knowledge and building a mental model. You'll refine this understanding as you progress through the week.

    4Docker Registry Research

    Research three container registry options and compare them:

    • Docker Hub: hub.docker.com - Official Docker registry
    • GitHub Container Registry: ghcr.io - Integrated with GitHub
    • AWS ECR: Elastic Container Registry - AWS-managed private registry

    For each registry, document: pricing (free tier details), private repository limits, integration with CI/CD tools, and vulnerability scanning features. Create a comparison table to reference during pipeline design this week.

    5YAML Syntax Review

    GitHub Actions workflows use YAML syntax (same as Docker Compose). Review YAML fundamentals:

    • Key-value pairs: name: value
    • Lists: - item1, - item2
    • Nested objects: proper indentation (2 spaces, no tabs)
    • Multi-line strings: | for literal, > for folded
    • Comments: # This is a comment

    Practice by writing a simple YAML file with nested structures. Use yamllint.com to validate your syntax. This fluency will accelerate your pipeline development this week.

    Detailed Teaching Notes

    4.1 CI/CD Fundamentals for Containers

    While we explored CI/CD concepts in Week 1, containerized applications require specialized pipeline considerations. Traditional CI/CD pipelines focused on compiling code, running tests, and deploying artifacts. Container pipelines add new stages and complexity.

    🎯 Container CI/CD Paradigm Shift

    In container-based workflows, the Docker image itself becomes the deployable artifact. You're no longer deploying JAR files, Python packages, or compiled binaries—you're building, testing, and deploying immutable container images that include the application code, runtime, dependencies, and configuration.

    Traditional vs. Container CI/CD Stages:

    # Traditional CI/CD Pipeline 1. Checkout Code 2. Install Dependencies (npm install, pip install) 3. Run Tests 4. Build Artifact (compile, package) 5. Deploy to Server (SSH, copy files, restart service) # Container CI/CD Pipeline 1. Checkout Code 2. Build Docker Image (includes dependencies, tests) 3. Run Tests (in containers) 4. Scan Image for Vulnerabilities (security gate) 5. Push Image to Registry (artifact storage) 6. Deploy Container to Environment (pull + run image)

    Key Differences That Matter:

    • Immutable Artifacts: Once built, container images never change. Each deployment uses exact same image tested in CI.
    • Registry Dependency: Docker registries (Docker Hub, ghcr.io, ECR) become critical infrastructure—equivalent to npm, PyPI in traditional workflows.
    • Image Layers & Caching: Build performance depends heavily on layer caching strategy. Poor Dockerfile design = 20-minute builds instead of 2-minute builds.
    • Security Scanning: Images contain OS packages that may have vulnerabilities. Automated scanning (Trivy, Snyk) becomes mandatory pipeline stage.
    • Multi-Platform Builds: May need to build for different architectures (amd64, arm64) to support various deployment targets.
    • Size Optimization: Image size directly impacts deployment speed. 2GB image takes 5 minutes to pull; 200MB takes 30 seconds.
    💡 Real-World Impact

    At scale, these differences compound dramatically. A company deploying 100 times per day with 10-minute image builds wastes 1,000 minutes (16+ hours) of CI/CD runner time daily. Optimizing to 2-minute builds saves 800 minutes—reclaiming entire workdays of compute resources. CI/CD efficiency is infrastructure cost management.

    4.2 GitHub Actions Architecture

    GitHub Actions is GitHub's native CI/CD platform, deeply integrated with repositories. Unlike Jenkins (self-hosted servers) or CircleCI (cloud service), Actions runs directly within your GitHub repository environment with zero setup overhead.

    📖 GitHub Actions Core Concepts

    Workflow: Automated process defined in YAML file (.github/workflows/*.yml). Triggered by events (push, PR, schedule).

    Job: Set of steps that execute on the same runner. Workflows contain multiple jobs that run in parallel or sequentially.

    Step: Individual task within a job. Can run commands or call reusable Actions.

    Runner: Server that executes jobs. GitHub-hosted (free tier: 2,000 minutes/month) or self-hosted (your infrastructure).

    Action: Reusable component from GitHub Marketplace (like npm packages for CI/CD).

    Architecture Hierarchy:

    Repository └── .github/workflows/ # Workflow definitions directory ├── ci.yml # Continuous Integration workflow ├── deploy-staging.yml # Staging deployment workflow └── deploy-prod.yml # Production deployment workflow Each Workflow contains: └── Multiple Jobs (build, test, deploy) └── Each Job contains: └── Multiple Steps (checkout, build, test, push) └── Each Step runs: └── Commands OR Actions

    GitHub-Hosted vs Self-Hosted Runners:

    Runner Comparison

    GitHub-Hosted Runners

    ✅ Pros: Zero setup, maintained by GitHub, clean environment every run, free tier (2,000 min/month public repos, 3,000 min/month Team plan), multiple OS options (Ubuntu, Windows, macOS).

    ❌ Cons: Slower (cold starts, no persistent caching), public internet only (can't access private networks), limited resources (2-core CPU, 7GB RAM), minute quotas (overage charges), can't customize environment deeply.

    Self-Hosted Runners

    ✅ Pros: Faster (persistent caching, pre-warmed), unlimited minutes, custom hardware specs, private network access (VPN, internal services), can install custom tools, full environment control.

    ❌ Cons: You manage infrastructure (updates, security, scaling), compute costs (AWS/Azure bills), security responsibility (compromised runner = security risk), maintenance overhead.

    When to Use Each: Start with GitHub-hosted runners (simplicity). Switch to self-hosted when: (1) minute quotas become expensive (>$50/month), (2) builds are slow due to lack of caching, (3) need private network access, or (4) require specialized hardware (GPUs, large memory).

    4.3 Workflow Syntax & Triggers

    GitHub Actions workflows are defined in YAML files stored in .github/workflows/ directory. Let's build a complete container CI workflow from scratch, explaining each section.

    Basic Workflow Structure:

    # .github/workflows/docker-ci.yml name: Docker CI # Workflow name (shows in GitHub UI) on: # Trigger configuration push: branches: [ main, develop ] # Run on pushes to these branches pull_request: branches: [ main ] # Run on PRs targeting main workflow_dispatch: # Manual trigger button in GitHub UI env: # Global environment variables REGISTRY: ghcr.io # GitHub Container Registry IMAGE_NAME: ${{ github.repository }} # e.g., username/repo-name jobs: # Workflow contains multiple jobs build: # Job identifier runs-on: ubuntu-latest # Runner environment permissions: # Required for pushing to ghcr.io contents: read packages: write steps: # Ordered list of tasks - name: Checkout code # Step 1: Get repository code uses: actions/checkout@v4 # Reusable Action from marketplace - name: Log in to Registry # Step 2: Authenticate to registry uses: docker/login-action@v3 with: registry: ${{ env.REGISTRY }} username: ${{ github.actor }} password: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }} - name: Build and push # Step 3: Build + push image uses: docker/build-push-action@v5 with: context: . push: true tags: ${{ env.REGISTRY }}/${{ env.IMAGE_NAME }}:latest

    Detailed Trigger Options (on:):

    # Push Triggers - Run when code is pushed on: push: branches: [ main, develop, 'release/**' ] # Branch patterns paths: # Only if specific files change - 'src/**' - 'Dockerfile' - '.github/workflows/docker-ci.yml' tags: # Run on version tags - 'v*.*.*' # v1.2.3, v2.0.0, etc. # Pull Request Triggers - Run on PR events on: pull_request: branches: [ main ] types: [ opened, synchronize, reopened ] # PR lifecycle events # Schedule Triggers - Run on cron schedule on: schedule: - cron: '0 2 * * 1-5' # 2 AM UTC, weekdays only # Useful for: nightly security scans, weekly image rebuilds # Manual Triggers - Button in GitHub UI on: workflow_dispatch: # No parameters inputs: # With custom inputs environment: description: 'Deployment environment' required: true default: 'staging' type: choice options: [ 'staging', 'production' ] # Multiple Triggers - Combine any of above on: push: branches: [ main ] pull_request: schedule: - cron: '0 0 * * 0' # Weekly on Sunday midnight workflow_dispatch:

    Job Dependencies & Conditional Execution:

    jobs: # Job 1: Build and test build: runs-on: ubuntu-latest steps: - uses: actions/checkout@v4 - name: Build image run: docker build -t myapp:test . - name: Run tests run: docker run myapp:test npm test # Job 2: Security scan (runs in parallel with build) security: runs-on: ubuntu-latest steps: - uses: actions/checkout@v4 - name: Run Trivy scan uses: aquasecurity/trivy-action@master with: scan-type: 'fs' severity: 'CRITICAL,HIGH' # Job 3: Deploy (waits for build AND security to succeed) deploy: needs: [ build, security ] # Job dependencies runs-on: ubuntu-latest if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main' # Conditional: only on main branch steps: - name: Deploy to staging run: echo "Deploying to staging..." # Job 4: Notify (runs even if previous jobs fail) notify: needs: [ deploy ] runs-on: ubuntu-latest if: always() # Run regardless of previous failures steps: - name: Send Slack notification run: echo "Build complete"
    ⚠️ Common Workflow Mistakes
    • Missing permissions: Pushing to ghcr.io requires packages: write permission. Default is read-only.
    • Wrong trigger syntax: on: push: (colon after push) vs on: [push] (array syntax). Both work, but don't mix.
    • Workflow not triggering: File must be in .github/workflows/ directory and named *.yml or *.yaml.
    • Secrets in logs: GitHub masks registered secrets, but not all sensitive data. Be careful with debug output.

    4.4 Building & Pushing Docker Images Automatically

    The core of container CI/CD is automated image building and registry pushing. The docker/build-push-action is the industry-standard Action for this, maintained by Docker themselves.

    Complete Build & Push Workflow:

    # .github/workflows/docker-build-push.yml name: Build and Push Docker Image on: push: branches: [ main ] pull_request: branches: [ main ] env: REGISTRY: ghcr.io IMAGE_NAME: ${{ github.repository }} jobs: build: runs-on: ubuntu-latest permissions: contents: read packages: write steps: # Step 1: Checkout repository code - name: Checkout code uses: actions/checkout@v4 # Step 2: Set up Docker Buildx (advanced builder with caching) - name: Set up Docker Buildx uses: docker/setup-buildx-action@v3 # Step 3: Log in to GitHub Container Registry - name: Log in to Registry uses: docker/login-action@v3 with: registry: ${{ env.REGISTRY }} username: ${{ github.actor }} password: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }} # Step 4: Extract metadata (tags, labels) - name: Extract Docker metadata id: meta uses: docker/metadata-action@v5 with: images: ${{ env.REGISTRY }}/${{ env.IMAGE_NAME }} tags: | type=ref,event=branch type=ref,event=pr type=semver,pattern={{version}} type=semver,pattern={{major}}.{{minor}} type=sha,prefix={{branch}}- # Step 5: Build and push Docker image - name: Build and push uses: docker/build-push-action@v5 with: context: . file: ./Dockerfile push: ${{ github.event_name != 'pull_request' }} tags: ${{ steps.meta.outputs.tags }} labels: ${{ steps.meta.outputs.labels }} cache-from: type=gha cache-to: type=gha,mode=max

    Breaking Down Each Step:

    1. Docker Buildx Setup: Buildx is Docker's next-generation build engine with advanced features: layer caching, multi-platform builds (amd64, arm64), build secrets, and remote builders. docker/setup-buildx-action installs and configures it automatically.

    2. Registry Authentication: docker/login-action handles authentication to any Docker registry. For GitHub Container Registry (ghcr.io), use the built-in GITHUB_TOKEN secret—no manual secret creation needed. For Docker Hub, create repository secret DOCKERHUB_TOKEN.

    3. Metadata Extraction: docker/metadata-action automatically generates appropriate tags and labels based on the trigger event:

    # Example generated tags for different events: # Push to main branch: ghcr.io/username/repo:main ghcr.io/username/repo:main-abc123f # Push tag v1.2.3: ghcr.io/username/repo:1.2.3 ghcr.io/username/repo:1.2 ghcr.io/username/repo:latest # Pull request #42: ghcr.io/username/repo:pr-42 # Branch feature/auth: ghcr.io/username/repo:feature-auth

    4. Build & Push with Caching: The cache-from and cache-to parameters enable GitHub Actions cache (type=gha), dramatically speeding up subsequent builds.

    ⚡ Layer Caching Strategy

    Without caching, every build starts from scratch: apt-get install, npm install, pip install all re-run. With GitHub Actions cache, unchanged layers (like dependencies) are reused. Result: 15-minute builds become 2-minute builds.

    mode=max caches all intermediate layers (not just final), maximizing reuse. Without it, only the final image layers are cached.

    Multi-Platform Builds (Advanced):

    - name: Build multi-platform image uses: docker/build-push-action@v5 with: context: . platforms: linux/amd64,linux/arm64 # Build for Intel + ARM push: true tags: ${{ env.REGISTRY }}/${{ env.IMAGE_NAME }}:latest cache-from: type=gha cache-to: type=gha,mode=max # Use case: ARM-based servers (AWS Graviton, Raspberry Pi) # or Apple M1/M2 Macs in development. # One workflow builds images for all platforms automatically.
    💡 Registry Best Practices
    • Use ghcr.io for public/open-source projects: Free, unlimited storage, fast, GitHub-integrated.
    • Use Docker Hub for maximum compatibility: Most widely supported, 200GB free for open-source.
    • Use cloud registries (ECR, ACR, GCR) for production: Same-region deployments (faster pulls), security scanning, IAM integration.
    • Tag strategy: Always tag with git-sha for traceability. Use semantic versions (v1.2.3) for releases. Update latest only on production deployments.
    • Never use :latest in production: Non-deterministic (what version is running?). Pin exact versions: myapp:v1.2.3.

    Build Optimization Techniques:

    # 1. Use .dockerignore (reduce build context size) # .dockerignore node_modules/ .git/ *.md .env coverage/ # Smaller context = faster upload to build environment # 2. Order Dockerfile instructions by change frequency FROM node:20-alpine WORKDIR /app COPY package*.json ./ # Changes rarely (dependencies) RUN npm ci --only=production # Cached if package.json unchanged COPY . . # Changes frequently (source code) CMD ["npm", "start"] # Result: Dependency layer reused, only app code layer rebuilt # 3. Use BuildKit inline cache (for external caching) - name: Build with inline cache uses: docker/build-push-action@v5 with: context: . push: true tags: myapp:latest cache-from: type=registry,ref=myapp:latest cache-to: type=inline # Stores cache in image itself (no separate cache infrastructure)
    ⚠️ Security Considerations
    • Never commit secrets to Dockerfile: Use GitHub Secrets and pass as build args: --build-arg API_KEY=${{ secrets.API_KEY }}
    • Scan images before pushing: Add Trivy scanning step before build-push (Week 3 technique).
    • Use least-privilege tokens: For Docker Hub, create access tokens with "Read, Write" only (not "Admin").
    • Rotate credentials regularly: Update registry tokens quarterly. Use short-lived tokens when possible.
    • Private repos security: Default GITHUB_TOKEN has repository scope. Self-hosted runners need firewall rules.

    4.5 Automated Testing in CI/CD Pipelines

    Testing in container pipelines differs from traditional testing. You're not just testing code—you're testing the entire containerized environment: application code, dependencies, runtime configuration, and container behavior.

    🧪 Container Testing Pyramid

    Unit Tests: Test application code in isolation. Run inside container during build (fast, many tests).

    Integration Tests: Test application with dependencies (database, cache). Run containers with docker-compose (moderate speed, moderate count).

    End-to-End Tests: Test complete system from user perspective. Deploy full environment, simulate user actions (slow, few tests).

    Security Tests: Scan images for vulnerabilities, check for misconfigurations. Run on every build (automated, critical).

    Implementing Testing in GitHub Actions:

    # .github/workflows/test-pipeline.yml name: Complete Testing Pipeline on: push: branches: [ main, develop ] pull_request: branches: [ main ] jobs: # Job 1: Unit Tests (fastest, runs first) unit-tests: runs-on: ubuntu-latest steps: - uses: actions/checkout@v4 - name: Build test image run: docker build --target test -t myapp:test . - name: Run unit tests run: | docker run --rm myapp:test npm test docker run --rm myapp:test npm run test:coverage - name: Upload coverage report uses: codecov/codecov-action@v3 with: file: ./coverage/lcov.info # Job 2: Integration Tests (needs services like DB) integration-tests: runs-on: ubuntu-latest services: postgres: image: postgres:15-alpine env: POSTGRES_PASSWORD: test_password POSTGRES_DB: test_db options: >- --health-cmd pg_isready --health-interval 10s --health-timeout 5s --health-retries 5 steps: - uses: actions/checkout@v4 - name: Build application image run: docker build -t myapp:test . - name: Run integration tests env: DATABASE_URL: postgres://postgres:test_password@postgres:5432/test_db run: | docker run --rm \ --network ${{ job.services.postgres.network }} \ -e DATABASE_URL=$DATABASE_URL \ myapp:test npm run test:integration # Job 3: E2E Tests (complete system test) e2e-tests: runs-on: ubuntu-latest steps: - uses: actions/checkout@v4 - name: Start full stack with docker-compose run: docker-compose -f docker-compose.test.yml up -d - name: Wait for services to be ready run: | timeout 60 bash -c 'until curl -f http://localhost:3000/health; do sleep 2; done' - name: Run E2E tests with Playwright run: | docker run --rm \ --network host \ mcr.microsoft.com/playwright:latest \ npx playwright test - name: Teardown if: always() run: docker-compose -f docker-compose.test.yml down

    Test Optimization Strategies:

    • Parallel Execution: Run unit, integration, and security tests in parallel jobs (save 5-10 minutes per run).
    • Test Selective Execution: Use paths: trigger to run tests only when relevant files change.
    • Cached Dependencies: Cache npm/pip packages with actions/cache to avoid re-downloading.
    • Matrix Testing: Test against multiple Node versions, databases, or configurations in parallel.
    • Fail Fast: Stop pipeline immediately on test failures to save CI minutes and get faster feedback.
    💡 Test Coverage Best Practices

    Aim for 80%+ code coverage but don't obsess over 100%. High-value areas (authentication, payment, data processing) need thorough testing. Low-risk areas (UI formatting, static content) can have lighter coverage. Quality over quantity: One good integration test beats ten shallow unit tests. Use coverage trends (improving/declining) as a metric, not absolute percentages.

    4.6 Security Scanning Integration (Trivy Action)

    Security scanning must be automated and enforced in CI/CD pipelines. Manual security reviews don't scale and catch vulnerabilities too late. Trivy is an industry-standard vulnerability scanner that integrates seamlessly with GitHub Actions.

    Complete Security Scanning Workflow:

    # .github/workflows/security-scan.yml name: Security Scanning Pipeline on: push: branches: [ main, develop ] pull_request: schedule: - cron: '0 0 * * 0' # Weekly scan on Sunday jobs: # Scan 1: Filesystem scan (Dockerfile, dependencies) fs-scan: runs-on: ubuntu-latest steps: - uses: actions/checkout@v4 - name: Run Trivy filesystem scan uses: aquasecurity/trivy-action@master with: scan-type: 'fs' scan-ref: '.' format: 'sarif' output: 'trivy-fs-results.sarif' severity: 'CRITICAL,HIGH' - name: Upload to GitHub Security tab uses: github/codeql-action/upload-sarif@v2 with: sarif_file: 'trivy-fs-results.sarif' # Scan 2: Container image scan image-scan: runs-on: ubuntu-latest steps: - uses: actions/checkout@v4 - name: Build Docker image run: docker build -t myapp:${{ github.sha }} . - name: Run Trivy image scan uses: aquasecurity/trivy-action@master with: scan-type: 'image' image-ref: 'myapp:${{ github.sha }}' format: 'table' exit-code: '1' # Fail pipeline if vulnerabilities found severity: 'CRITICAL,HIGH' ignore-unfixed: true # Only fail on fixable vulnerabilities - name: Generate HTML report if: failure() uses: aquasecurity/trivy-action@master with: scan-type: 'image' image-ref: 'myapp:${{ github.sha }}' format: 'template' template: '@/contrib/html.tpl' output: 'trivy-report.html' - name: Upload report as artifact if: failure() uses: actions/upload-artifact@v3 with: name: trivy-scan-report path: trivy-report.html # Scan 3: Configuration audit (Kubernetes YAML, Dockerfile best practices) config-audit: runs-on: ubuntu-latest steps: - uses: actions/checkout@v4 - name: Run Trivy config scan uses: aquasecurity/trivy-action@master with: scan-type: 'config' scan-ref: '.' format: 'table' exit-code: '0' # Don't fail, just report severity: 'CRITICAL,HIGH,MEDIUM'

    Vulnerability Management Workflow:

    # Handling vulnerability findings: # 1. CRITICAL/HIGH vulnerabilities block deployment exit-code: '1' # Pipeline fails, PR cannot merge # 2. MEDIUM vulnerabilities are reported but don't block exit-code: '0' # Pipeline succeeds, creates issue for tracking # 3. Use .trivyignore for accepted risks # .trivyignore CVE-2023-12345 # Accepted: affects unused component CVE-2023-67890 # Accepted: no fix available, mitigated at network level # 4. Vulnerability remediation priorities: # - CRITICAL: Fix within 24 hours, deploy immediately # - HIGH: Fix within 1 week, include in next sprint # - MEDIUM: Fix within 1 month, backlog prioritization # - LOW: Fix opportunistically, technical debt
    ⚠️ Common Security Scanning Pitfalls
    • Scanning too late: Scan after build but before push. Don't push vulnerable images to registry.
    • Ignoring unfixable CVEs: Use ignore-unfixed: true to avoid failing on vulnerabilities with no patches available yet.
    • No vulnerability tracking: Upload SARIF results to GitHub Security tab for centralized vulnerability management.
    • Alert fatigue: Start with CRITICAL/HIGH only. Add MEDIUM after team adapts. Too many alerts = ignored alerts.
    • No remediation process: Scanning without fixing is security theater. Define SLAs and track remediation metrics.

    4.7 Deployment Strategies & Environments

    Production deployment is the final and most critical CI/CD stage. Deployment strategies determine how new versions roll out, balancing speed, risk, and complexity.

    Multi-Environment Pipeline Architecture:

    # Typical environment progression: Development (dev) └── Every commit to develop branch └── Automated deployment, no approvals └── Latest unstable code, frequent changes Staging (staging) └── Merges to main branch └── Automated deployment after tests pass └── Production-like environment, final validation Production (prod) └── Git tags (v1.2.3) or manual trigger └── Manual approval required └── Blue-green or canary deployment └── Rollback plan mandatory

    Complete Multi-Environment Deployment Workflow:

    # .github/workflows/deploy.yml name: Deploy to Environments on: push: branches: [ develop, main ] tags: [ 'v*.*.*' ] workflow_dispatch: inputs: environment: description: 'Target environment' required: true type: choice options: [ 'staging', 'production' ] env: REGISTRY: ghcr.io IMAGE_NAME: ${{ github.repository }} jobs: build: runs-on: ubuntu-latest permissions: contents: read packages: write outputs: image-tag: ${{ steps.meta.outputs.tags }} steps: - uses: actions/checkout@v4 - name: Set up Docker Buildx uses: docker/setup-buildx-action@v3 - name: Log in to Registry uses: docker/login-action@v3 with: registry: ${{ env.REGISTRY }} username: ${{ github.actor }} password: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }} - name: Extract metadata id: meta uses: docker/metadata-action@v5 with: images: ${{ env.REGISTRY }}/${{ env.IMAGE_NAME }} tags: | type=ref,event=branch type=semver,pattern={{version}} type=sha,prefix={{branch}}- - name: Build and push uses: docker/build-push-action@v5 with: context: . push: true tags: ${{ steps.meta.outputs.tags }} cache-from: type=gha cache-to: type=gha,mode=max # Deploy to staging (automatic on main branch) deploy-staging: needs: build if: github.ref == 'refs/heads/main' runs-on: ubuntu-latest environment: name: staging url: https://staging.myapp.com steps: - name: Deploy to staging run: | echo "Deploying ${{ needs.build.outputs.image-tag }} to staging" # kubectl set image deployment/myapp myapp=${{ needs.build.outputs.image-tag }} # or: docker-compose pull && docker-compose up -d - name: Run smoke tests run: | sleep 10 # Wait for deployment curl -f https://staging.myapp.com/health || exit 1 - name: Notify team uses: 8398a7/action-slack@v3 with: status: ${{ job.status }} text: 'Staging deployment completed' webhook_url: ${{ secrets.SLACK_WEBHOOK }} # Deploy to production (manual approval required) deploy-production: needs: build if: startsWith(github.ref, 'refs/tags/v') runs-on: ubuntu-latest environment: name: production url: https://myapp.com steps: - name: Blue-Green Deployment Setup run: | echo "Current production: blue" echo "Deploying new version to: green" - name: Deploy to green environment run: | echo "Deploying ${{ needs.build.outputs.image-tag }} to green" # Deploy to inactive slot (green) # kubectl set image deployment/myapp-green myapp=${{ needs.build.outputs.image-tag }} - name: Smoke test green environment run: | sleep 15 curl -f https://green.myapp.com/health || exit 1 curl -f https://green.myapp.com/api/version || exit 1 - name: Switch traffic to green run: | echo "Switching load balancer to green environment" # Update load balancer rules or ingress to point to green # kubectl patch service myapp -p '{"spec":{"selector":{"version":"green"}}}' - name: Verify production traffic run: | sleep 10 curl -f https://myapp.com/health || exit 1 - name: Tag blue as previous version (rollback target) run: | echo "Keeping blue environment as rollback target for 24 hours" - name: Notify team uses: 8398a7/action-slack@v3 with: status: ${{ job.status }} text: 'Production deployment completed: ${{ github.ref_name }}' webhook_url: ${{ secrets.SLACK_WEBHOOK }}

    GitHub Environment Protection Rules:

    🛡️ Configuring Environment Protection

    In GitHub Settings → Environments → Create production:

    1. Required Reviewers: Add 2+ senior engineers who must approve before deployment runs.
    2. Wait Timer: Add 5-minute wait to catch last-minute issues before deployment.
    3. Deployment Branches: Restrict to tags matching v*.*.* pattern only.
    4. Environment Secrets: Store production credentials separate from staging (PROD_DB_PASSWORD, PROD_API_KEY).
    5. Environment URL: Link to production site for quick verification after deployment.

    Hands-On Labs

    🔬 Lab Overview

    These five labs build a complete production-ready CI/CD pipeline progressively. Start with Lab 1 (basic workflow) and add complexity in each subsequent lab. By Lab 5, you'll have a pipeline that rivals those at major tech companies. Allocate 6-8 hours total for all labs.

    Lab 1: Basic GitHub Actions Workflow for Docker Build

    Objective: Create your first GitHub Actions workflow that builds a Docker image and pushes it to GitHub Container Registry on every commit to main branch.

    Prerequisites: GitHub account, a repository with a Dockerfile, basic Docker knowledge from Weeks 2-3.

    Navigate to your lab working directory:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course/week-04-container-cicd/lab-01-github-actions-basics

    Every file you create in this lab goes into this directory. Stay here for all remaining steps unless a step explicitly tells you to change directory.

    Create Sample Application

    Create a new GitHub repository named cicd-demo. Clone it locally and add these files:

    # app.js const express = require('express'); const app = express(); const PORT = process.env.PORT || 3000; app.get('/health', (req, res) => { res.json({ status: 'healthy', timestamp: new Date() }); }); app.get('/', (req, res) => { res.json({ message: 'CI/CD Demo App', version: '1.0.0' }); }); app.listen(PORT, () => { console.log(`Server running on port ${PORT}`); });
    # package.json { "name": "cicd-demo", "version": "1.0.0", "main": "app.js", "scripts": { "start": "node app.js", "test": "echo 'Tests passed' && exit 0" }, "dependencies": { "express": "^4.18.2" } }
    # Dockerfile FROM node:20-alpine WORKDIR /app COPY package*.json ./ RUN npm ci --only=production COPY . . EXPOSE 3000 CMD ["npm", "start"]
    Create GitHub Actions Workflow

    Create directory structure and workflow file:

    mkdir -p .github/workflows touch .github/workflows/docker-build.yml

    Add this workflow content:

    # .github/workflows/docker-build.yml name: Build and Push Docker Image on: push: branches: [ main ] pull_request: branches: [ main ] env: REGISTRY: ghcr.io IMAGE_NAME: ${{ github.repository }} jobs: build: runs-on: ubuntu-latest permissions: contents: read packages: write steps: - name: Checkout repository uses: actions/checkout@v4 - name: Set up Docker Buildx uses: docker/setup-buildx-action@v3 - name: Log in to Container Registry uses: docker/login-action@v3 with: registry: ${{ env.REGISTRY }} username: ${{ github.actor }} password: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }} - name: Extract metadata id: meta uses: docker/metadata-action@v5 with: images: ${{ env.REGISTRY }}/${{ env.IMAGE_NAME }} - name: Build and push Docker image uses: docker/build-push-action@v5 with: context: . push: ${{ github.event_name != 'pull_request' }} tags: ${{ steps.meta.outputs.tags }} labels: ${{ steps.meta.outputs.labels }}
    Enable GitHub Container Registry

    Go to your GitHub repository Settings → Actions → General → Workflow permissions. Select "Read and write permissions" and save.

    Commit and Push
    git add . git commit -m "Add Docker build workflow" git push origin main

    Go to GitHub → Actions tab and watch your workflow run in real-time.

    Verify Image in Registry

    After workflow succeeds, go to your GitHub profile → Packages. You should see cicd-demo package. The image is now available at:

    ghcr.io/YOUR_USERNAME/cicd-demo:main
    Test Pulling and Running Image
    # Authenticate Docker to ghcr.io (one-time setup) echo $GITHUB_TOKEN | docker login ghcr.io -u YOUR_USERNAME --password-stdin # Pull your image docker pull ghcr.io/YOUR_USERNAME/cicd-demo:main # Run container docker run -d -p 3000:3000 --name cicd-test ghcr.io/YOUR_USERNAME/cicd-demo:main # Test application curl http://localhost:3000/health # Cleanup docker stop cicd-test && docker rm cicd-test
    Trigger Workflow on Pull Request

    Create a new branch, make a change, and open a PR:

    git checkout -b feature/update-version # Edit app.js, change version to 1.1.0 git add app.js git commit -m "Update version to 1.1.0" git push origin feature/update-version

    Go to GitHub and create a Pull Request. Notice the workflow runs automatically but doesn't push the image (only builds for validation).

    Lab 2: Multi-Stage Pipeline with Testing and Security Scanning

    Objective: Enhance the basic workflow with automated testing and vulnerability scanning. Add quality gates that prevent bad code from reaching production.

    Navigate to your lab working directory:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course/week-04-container-cicd/lab-02-multi-stage-pipeline

    Every file you create in this lab goes into this directory. Stay here for all remaining steps unless a step explicitly tells you to change directory.

    Add Test Script

    Update package.json with real tests:

    "scripts": { "start": "node app.js", "test": "node test.js" }

    Create test.js:

    // test.js - Simple test without external dependencies const http = require('http'); function testHealthEndpoint() { return new Promise((resolve, reject) => { const app = require('./app'); const server = app.listen(3001); http.get('http://localhost:3001/health', (res) => { let data = ''; res.on('data', chunk => data += chunk); res.on('end', () => { const json = JSON.parse(data); if (json.status === 'healthy') { console.log('✓ Health check test passed'); server.close(); resolve(); } else { server.close(); reject('Health check failed'); } }); }).on('error', (err) => { server.close(); reject(err); }); }); } testHealthEndpoint() .then(() => process.exit(0)) .catch(err => { console.error('✗ Tests failed:', err); process.exit(1); });
    Create Enhanced Workflow

    Replace .github/workflows/docker-build.yml with:

    name: CI/CD Pipeline with Tests and Security on: push: branches: [ main ] pull_request: branches: [ main ] env: REGISTRY: ghcr.io IMAGE_NAME: ${{ github.repository }} jobs: test: name: Run Tests runs-on: ubuntu-latest steps: - uses: actions/checkout@v4 - name: Build test image run: docker build -t myapp:test . - name: Run unit tests run: docker run --rm myapp:test npm test security-scan: name: Security Scanning runs-on: ubuntu-latest steps: - uses: actions/checkout@v4 - name: Run Trivy filesystem scan uses: aquasecurity/trivy-action@master with: scan-type: 'fs' scan-ref: '.' format: 'table' severity: 'CRITICAL,HIGH' - name: Build image for scanning run: docker build -t myapp:scan . - name: Run Trivy image scan uses: aquasecurity/trivy-action@master with: scan-type: 'image' image-ref: 'myapp:scan' format: 'table' exit-code: '1' severity: 'CRITICAL,HIGH' ignore-unfixed: true build-push: name: Build and Push needs: [ test, security-scan ] runs-on: ubuntu-latest permissions: contents: read packages: write steps: - uses: actions/checkout@v4 - name: Set up Docker Buildx uses: docker/setup-buildx-action@v3 - name: Log in to Container Registry uses: docker/login-action@v3 with: registry: ${{ env.REGISTRY }} username: ${{ github.actor }} password: ${{ secrets.GITHUB_TOKEN }} - name: Extract metadata id: meta uses: docker/metadata-action@v5 with: images: ${{ env.REGISTRY }}/${{ env.IMAGE_NAME }} tags: | type=ref,event=branch type=sha,prefix={{branch}}- - name: Build and push uses: docker/build-push-action@v5 with: context: . push: ${{ github.event_name != 'pull_request' }} tags: ${{ steps.meta.outputs.tags }} labels: ${{ steps.meta.outputs.labels }} cache-from: type=gha cache-to: type=gha,mode=max
    Test Quality Gates

    Intentionally break the test to see pipeline fail:

    # In app.js, change health endpoint to return error: app.get('/health', (req, res) => { res.status(500).json({ status: 'unhealthy' }); }); git add app.js git commit -m "Break health check (testing quality gates)" git push origin main

    Watch GitHub Actions fail at the test stage. Image is never built or pushed. Fix the code and push again to see it succeed.

    Review Security Scan Results

    After successful run, check Actions logs. Look for Trivy output showing scanned vulnerabilities. If using node:20-alpine, you should see minimal vulnerabilities (Alpine is minimal and secure).

    Lab 3: Automated Deployment to Staging Environment

    Objective: Add automated deployment to a staging environment. Use GitHub Environments for configuration management and deployment tracking.

    Navigate to your lab working directory:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course/week-04-container-cicd/lab-03-automated-deployment

    Every file you create in this lab goes into this directory. Stay here for all remaining steps unless a step explicitly tells you to change directory.

    Setup Staging Environment in GitHub

    Go to Repository Settings → Environments → New environment. Name it staging. Add environment URL: http://YOUR_SERVER:3001 (or cloud URL if available).

    Create Deployment Workflow

    Create .github/workflows/deploy-staging.yml:

    name: Deploy to Staging on: push: branches: [ main ] env: REGISTRY: ghcr.io IMAGE_NAME: ${{ github.repository }} jobs: deploy: runs-on: ubuntu-latest environment: name: staging url: http://YOUR_SERVER:3001 steps: - name: Set up deployment context run: | echo "Deploying to staging environment" echo "Image: ${{ env.REGISTRY }}/${{ env.IMAGE_NAME }}:main" - name: Deploy container (simulation) run: | echo "SSH into server and run:" echo "docker pull ${{ env.REGISTRY }}/${{ env.IMAGE_NAME }}:main" echo "docker stop cicd-staging || true" echo "docker rm cicd-staging || true" echo "docker run -d -p 3001:3000 --name cicd-staging \\" echo " --restart unless-stopped \\" echo " ${{ env.REGISTRY }}/${{ env.IMAGE_NAME }}:main" # If you have actual server access, use this instead: # - name: Deploy to server via SSH # uses: appleboy/ssh-action@v1.0.0 # with: # host: ${{ secrets.STAGING_HOST }} # username: ${{ secrets.STAGING_USER }} # key: ${{ secrets.STAGING_SSH_KEY }} # script: | # docker pull ${{ env.REGISTRY }}/${{ env.IMAGE_NAME }}:main # docker stop cicd-staging || true # docker rm cicd-staging || true # docker run -d -p 3001:3000 --name cicd-staging \ # --restart unless-stopped \ # ${{ env.REGISTRY }}/${{ env.IMAGE_NAME }}:main - name: Wait for application startup run: sleep 10 - name: Smoke test staging deployment run: | # If you have actual server: # response=$(curl -s -o /dev/null -w "%{http_code}" http://YOUR_SERVER:3001/health) # if [ "$response" != "200" ]; then # echo "Smoke test failed: HTTP $response" # exit 1 # fi echo "Smoke test passed (simulated)" - name: Notify team run: | echo "Staging deployment completed successfully" echo "URL: http://YOUR_SERVER:3001"
    Test Staging Deployment

    Make a change and push:

    # Update version in app.js version: '1.2.0' git add app.js git commit -m "Release v1.2.0 to staging" git push origin main

    Watch both workflows run: build pipeline completes first, then deployment triggers automatically.

    View Deployment History

    Go to Repository → Environments → staging. You'll see deployment history with timestamps, actors, and status. Click any deployment to see full logs.

    Lab 4: Production Deployment with Manual Approval

    Objective: Implement production deployment requiring manual approval. Use GitHub's environment protection rules to enforce review before production changes.

    Navigate to your lab working directory:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course/week-04-container-cicd/lab-04-production-deployment

    Every file you create in this lab goes into this directory. Stay here for all remaining steps unless a step explicitly tells you to change directory.

    Create Production Environment with Protection

    Go to Repository Settings → Environments → New environment. Name: production.

    Enable protection rules:

    • Required reviewers: Add yourself (and teammates if applicable)
    • Wait timer: 5 minutes (gives time to catch mistakes)
    • Deployment branches: Select "Protected branches" or specify tags pattern v*.*.*
    Create Production Deployment Workflow

    Create .github/workflows/deploy-production.yml:

    name: Deploy to Production on: push: tags: - 'v*.*.*' workflow_dispatch: env: REGISTRY: ghcr.io IMAGE_NAME: ${{ github.repository }} jobs: deploy: runs-on: ubuntu-latest environment: name: production url: https://YOUR_DOMAIN.com steps: - uses: actions/checkout@v4 - name: Extract version from tag id: version run: | VERSION=${GITHUB_REF#refs/tags/} echo "version=$VERSION" >> $GITHUB_OUTPUT echo "Deploying version: $VERSION" - name: Deploy to production run: | echo "Blue-Green Deployment Strategy:" echo "1. Deploy to inactive slot (green)" echo "2. Run smoke tests on green" echo "3. Switch traffic to green" echo "4. Keep blue as rollback target" echo "" echo "docker pull ${{ env.REGISTRY }}/${{ env.IMAGE_NAME }}:${{ steps.version.outputs.version }}" - name: Smoke tests run: | echo "Running production smoke tests..." sleep 5 echo "✓ Health check passed" echo "✓ API version check passed" echo "✓ Database connectivity verified" - name: Rollback procedure (if needed) if: failure() run: | echo "ROLLBACK TRIGGERED" echo "Switching traffic back to blue environment" echo "Incident requires investigation" - name: Success notification if: success() run: | echo "🚀 Production deployment successful!" echo "Version: ${{ steps.version.outputs.version }}" echo "Deployed at: $(date)"
    Create Production Release

    Tag a release and push:

    git tag -a v1.0.0 -m "Release v1.0.0 - First production release" git push origin v1.0.0
    Approve Deployment

    Go to GitHub → Actions → Select the running workflow. You'll see a yellow "Review deployments" button. Click it, review the deployment details, and click "Approve and deploy". The workflow will wait for your approval (plus the 5-minute wait timer) before proceeding.

    Verify Deployment Audit Trail

    After deployment completes, go to Environments → production. You'll see complete audit trail: who approved, when, deployment duration, success/failure status. This is critical for compliance and incident investigation.

    Lab 5: Complete Pipeline with Rollback Capability

    Objective: Build a complete production-grade pipeline with automated rollback on deployment failure. Implement health checks and automated recovery.

    Navigate to your lab working directory:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course/week-04-container-cicd/lab-05-complete-pipeline

    Every file you create in this lab goes into this directory. Stay here for all remaining steps unless a step explicitly tells you to change directory.

    Add Health Check Endpoint

    Enhance app.js with detailed health check:

    const startTime = Date.now(); app.get('/health', (req, res) => { const uptime = Math.floor((Date.now() - startTime) / 1000); res.json({ status: 'healthy', uptime: uptime, timestamp: new Date().toISOString(), version: '1.0.0' }); }); app.get('/health/ready', (req, res) => { // Check if app is ready to serve traffic // In real app: check database connection, cache, etc. res.json({ ready: true }); }); app.get('/health/live', (req, res) => { // Liveness check - is process alive? res.json({ alive: true }); });
    Create docker-compose for local deployment

    Create docker-compose.prod.yml:

    version: '3.8' services: app-blue: image: ghcr.io/YOUR_USERNAME/cicd-demo:${BLUE_VERSION} container_name: app-blue ports: - "3000:3000" restart: unless-stopped healthcheck: test: ["CMD", "wget", "--quiet", "--tries=1", "--spider", "http://localhost:3000/health"] interval: 10s timeout: 3s retries: 3 app-green: image: ghcr.io/YOUR_USERNAME/cicd-demo:${GREEN_VERSION} container_name: app-green ports: - "3001:3000" restart: unless-stopped healthcheck: test: ["CMD", "wget", "--quiet", "--tries=1", "--spider", "http://localhost:3000/health"] interval: 10s timeout: 3s retries: 3
    Create Rollback Workflow

    Create .github/workflows/rollback.yml:

    name: Rollback Production on: workflow_dispatch: inputs: target-version: description: 'Version to rollback to' required: true type: string jobs: rollback: runs-on: ubuntu-latest environment: name: production url: https://YOUR_DOMAIN.com steps: - name: Confirm rollback run: | echo "⚠️ ROLLBACK INITIATED" echo "Rolling back to version: ${{ inputs.target-version }}" echo "Current time: $(date)" - name: Deploy previous version run: | echo "Switching traffic to previous version" echo "docker-compose up -d app-blue" echo "export BLUE_VERSION=${{ inputs.target-version }}" - name: Verify rollback run: | sleep 10 echo "Checking health of rolled-back version..." # curl -f http://YOUR_DOMAIN/health || exit 1 echo "✓ Rollback successful" - name: Notify team run: | echo "🔄 ROLLBACK COMPLETED" echo "Restored version: ${{ inputs.target-version }}" echo "Incident requires root cause analysis"
    Test Complete Pipeline

    Test the entire flow:

    # 1. Make changes echo "Testing complete pipeline" >> README.md git add README.md git commit -m "Test complete CI/CD pipeline" # 2. Create release git tag -a v1.0.1 -m "Release v1.0.1" git push origin main git push origin v1.0.1 # 3. Watch pipeline execute: # - Build and test # - Security scan # - Wait for approval # - Deploy to production # - Smoke tests # - Success notification
    Test Rollback Procedure

    Go to GitHub Actions → Rollback Production workflow → Run workflow. Enter previous version (v1.0.0) and click "Run". Watch rollback execute and verify in environment history.

    Document Your Pipeline

    Create comprehensive README documenting:

    • Pipeline architecture diagram
    • Deployment process (how to release)
    • Rollback procedure (emergency instructions)
    • Environment URLs and access
    • Troubleshooting common issues

    Weekly Test: Applied CI/CD Scenarios

    Instructions: This test presents realistic scenarios requiring applied problem-solving. Provide detailed answers explaining your reasoning. This test evaluates your ability to design, troubleshoot, and optimize CI/CD pipelines. Time limit: 60 minutes. Submit as document or text response.

    • Scenario 1: Pipeline Failure Troubleshooting

      Your GitHub Actions workflow fails at the "Build Docker Image" step with the error: "failed to solve with frontend dockerfile.v0: failed to read dockerfile". The Dockerfile exists in the repository root and builds successfully locally with `docker build`. What are three potential causes and how would you diagnose each?

    • Scenario 2: Security Gate Implementation

      You're implementing a security scanning gate in your CI/CD pipeline. The Trivy scan must fail the pipeline if CRITICAL vulnerabilities are found, but allow MEDIUM and LOW severity issues to pass. Describe: (1) How to configure the Trivy action, (2) How to handle the "ignore-unfixed" scenario where no patch is available, and (3) How to decide between fixing vulnerabilities vs. accepting risk.

    • Scenario 3: Deployment Strategy Design

      Your company is deploying a payment processing service (extremely high availability requirement, 99.99% SLA). The previous rolling deployment caused a 15-minute outage when a configuration bug was introduced. Your team is considering: (A) Blue-Green Deployment or (B) Canary Deployment. Compare both approaches for this use case and recommend one with justification.

    • Scenario 4: Rollback Execution

      You deployed v1.0.5 to production at 2:00 PM. At 2:15 PM, your monitoring team alerts: database query latency increased 10x, application error rate went from 0.1% to 5%. The database team identified that a query introduced in v1.0.5 is causing table locks. Your production environment uses blue-green deployment with Nginx load balancer. You have v1.0.4 still running in blue slot. Describe your step-by-step rollback procedure, including timing, verification steps, and post-incident actions.

    • Scenario 5: Pipeline Optimization Challenge

      Your team's GitHub Actions pipeline currently takes 18 minutes from push to deployment: checkout (1 min), build Docker image (12 min), run tests (3 min), push image (2 min). Your team deploys ~50 times per day. You estimate each minute of pipeline time costs $0.10 in GitHub Actions minutes. Propose three optimizations and calculate ROI for each.

    Weekly Assignment: End-to-End CI/CD Pipeline for E-Commerce Application

    Project Folder Source of Truth (Week 04)

    Put the final weekly assignment deliverables inside ~/devops-engineering-course/week-04-container-cicd/weekly-project/. This folder is the grading source of truth for Google Classroom and mentorship review.

    • Main submission document: create or update week-04-container-cicd/weekly-project/README.md as the assignment brief, summary, and runbook.
    • Supporting artefacts: keep screenshots, diagrams, exported evidence, configs, and code samples in clearly named files under week-04-container-cicd/weekly-project/.
    • Lab handoff rule: if the weekly project builds on a lab, copy or reference the relevant files from the lab folder into weekly-project/ rather than leaving your final submission scattered across lab directories.
    • Submission path: when you submit to Google Classroom, include the repository URL and the exact path week-04-container-cicd/weekly-project/.
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course/week-04-container-cicd/weekly-project git add . git commit -m "feat(week-04-container-cicd): submit week 04 weekly project artefacts" git push

    Objective: Design and implement a production-grade CI/CD pipeline for an e-commerce microservice. This assignment integrates all Week 4 concepts: GitHub Actions, Docker image building, automated testing, security scanning, multi-environment deployment, and rollback capability.

    📋 Assignment Overview

    Scenario: You're the DevOps engineer for a growing e-commerce startup. The product team wants to deploy their "Product Catalog Service" (Node.js backend) to production with confidence. Your task: Build a complete CI/CD pipeline that prevents bugs, catches security issues, and enables fast, safe deployments.

    Part 1: GitHub Actions Workflow Design (40 points)

    Create a `.github/workflows/ci-cd-pipeline.yml` workflow with the following stages:

    • Trigger: On push to main branch and on pull requests
    • Build Stage: Checkout, build Docker image using node:18-alpine, tag with git SHA and "latest"
    • Test Stage: Run npm test in parallel with build (don't wait for image push)
    • Security Stage: Scan image with Trivy, fail if CRITICAL vulnerabilities found
    • Push Stage: Push image to ghcr.io with proper authentication and caching
    • Deploy Staging: Automatic deployment to staging environment (main branch only)
    • Deploy Production: Manual approval required, can only deploy from Git tags (v*.*.*))

    Requirements:

    • ✓ Use docker/build-push-action@v5 with BuildKit and caching
    • ✓ Add GitHub Environments with protection rules (Production env requires 1 approval + 5 min wait)
    • ✓ Handle secrets securely (GITHUB_TOKEN, database connection strings)
    • ✓ Output: Provide complete workflow YAML file and explanation of each section

    Part 2: Dockerfile Optimization (20 points)

    Create an optimized multi-stage Dockerfile for the Node.js e-commerce service:

    • Stage 1 (Build): Use node:18 base, copy package files, install dependencies
    • Stage 2 (Test): Copy source code, run tests
    • Stage 3 (Production): Use node:18-alpine, copy built artifacts from build stage, minimize final image size
    • Security: Run container as non-root user (node user, UID 1000)
    • Best Practices: Include health check, appropriate node environment, .dockerignore optimization

    Requirements:

    • ✓ Final image size <200MB< /li>
    • ✓ Health check endpoint: GET /api/health → JSON response with status
    • ✓ Output: Provide Dockerfile, .dockerignore, and size comparison (build vs. production image)

    Part 3: Deployment Strategy & Rollback Plan (20 points)

    Document your production deployment and rollback strategy:

    • Choose deployment strategy: Blue-Green or Canary? Justify your choice for an e-commerce product catalog service.
    • Environment setup: Describe staging (auto-deploy) and production (manual approval + automatic canary/blue-green)
    • Verification steps: What must pass before promoting from staging to production?
    • Rollback procedure: Step-by-step rollback from v1.0.5 to v1.0.4 in production
    • Monitoring & alerting: What metrics trigger automatic rollback?

    Requirements:

    • ✓ Output: Document (2-3 pages) with deployment diagram, rollback procedures, monitoring thresholds
    • ✓ Include specific metrics: error rate threshold, latency threshold, traffic split strategy if canary

    Part 4: Implementation & Testing (20 points)

    Implement and test your pipeline:

    • GitHub Actions Validation: Push workflow to GitHub; verify it triggers correctly on push and PR
    • Local Testing: Build Docker image locally with your Dockerfile; verify health check works
    • Security Scan: Run Trivy scan locally: `trivy image node:18 --severity CRITICAL` and document findings
    • Deployment Simulation: Use docker-compose or kind (Kubernetes in Docker) to simulate staging/production deployment
    • Rollback Test: Deploy v1.0.0, then "rollback" to previous version; document steps and timing

    Requirements:

    • ✓ Output: GitHub Actions workflow run screenshot (showing all stages passing)
    • ✓ Output: docker images output showing image size
    • ✓ Output: Trivy scan results summary
    • ✓ Output: Evidence of successful deployment and rollback test
    Criteria Excellent (4) Good (3) Satisfactory (2) Needs Improvement (1) Points
    GitHub Actions Workflow Complete multi-stage workflow with all stages, proper caching, security scanning, environment protection rules, comprehensive error handling Most stages implemented correctly, caching and security present but could be optimized, protection rules partially configured Basic workflow with build and deploy, missing caching or security scanning, minimal protection rules Incomplete workflow, missing critical stages, no security scanning or environment protection 40 pts
    Dockerfile Optimization Well-designed multi-stage build, final image <150MB, non-root user, health check implemented, .dockerignore optimized Multi-stage build present, image <200MB, mostly optimized, health check working Single or basic multi-stage, image <250MB, basic optimization, health check missing or incomplete Inefficient Dockerfile, large image size, missing optimization or security best practices 20 pts
    Deployment Strategy Clear strategy choice (blue-green/canary) with detailed justification, complete rollback procedures, specific metrics and thresholds, deployment diagram Strategy chosen and justified, rollback procedures mostly complete, metrics defined, diagram included Strategy identified, basic justification, rollback procedures outlined, minimal metrics Strategy unclear or unjustified, incomplete rollback procedures, no metrics defined 20 pts
    Implementation & Testing All components tested locally and on GitHub Actions, evidence provided (screenshots), successful deployment and rollback demonstrated Most components tested, some evidence provided, deployment successful, rollback tested Basic testing completed, minimal evidence, deployment works, rollback not fully tested Limited testing, insufficient evidence, deployment issues, rollback not tested 20 pts
    Documentation & Communication Excellent documentation with clear explanations, code comments, assumptions stated, deployment runbook complete Good documentation, mostly clear, some code comments, runbook adequate Adequate documentation, minimal comments, basic runbook Poor documentation, missing explanations, incomplete runbook Bonus +5
    📚 Submission & Evaluation

    Submission Format: Create a GitHub repository with:

    1. `.github/workflows/ci-cd-pipeline.yml` - Complete workflow file
    2. `Dockerfile` - Optimized multi-stage Dockerfile
    3. `.dockerignore` - Optimized ignore file
    4. `DEPLOYMENT_STRATEGY.md` - 2-3 page document describing deployment and rollback
    5. `TESTING_EVIDENCE.md` - Screenshots and documentation of testing results
    6. `README.md` - Overview of the pipeline and how to use it
    7. Sample Node.js app (provided template or your own)

    Grading Timeline: Submit by end of Week 4. Grading within 3 days. Feedback provided in Google Classroom.

    Evaluation Criteria: Does your pipeline successfully build, test, scan, and deploy a containerized application? Can you demonstrate rollback? Is the workflow optimized for speed and cost?

    💡 Assignment Success Tips
    1. Start with a real Node.js app: Don't over-complicate—simple Express server with /api/health endpoint is sufficient.

    2. Test locally first: Build Docker image locally with `docker build -t myapp .` before GitHub Actions.

    3. Use GitHub Actions cache from day one: Don't optimize later—enable caching immediately to see performance benefit.

    4. Document your decisions: Why did you choose blue-green over canary? What metrics trigger rollback? These explanations demonstrate deep understanding.

    5. Iterate on the workflow: Your workflow doesn't need to be perfect on day one. Push to GitHub, run it, fix errors, iterate. Learning through failure is the goal.

    6. Share your work: Show your workflow to peers, discuss deployment strategies, learn from each other's approaches.
    Mentorship Review for Week 4

    Mentorship students: your Week 4 work (Container CI/CD & Automation) receives expert feedback on:

    • Core concepts: accuracy and depth of the week's technical objectives
    • Implementation quality: correctness, reliability, and operational readiness
    • Security and risk: safeguards, least privilege, and failure handling
    • Architecture decisions: tradeoffs, scalability, and maintainability
    • Documentation: clarity, reproducibility, and deployment notes

    Submit via Google Classroom and schedule a review call for feedback and portfolio guidance.

    5

    Kubernetes Architecture & Core Concepts

    Mastering Container Orchestration, Control Plane Internals, and Declarative Infrastructure Management

    Welcome to the pivotal point in your DevOps journey. In the previous weeks, you mastered the art of packaging applications into isolated, portable containers using Docker. You learned how to build images, manage volumes, and network containers together on a single host. However, in enterprise environments, running containers on a single machine is rarely sufficient. Applications need to scale across dozens or hundreds of servers, heal themselves when crashes occur, and update seamlessly without downtime.

    This week, we introduce Kubernetes (K8s), the de facto standard for container orchestration. Kubernetes abstracts the underlying hardware, allowing you to treat a cluster of machines as a single, logical computer. We will peel back the layers of the Kubernetes architecture to understand how the Control Plane makes decisions and how Worker Nodes execute them.

    We will transition from the imperative mindset of Docker ("run this container now") to the declarative mindset of Kubernetes ("ensure this state exists"). By the end of this week, you will have a running local cluster, a deep understanding of Pods, Deployments, and Services, and the skills to deploy your first self-healing application.

    Mentorship Guidance

    Mentorship students receive structured support for Kubernetes Architecture & Core Concepts: live Q&A, assignment feedback, and office hours focused on this week's goals.

    Share blockers in Google Classroom and schedule a review session once your lab and assignment work are ready.

    Learning Objectives

    This curriculum is designed to move you beyond simple "hello world" examples. By the end of this week, you will be able to demonstrate mastery across all levels of Bloom's Taxonomy:

    Remember

    Define and Recall: Accurately identify the core components of the Kubernetes architecture (API Server, etcd, Scheduler, Controller Manager, Kubelet, Kube-proxy) and describe the specific responsibility of each within the cluster ecosystem.

    Understand

    Explain Concepts: Articulate the difference between the Control Plane (Master) and Worker Nodes. Explain the "Desired State" model and how the Kubernetes Reconciliation Loop continuously monitors and corrects cluster state.

    Apply

    Execute Skills: Install and configure a functional local Kubernetes cluster using Minikube or Kind. proficiently use the kubectl command-line tool to interact with the API server, retrieve cluster information, and manage basic resources.

    Analyze

    Diagnose Issues: Inspect Pod lifecycles and statuses (Pending, Running, CrashLoopBackOff). Debug failed deployments by analyzing events, describing resources, and viewing container logs to identify architectural bottlenecks.

    Evaluate

    Compare Approaches: Critically evaluate Imperative management (CLI commands) versus Declarative management (YAML manifests). Justify why declarative Infrastructure-as-Code (IaC) is the required standard for production environments.

    Create

    Build Solutions: Author syntactically correct and production-ready YAML manifests for Pods, Deployments, and Services. Construct a multi-component application deployment that demonstrates self-healing and load balancing capabilities.

    Pre-Class Exercises

    Mandatory Requirement: Complete these exercises before beginning the main teaching notes. Kubernetes introduces significant complexity and new vocabulary. These preparatory tasks ensure your local environment is ready and your mental model is primed for the architectural deep dive. Budget approximately 3-4 hours for this section.

    ⚙ Week 05 Workspace Setup — Complete This First

    Create your working directory for this week inside the course repository. Every pre-class note, lab file, screenshot, and project deliverable belongs under this folder tree — nothing goes anywhere else.

    1. Navigate to your course repository root:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course
    2. Create this week's folder and all sub-directories in one command:
    mkdir -p week-05-kubernetes-architecture/{preclass-notes,lab-01-cluster-init,lab-02-pods-declarative,lab-03-deployments-scaling,lab-04-services-networking,lab-05-debugging,weekly-project}

    This single command creates the whole week's workspace tree. You will stay within this tree for pre-class work, every lab, and the weekly project.

    3. Verify the structure was created correctly:
    find week-05-kubernetes-architecture -type d | sort

    You should see 7 directories (preclass-notes + 5 lab folders + weekly-project).

    4. Create your pre-class notes file:
    touch week-05-kubernetes-architecture/preclass-notes/week-05-notes.md

    All research, written answers, and reflections for this week's pre-class exercises go into this Markdown file. Keep it open as you work through the exercises below.

    5. Commit the empty scaffold to your GitHub portfolio:
    git add week-05-kubernetes-architecture/ git commit -m "chore(week-05-kubernetes-architecture): scaffold week 05 working directory" git push
