    Pushing the empty scaffold creates a timestamped entry on your GitHub profile showing when you started this week — visible evidence for your portfolio and mentorship reviews.

    📁 Week 05 Folder Structure
    ~/devops-engineering-course/ └── week-05-kubernetes-architecture/ ├── preclass-notes/ ├── lab-01-cluster-init/ ├── lab-02-pods-declarative/ ├── lab-03-deployments-scaling/ ├── lab-04-services-networking/ ├── lab-05-debugging/ └── weekly-project/

    1The "Why" of Orchestration

    Before learning how to use Kubernetes, you must understand why it exists. Research the concept of "Container Orchestration" and write a structured 300-word summary addressing the following scenarios:

    • High Availability: What happens to a standard Docker container if the physical server it is running on catches fire? How does an orchestrator mitigate this?
    • Scaling: If traffic to your application spikes at 2 AM, how would you manually scale up using only Docker? How does an orchestrator automate this?
    • Service Discovery: In a cluster of 50 servers, how does Service A find Service B without hardcoding IP addresses?
    💡 Research Tip

    Look for resources discussing "Borg" (Google's internal system) and the history of Kubernetes. Understanding that K8s was born from 15 years of running production workloads at Google helps explain its design choices.

    2Environment Setup: Kubectl & Minikube/Kind

    You cannot learn Kubernetes theoretically; you need muscle memory. We will be using a local cluster for this course. You must install two primary tools: the command-line client and a local cluster provisioner.

    Part A: Install the Kubernetes Client (kubectl)

    Install kubectl, the CLI tool used to communicate with the Kubernetes API Server.

    # Verify installation after following official docs for your OS kubectl version --client --output=yaml

    Part B: Install a Local Cluster Tool

    Choose one of the following tools to run Kubernetes on your laptop. Minikube is recommended for beginners due to its robust dashboard and addons. Kind (Kubernetes in Docker) is excellent if you are already comfortable with Docker.

    # Option 1: Minikube (Recommended) # Visit minikube.sigs.k8s.io for installation minikube start --driver=docker # Option 2: Kind # Visit kind.sigs.k8s.io kind create cluster --name devops-core
    ⚠️ Hardware Requirements

    Running a Kubernetes cluster—even a local one—is resource-intensive. Ensure your machine has at least 2 CPUs and 4GB of free RAM available for the virtual machine or Docker containers. If your machine is older, look into K3d, a highly lightweight wrapper for Rancher's K3s distribution.

    3YAML Syntax Bootcamp

    Kubernetes configuration is written almost exclusively in YAML. It is a whitespace-sensitive language. If your indentation is off by a single space, your deployment will fail, and the error messages can be cryptic.

    Task: Create a file named practice.yaml. Write a YAML structure describing a "ServerInventory" object with the following nested fields:

    • hostname: "web-01"
    • specs: (nested object with cpu: "4 cores", ram: "16GB")
    • tags: (list of strings: "production", "web", "frontend")
    • owner: (nested object with name: "DevOps Team", email: "admin@hexadigitall.com")

    Use an online YAML validator (like yamllint.com) or a VS Code extension to verify your syntax is valid.

    💡 Tool Recommendation

    Install the Red Hat YAML and Kubernetes extensions for VS Code immediately. They provide real-time syntax validation, auto-completion, and schema validation that will save you hours of troubleshooting later.

    4Architecture Diagram Sketch

    Visualizing the cluster is key to debugging. Search for "Kubernetes Architecture Diagram" (look for the official CNCF version).

    Task: Sketch the diagram by hand or use a tool like Excalidraw. You must include and label the relationships between:

    • User (using kubectl)
    • Control Plane Components: API Server, etcd, Scheduler, Controller Manager
    • Worker Node Components: Kubelet, Kube-proxy, Container Runtime (Docker/containerd)
    • Pod (running inside the node)

    Keep this sketch on your desk. We will reference the data flow between these components constantly during the Teaching Notes.

    5Cluster Verification

    Verify that your local cluster is healthy and ready for the week's labs. Run the following commands and screenshot the output.

    # Check cluster information kubectl cluster-info # List all nodes in your cluster (should see 'minikube' or 'kind-control-plane') kubectl get nodes # List all pods in all namespaces (to see system components running) kubectl get pods --all-namespaces

    Success Criteria: You should see a list of system pods (like coredns, etcd, kube-apiserver) with a STATUS of "Running". If any are "Pending" or "CrashLoopBackOff", troubleshoot your installation before proceeding.

    Detailed Teaching Notes

    Kubernetes (K8s) is not just a tool; it is a philosophy of infrastructure management. It shifts the responsibility of "keeping things running" from the human operator to the software controller. To master Kubernetes, you must understand the "Brain" (Control Plane) that makes decisions and the "Muscle" (Worker Nodes) that executes them.

    5.1 The "Single Computer" Abstraction

    In traditional IT, you name servers (e.g., `web-server-01`, `db-server-02`). You care about their individual health. In Kubernetes, you treat the entire cluster as a single logical computer. You submit a workload to the API, and Kubernetes figures out which specific CPU cycles and RAM sticks will execute it.

    🧠 Control Plane vs. Data Plane

    Control Plane (The Brain): The collection of processes that control the cluster state. It makes global decisions (scheduling), detects and responds to cluster events (starting up a new pod), and stores the cluster configuration.

    Data Plane (The Muscle): The Worker Nodes where your actual application workloads (Pods) run. If the Control Plane goes down, your apps might keep running, but you can't change anything or heal failures.

    5.2 Deep Dive: Control Plane Components

    The Control Plane is the "nerve center" of the cluster. In a production environment, these components are distributed across multiple machines for high availability.

    1. The API Server (kube-apiserver)

    The Frontend of the Kubernetes control plane. It exposes the Kubernetes API (REST).

    • Gatekeeper: Every command you type (`kubectl get pods`) goes here.
    • Authentication & Authorization: Verifies who you are and what you can do.
    • Hub: It is the only component that communicates directly with the database (etcd). All other components (Scheduler, Kubelet) must talk to the API Server to read or write state.

    2. etcd (The Database)

    A consistent and highly-available Key-Value Store used as Kubernetes' backing store for all cluster data.

    • Source of Truth: If you delete the API server, you can restart it. If you lose the etcd data, you lose the cluster state entirely.
    • State Storage: It stores config, secrets, and the status of every object in the cluster.
    💡 Production Tip

    In production, always have a robust backup plan for etcd. It is the single most critical stateful component of the Control Plane.

    3. The Scheduler (kube-scheduler)

    The Decision Maker regarding placement.

    • Watch Loop: It watches for newly created Pods that have no Node assigned.
    • Filtering: "Which nodes have enough CPU/RAM? Which nodes match the required labels?"
    • Scoring: "Of the valid nodes, which is the best fit?" (e.g., spreading workload across zones).
    • Binding: Tells the API Server: "Run Pod A on Node B."

    4. Controller Manager (kube-controller-manager)

    The Reconciler. A collection of control loops packaged into a single binary.

    • Node Controller: Notices when nodes go down.
    • Replication Controller: Ensures the correct number of pods are running.
    • Job Controller: Watches for one-off tasks.
    • Goal: Continuously compares Current State to Desired State and takes action to fix discrepancies.

    5.3 Deep Dive: Worker Node Components

    While the Control Plane manages the cluster, the Worker Nodes do the heavy lifting. They host the Pods that run your applications.

    ⚙️ Kubelet: The Primary Node Agent

    The Kubelet is the "Captain" of the specific node. It runs on every worker node.
    1. It registers the node with the API Server.
    2. It receives instructions (PodSpecs) from the API Server.
    3. It commands the Container Runtime (Docker/containerd) to start/stop containers.
    4. It reports health status back to the Control Plane.

    Kube-proxy: The network proxy running on each node. It maintains network rules (using iptables or IPVS) on the host to allow network communication to your Pods from inside or outside the cluster. It is the core implementation of the Kubernetes "Service" concept.

    Container Runtime: The software responsible for running containers (e.g., containerd, CRI-O, Docker Engine). Kubernetes orchestrates the runtime; the runtime performs the low-level execution (pulling images, starting namespaces).

    5.4 The Atomic Unit: The Pod

    In Docker, the atomic unit is a container. In Kubernetes, the atomic unit is a Pod. Kubernetes does not run containers directly; it runs Pods, which wrap containers.

    What is a Pod?

    A Pod represents a single instance of a running process in your cluster. It encapsulates:

    • One or more containers: Usually just one, but can be multiple tightly-coupled containers (e.g., a main app and a logging sidecar).
    • Storage Resources: Shared volumes accessible by all containers in the Pod.
    • A unique network IP: The Pod gets an IP address. Containers inside the Pod share this IP and communicate via localhost.
    💡 The "Pea in a Pod" Analogy

    Think of a Pod like a "logical host" or a VM. The containers inside it are like processes running on that VM. They share the same filesystem namespaces (if configured), the same IP address, and the same port range.

    5.5 Imperative vs. Declarative Management

    This is the most significant mental shift for engineers moving from sysadmin scripting to DevOps engineering.

    The Paradigm Shift

    Imperative (The "How")

    "First, pull the image. Then, start the container. Then, check if it's running. If not, restart it."

    Commands: kubectl run, kubectl scale, kubectl edit

    Risk: Hard to reproduce. If the server crashes, your manual commands are lost.

    Declarative (The "What")

    "Here is a file. It says I want 3 copies of Nginx running. Make it happen. I don't care how."

    Commands: kubectl apply -f config.yaml

    Benefit: The file is the source of truth. Version control friendly. Self-healing.

    5.6 Workloads: Deployments & ReplicaSets

    While you can create a single Pod manually, you almost never should in production. Pods are ephemeral; if they die, they stay dead. To achieve self-healing, we use higher-level controllers.

    The Hierarchy:

    Deployment └── manages --> ReplicaSet └── manages --> Pods
    • Deployment: Defines the desired state (e.g., "I want 3 replicas of Nginx v1.14"). Handles rolling updates (upgrading to v1.15 without downtime) and rollbacks.
    • ReplicaSet: The mathematical enforcer. It runs a loop: if (current_pods < desired_pods) { start_pod() } else if (current_pods > desired_pods) { kill_pod() }.

    Anatomy of a Deployment YAML:

    apiVersion: apps/v1 # API version kind: Deployment # Resource type metadata: name: web-server # Name of the deployment spec: replicas: 3 # Desired number of pods selector: matchLabels: # How to find the pods to manage app: nginx template: # The blueprint for the Pods metadata: labels: app: nginx spec: containers: - name: nginx image: nginx:1.21 ports: - containerPort: 80

    5.7 Services: Solving the Networking Puzzle

    Pods are mortal. When a deployment updates, old pods die (IP 10.1.1.5) and new pods are born (IP 10.1.1.9). If your frontend talks directly to IP 10.1.1.5, the application breaks when that pod dies.

    A Service is a stable networking abstraction. It provides a static virtual IP address (ClusterIP) and a DNS name that routes traffic to a dynamic set of Pods.

    🔌 Service Types
    • ClusterIP (Default): Exposes the Service on a cluster-internal IP. Reachable only from within the cluster. Use this for databases or internal APIs.
    • NodePort: Exposes the Service on each Node's IP at a static port (range 30000-32767). Allows external access via NodeIP:NodePort. Good for local testing.
    • LoadBalancer: Provisions an external Load Balancer (like AWS ELB) from the cloud provider. The standard way to expose apps to the internet in the cloud.

    Hands-On Labs

    The theory of Kubernetes is vast, but the only way to truly understand it is by getting your hands dirty with kubectl. In these five labs, you will build a local cluster, deploy applications using both imperative and declarative methods, expose them to the network, and learn the art of debugging broken workloads.

    ⚠️ Prerequisites

    Ensure you have Docker Desktop running and kubectl installed. You will also need Minikube or Kind installed from the Pre-Class Exercises.

    Lab 1: Cluster Initialization & The Kubectl Interface

    Objective: Initialize a local Kubernetes cluster, verify control plane health, and master the syntax of the kubectl command-line tool.

    Learning Goals:

    • Start a single-node cluster using Minikube or Kind
    • Interact with the API Server using kubectl
    • Inspect cluster nodes and system namespaces
    Navigate to your lab working directory:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course/week-05-kubernetes-architecture/lab-01-cluster-init

    Every file you create in this lab goes into this directory. Stay here for all remaining steps unless a step explicitly tells you to change directory.

    Start your local cluster:

    Choose the command matching your installed tool:

    # Option A: Minikube minikube start --driver=docker # Option B: Kind kind create cluster --name k8s-lab
    Verify cluster connection (Context):

    kubectl relies on a config file (usually at ~/.kube/config) to know which cluster to talk to. This is called the "context".

    # Check current context kubectl config current-context # Verify cluster info kubectl cluster-info
    Kubernetes control plane is running at https://127.0.0.1:YOUR_PORT CoreDNS is running at https://127.0.0.1:YOUR_PORT/api/v1/namespaces/kube-system...
    Inspect the Nodes:

    In a local setup, your "node" acts as both Control Plane and Worker.

    kubectl get nodes -o wide

    Observe: Look at the ROLES column. It likely says control-plane. In production, you would see multiple nodes listed here.

    Explore Namespaces:

    Kubernetes uses namespaces to isolate resources. Let's see what's already running.

    # List all namespaces kubectl get namespaces # List system pods running in the kube-system namespace kubectl get pods -n kube-system
    💡 System Pods

    You will see pods like coredns (networking), etcd (database), and kube-apiserver. These are the Control Plane components running as pods themselves! This is called "static pods" or "self-hosting".

    Lab 2: The Pod - Imperative vs. Declarative

    Objective: Deploy your first application container (Nginx) using imperative CLI commands, then transition to the industry-standard declarative YAML manifest approach.

    Learning Goals:

    • Create pods using kubectl run
    • Generate YAML manifests using --dry-run
    • Apply configurations using kubectl apply
    Navigate to your lab working directory:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course/week-05-kubernetes-architecture/lab-02-pods-declarative

    Every file you create in this lab goes into this directory. Stay here for all remaining steps unless a step explicitly tells you to change directory.

    Imperative Creation (The "Quick & Dirty" Way):

    Tell Kubernetes exactly what to do via the CLI.

    # Run an Nginx pod named 'nginx-imperative' kubectl run nginx-imperative --image=nginx:alpine --port=80 # Check status kubectl get pods
    Declarative Creation (The "Production" Way):

    Instead of running it directly, we will generate a "manifest" file that describes the desired state.

    # Generate YAML without creating the object (--dry-run=client) kubectl run nginx-declarative \ --image=nginx:alpine \ --port=80 \ --dry-run=client \ -o yaml > nginx-pod.yaml
    Examine and Apply the Manifest:

    Open nginx-pod.yaml in your editor. It should look like this:

    apiVersion: v1 kind: Pod metadata: labels: run: nginx-declarative name: nginx-declarative spec: containers: - image: nginx:alpine name: nginx-declarative ports: - containerPort: 80

    Now, apply this state to the cluster:

    kubectl apply -f nginx-pod.yaml
    Verify and Port Forward:

    Pods have internal IPs not reachable from your laptop. Use port-forward to tunnel traffic.

    # Forward local port 8080 to the pod's port 80 # This command blocks the terminal (Ctrl+C to stop) kubectl port-forward nginx-declarative 8080:80

    Open your browser to http://localhost:8080. You should see "Welcome to nginx!".

    Cleanup:
    kubectl delete pod nginx-imperative kubectl delete -f nginx-pod.yaml

    Lab 3: Deployments, Scaling & Self-Healing

    Objective: Move beyond single Pods. Create a Deployment to manage a set of replicas, scale them up, and witness Kubernetes' self-healing capabilities in action.

    Navigate to your lab working directory:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course/week-05-kubernetes-architecture/lab-03-deployments-scaling

    Every file you create in this lab goes into this directory. Stay here for all remaining steps unless a step explicitly tells you to change directory.

    Create Deployment Manifest:

    Create a file named deployment.yaml.

    apiVersion: apps/v1 kind: Deployment metadata: name: web-app-deploy spec: replicas: 2 selector: matchLabels: app: web-app template: metadata: labels: app: web-app spec: containers: - name: nginx image: nginx:1.21 ports: - containerPort: 80
    Apply and Observe:
    kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml # Watch the pods being created kubectl get pods -l app=web-app -w

    You should see 2 pods created with random suffixes (e.g., web-app-deploy-5d4c7-xhz92).

    Test Self-Healing:

    Kubernetes guarantees the desired state. If we delete a pod manually, the Deployment Controller should notice the discrepancy (1 vs 2) and immediately start a new one.

    # List pods to get a name kubectl get pods # Delete one of them kubectl delete pod web-app-deploy-XXXXX-XXXXX # Immediately check status again kubectl get pods
    ✨ Magic Moment

    You will see the old pod "Terminating" and a brand new pod "ContainerCreating" or "Running". This is the Reconciliation Loop in action. You don't wake up at 3 AM to restart servers; Kubernetes does it for you.

    Scaling:

    Update the replicas: 2 to replicas: 5 in your YAML file, then re-apply.

    # Edit deployment.yaml, change replicas to 5 kubectl apply -f deployment.yaml # Verify 5 pods running kubectl get pods

    Lab 4: Services - Exposing Your Application

    Objective: Create a stable network endpoint for your dynamic Deployment using a Service. Understand the difference between ClusterIP and NodePort.

    Navigate to your lab working directory:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course/week-05-kubernetes-architecture/lab-04-services-networking

    Every file you create in this lab goes into this directory. Stay here for all remaining steps unless a step explicitly tells you to change directory.

    The Problem with Pod IPs:
    # Get IPs of your current pods kubectl get pods -o wide

    Note the IPs. If you delete these pods (as you did in Lab 3), the new pods get new IPs. You cannot configure a load balancer with changing IPs.

    Create a Service Manifest:

    Create service.yaml. We will use a NodePort type to make it accessible easily.

    apiVersion: v1 kind: Service metadata: name: web-service spec: type: NodePort selector: app: web-app # Must match Deployment labels! ports: - protocol: TCP port: 80 # Service port targetPort: 80 # Container port nodePort: 30007 # Static port on the node
    Apply and Inspect:
    kubectl apply -f service.yaml kubectl get svc
    NAME TYPE CLUSTER-IP EXTERNAL-IP PORT(S) AGE web-service NodePort 10.96.134.45 <none> 80:30007/TCP 5s
    Access the Application:

    The method depends on your cluster type:

    # If using Minikube (creates a tunnel for you) minikube service web-service # If using Kind/Docker Desktop # You access via localhost:30007 (if port mapped) or port-forward kubectl port-forward svc/web-service 8080:80

    Visiting the URL hits the Service, which load balances traffic across your 5 Pods. Refresh multiple times; you are hitting different containers (though Nginx default page looks the same).

    Lab 5: The Art of Debugging

    Objective: Deploy a deliberately broken application and use Kubernetes diagnostic tools to identify and fix the issue. This is the most valuable skill in this week's curriculum.

    Navigate to your lab working directory:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course/week-05-kubernetes-architecture/lab-05-debugging

    Every file you create in this lab goes into this directory. Stay here for all remaining steps unless a step explicitly tells you to change directory.

    Deploy a Broken Pod:

    Create broken.yaml with a typo in the image name.

    apiVersion: v1 kind: Pod metadata: name: broken-pod spec: containers: - name: nginx image: nginxxxx:latest # Typo!
    kubectl apply -f broken.yaml
    Phase 1: Identify the Status
    kubectl get pods
    NAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGE broken-pod 0/1 ImagePullBackOff 0 15s

    ErrImagePull or ImagePullBackOff indicates Kubernetes cannot retrieve the container image.

    Phase 2: Describe for Details

    The describe command is your X-ray machine. It shows Events.

    kubectl describe pod broken-pod

    Scroll to the bottom Events section. You should see:

    Warning Failed ... Failed to pull image "nginxxxx:latest": ... repository does not exist
    Phase 3: Check Logs (For App Errors)

    If the status was CrashLoopBackOff (image pulled but app crashed), you would use logs.

    kubectl logs broken-pod

    Note: In this specific case, logs will be empty because the container never started. But remember this command for app crashes!

    Phase 4: Fix It

    Edit broken.yaml to use nginx:latest and re-apply.

    kubectl apply -f broken.yaml # Watch it recover kubectl get pods -w
    🛠️ Troubleshooting Checklist
    • Pending? Check Scheduler / Node Resources (`kubectl describe node`).
    • ImagePullBackOff? Check image name, tag, or registry credentials.
    • CrashLoopBackOff? Check application logs (`kubectl logs`). App is starting but crashing.
    • Running but not working? Check Service selector labels matching Pod labels.
    6

    Kubernetes Workloads & Configuration

    Mastering Controllers, Configuration Management, and Application Lifecycle

    In Week 5, you learned the architecture of Kubernetes and deployed simple stateless pods. But real-world applications are diverse. Some need to run on every node (logging agents), some need stable identities (databases), and almost all need to read configuration data that differs between Dev and Prod environments.

    This week, we dive deep into the Workload APIs that manage your applications. We will move beyond the basic Deployment to explore StatefulSets for databases and DaemonSets for system agents. Crucially, we will decouple your configuration from your code using ConfigMaps and Secrets, adhering to the Twelve-Factor App methodology. By the end of this week, you will be able to deploy complex, stateful, and well-configured applications ready for any environment.

    Mentorship Guidance

    Mentorship students receive structured support for Kubernetes Workloads & Configuration: live Q&A, assignment feedback, and office hours focused on this week's goals.

    Share blockers in Google Classroom and schedule a review session once your lab and assignment work are ready.

    Learning Objectives

    By the end of this week, you will be able to demonstrate mastery across all levels of Bloom's Taxonomy:

    Remember

    Define Controllers: Differentiate between the use cases for Deployments, StatefulSets, DaemonSets, and Jobs/CronJobs. Recall the rules for Secret encoding (base64) vs. encryption.

    Understand

    Explain Behavior: Explain how a StatefulSet maintains stickiness (stable network ID and storage) compared to a Deployment. Describe how ConfigMaps are injected into pods as environment variables or volumes.

    Apply

    Execute Configuration: Inject configuration into applications using Environment Variables and Volume Mounts. Create Secrets to safely store credentials and mount them into pods.

    Analyze

    Diagnose Failures: Troubleshoot "CrashLoopBackOff" caused by missing ConfigMap keys. Analyze why a StatefulSet pod refuses to start until its predecessor is ready.

    Evaluate

    Assess Architecture: Critique an application architecture to decide if it requires a Deployment (stateless) or StatefulSet (stateful). Evaluate the security risks of storing Secrets in Git repositories.

    Create

    Build Systems: Author a multi-tier application manifest that uses a Deployment for the frontend, a StatefulSet for the database, and ConfigMaps/Secrets to connect them securely.

    Pre-Class Exercises

    Mandatory Requirement: Complete these exercises before beginning the main teaching notes. This week focuses on data injection and specialized controllers. Budget approximately 3 hours.

    ⚙ Week 06 Workspace Setup — Complete This First

    Create your working directory for this week inside the course repository. Every pre-class note, lab file, screenshot, and project deliverable belongs under this folder tree — nothing goes anywhere else.

    1. Navigate to your course repository root:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course
    2. Create this week's folder and all sub-directories in one command:
    mkdir -p week-06-kubernetes-workloads/{preclass-notes,lab-01-configmaps,lab-02-secrets,lab-03-rolling-deployments,lab-04-statefulsets,lab-05-cronjobs,weekly-project}

    This single command creates the whole week's workspace tree. You will stay within this tree for pre-class work, every lab, and the weekly project.

    3. Verify the structure was created correctly:
    find week-06-kubernetes-workloads -type d | sort

    You should see 7 directories (preclass-notes + 5 lab folders + weekly-project).

    4. Create your pre-class notes file:
    touch week-06-kubernetes-workloads/preclass-notes/week-06-notes.md

    All research, written answers, and reflections for this week's pre-class exercises go into this Markdown file. Keep it open as you work through the exercises below.

    5. Commit the empty scaffold to your GitHub portfolio:
    git add week-06-kubernetes-workloads/ git commit -m "chore(week-06-kubernetes-workloads): scaffold week 06 working directory" git push

    Pushing the empty scaffold creates a timestamped entry on your GitHub profile showing when you started this week — visible evidence for your portfolio and mentorship reviews.

    📁 Week 06 Folder Structure
    ~/devops-engineering-course/ └── week-06-kubernetes-workloads/ ├── preclass-notes/ ├── lab-01-configmaps/ ├── lab-02-secrets/ ├── lab-03-rolling-deployments/ ├── lab-04-statefulsets/ ├── lab-05-cronjobs/ └── weekly-project/

    1The "12-Factor App" Config Principle

    Kubernetes is designed around the "12-Factor App" methodology. Read the section on III. Config (Store config in the environment).

    Task: Create a simple Python or Node.js script that reads a database URL from an environment variable named DB_URL. If the variable is missing, the script should print an error and exit. Run this script locally by exporting the variable in your terminal first.

    # Example testing flow export DB_URL="postgres://localhost:5432/mydb" python3 my_script.py # Output: Connected to postgres://localhost:5432/mydb
    💡 Why this matters

    In Kubernetes, we never hardcode settings in the container image. We inject them at runtime using ConfigMaps. This exercise proves you understand how apps read from their environment.

    2Base64 Encoding vs. Encryption

    Kubernetes Secrets use Base64 encoding by default, which is not encryption. You must understand the difference.

    Task:
    1. Take a "secret" string (e.g., "mypassword").
    2. Encode it to base64 via command line.
    3. Decode it back.

    # Linux/Mac echo -n "mypassword" | base64 echo "bXlwYXNzd29yZA==" | base64 --decode # Windows (PowerShell) [Convert]::ToBase64String([Text.Encoding]::UTF8.GetBytes("mypassword"))
    ⚠️ Security Warning

    If you can decode it this easily, so can a hacker. Base64 is for transport serialization, not security. We will discuss Encryption at Rest in the teaching notes.

    3Stateful vs. Stateless Research

    Task: Write a 200-word comparison of "Pets vs. Cattle" in the context of servers.

    • Cattle (Stateless): If a web server dies, do you care which specific one it was? Or do you just replace it?
    • Pets (Stateful): If your primary database server dies, can you just spin up a blank new one and expect it to work immediately? Why does identity matter here?

    4Clean Up Week 5 Cluster

    We need a clean slate. If you have resources left over from Week 5 (like the "phoenix-prod" namespace), delete them to free up resources.

    kubectl delete namespace phoenix-prod kubectl delete deployment --all kubectl delete svc --all

    5Install "kubectx" and "kubens" (Optional but Recommended)

    As we start working with multiple namespaces (Dev/Prod), typing -n my-namespace on every command becomes tedious.

    Task: Install kubens (part of the kubectx package). It allows you to switch your active namespace permanently for your current session.

    # Mac brew install kubectx # Windows choco install kubens

    Detailed Teaching Notes

    In Week 5, we explored the architecture of the cluster itself. Now, we turn our attention to the applications running on that architecture. In Kubernetes terms, an application running on the cluster is called a Workload.

    Managing individual Pods manually is an anti-pattern. Pods are mortal; they die, they get evicted, and they do not resurrect themselves. To build resilient systems, we use Workload Controllers—higher-level abstractions that manage the lifecycle, scaling, and health of our Pods. Furthermore, to build portable applications, we must strictly separate our configuration (environment variables, config files) from our application code.

    6.1 The Hierarchy of Controllers

    Kubernetes offers different "shapes" of controllers depending on what your application needs. Choosing the right controller is the first architectural decision you make.

    Controller Decision Matrix

    Deployment

    Use for: Stateless Apps (Web Servers, APIs).
    Behavior: Pods are interchangeable. "Cattle".
    Network ID: Random (e.g., `web-7d5b...`).

    StatefulSet

    Use for: Databases, Queues (MySQL, Kafka).
    Behavior: Pods have unique identities. "Pets".
    Network ID: Stable (e.g., `db-0`, `db-1`).

    DaemonSet

    Use for: System Agents (Logs, Monitoring).
    Behavior: Runs exactly one pod per Node.
    Scaling: Scales with Node count.

    Job / CronJob

    Use for: Batch tasks, backups, reports.
    Behavior: Runs to completion, then exits.

    6.2 Stateless Applications: The Deployment

    The Deployment is the standard for 80-90% of workloads. It is designed for stateless applications where any pod can replace any other pod.

    🔄 Rolling Updates

    Deployments enable zero-downtime updates. When you update the image tag from `v1` to `v2`, the Deployment creates a new ReplicaSet. It spins up a `v2` pod, waits for it to be Ready, and then terminates a `v1` pod. It repeats this until all pods are replaced.

    # Deployment Example apiVersion: apps/v1 kind: Deployment metadata: name: nginx-deployment spec: replicas: 3 strategy: type: RollingUpdate rollingUpdate: maxSurge: 1 # Can create 1 extra pod during update (4 total) maxUnavailable: 0 # Must keep at least 3 running at all times selector: matchLabels: app: nginx template: # Pod Template starts here metadata: labels: app: nginx spec: containers: - name: nginx image: nginx:1.14.2

    6.3 Stateful Applications: The StatefulSet

    Databases are different. You cannot just kill the primary database server and replace it with a blank one. Order matters. Identity matters. Storage matters.

    The StatefulSet controller provides three guarantees that Deployments do not:

    • Stable Network Identity: Pods are named sequentially: `web-0`, `web-1`, `web-2`. These names persist across restarts. `web-0` will always be `web-0`.
    • Stable Storage: Each pod gets its own unique PersistentVolumeClaim (PVC). `web-0` gets `pvc-0`. If `web-0` dies and restarts on a different node, it re-attaches to `pvc-0`, ensuring data persistence.
    • Ordered Deployment and Scaling: It starts `web-0` first. It waits for it to be Ready before starting `web-1`. Scaling down is the reverse (`web-2` terminates first). This is crucial for master-replica architectures.
    ⚠️ When to use StatefulSets?

    Only use StatefulSets if your application requires unique identities or sticky storage (like Zookeeper, Kafka, MongoDB). If your app just needs to save files to a shared folder (like S3 or NFS), a standard Deployment is simpler and preferred.

    6.4 Specialized Workloads: DaemonSets & Jobs

    DaemonSets: Imagine you need a log collector (like Fluentd) running on every single server in your cluster. If you add a new node, you want this agent to start automatically. This is exactly what a DaemonSet does. It bypasses the scheduler's normal placement logic to ensure one-to-one node coverage.

    Jobs: Unlike other controllers that restart pods when they exit (assuming a crash), a Job creates pods that are expected to terminate successfully (exit code 0). Used for database migrations, batch processing, or backups.

    6.5 Configuration Management: ConfigMaps

    One of the core tenets of the Twelve-Factor App methodology is "Store config in the environment." You should never rebuild your Docker image just to change a database URL or a feature flag.

    A ConfigMap allows you to decouple configuration artifacts from image content. You can inject configuration data into Pods in two ways:

    Data Injection Strategies

    1. As Environment Variables

    Best for simple key-value pairs (API_URL, DEBUG_MODE). The application reads them via os.getenv() (Python) or process.env (Node).

    2. As Mounted Volumes

    Best for complete config files (nginx.conf, redis.conf). Kubernetes creates a file inside the container populated with the ConfigMap data. If you update the ConfigMap, the file inside the container updates automatically (though the app might need a reload to see it).

    # ConfigMap Example apiVersion: v1 kind: ConfigMap metadata: name: app-config data: # Key-value pair log_level: "debug" # File-like content game.properties: | enemies=aliens lives=3

    6.6 Handling Sensitive Data: Secrets

    Secrets are similar to ConfigMaps but are intended to hold sensitive information, such as passwords, OAuth tokens, and SSH keys.

    🔒 Secrets vs. ConfigMaps
    • Encoding: Secrets are stored as Base64-encoded strings in your YAML files (to handle binary data), whereas ConfigMaps store plain text. Note: Base64 is NOT encryption!
    • Handling: Kubernetes treats Secrets with extra care. They are stored in tmpfs (RAM) on the worker nodes, never written to disk, to prevent data leakage.
    • Encryption at Rest: In a production cluster, you must enable "Encryption at Rest" for etcd to ensure Secrets are encrypted specifically within the cluster database.
    # Secret Example apiVersion: v1 kind: Secret metadata: name: db-credentials type: Opaque data: # 'username' is base64 encoded "admin" username: YWRtaW4= # 'password' is base64 encoded "secret123" password: c2VjcmV0MTIz
    💡 Secret Management in Production

    Checking Secret manifests (even Base64 encoded) into Git is a security risk ("Secret Sprawl"). In advanced DevOps (Week 12/18), we use tools like Sealed Secrets, External Secrets Operator (connecting to AWS Secrets Manager/HashiCorp Vault), or SOPS to handle secrets securely in GitOps workflows.

    6.7 Architecture: Bringing It All Together

    A robust microservice architecture typically combines these elements:

    • A Deployment manages the stateless application logic (Frontend/API).
    • A StatefulSet manages the persistence layer (Database/Cache), ensuring stable storage.
    • ConfigMaps inject non-sensitive environment configuration (URLs, feature flags).
    • Secrets inject credentials (DB passwords, API keys).
    • A Service creates a stable network interface for the Deployment to talk to the StatefulSet.

    Hands-On Labs

    Theory is essential, but execution is what builds careers. In these five labs, you will move beyond simple stateless pods. You will configure applications dynamically, secure sensitive data, manage rolling updates for deployments, orchestrate stateful databases, and schedule batch jobs.

    ⚠️ Prerequisites

    Ensure your local cluster (Minikube/Kind) is running and kubectl is configured. Create a clean namespace for these labs to avoid clutter:
    kubectl create namespace week6
    kubens week6 (or kubectl config set-context --current --namespace=week6)

    Lab 1: Decoupling Config with ConfigMaps

    Objective: Deploy a Python application that reads configuration from the environment. You will inject this configuration using Kubernetes ConfigMaps instead of hardcoding it in the image.

    Navigate to your lab working directory:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course/week-06-kubernetes-workloads/lab-01-configmaps

    Every file you create in this lab goes into this directory. Stay here for all remaining steps unless a step explicitly tells you to change directory.

    Create the ConfigMap (Imperative):

    First, let's create a simple configuration object imperatively.

    kubectl create configmap app-settings \ --from-literal=APP_COLOR=blue \ --from-literal=APP_MODE=production \ --from-literal=MAX_RETRIES=5
    Inspect the ConfigMap:
    kubectl get configmap app-settings -o yaml

    Notice how the data is stored as plain text key-value pairs.

    Create a Pod Manifest consuming the ConfigMap:

    Create a file named config-pod.yaml. We will use a basic image that prints environment variables.

    apiVersion: v1 kind: Pod metadata: name: config-test-pod spec: containers: - name: test-container image: busybox command: [ "sh", "-c", "env | grep APP_ && sleep 3600" ] envFrom: - configMapRef: name: app-settings
    Apply and Verify:
    kubectl apply -f config-pod.yaml # Wait for it to start sleep 5 # Check logs to see injected variables kubectl logs config-test-pod
    APP_COLOR=blue APP_MODE=production
    Update Configuration (Hot Reload Simulation):
    # Edit the ConfigMap kubectl edit configmap app-settings # Change APP_COLOR to "green"

    Observation: Check the logs again. Did the value change?
    Spoiler: No. Environment variables are injected at startup. To pick up changes, you must restart the Pod. (We will see how Volumes solve this in Lab 4).

    Lab 2: Managing Sensitive Data with Secrets

    Objective: Store database credentials securely using Kubernetes Secrets and mount them into an application Pod securely.

    Navigate to your lab working directory:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course/week-06-kubernetes-workloads/lab-02-secrets

    Every file you create in this lab goes into this directory. Stay here for all remaining steps unless a step explicitly tells you to change directory.

    Create a Secret (Imperative):

    We will create a generic secret for database credentials.

    kubectl create secret generic db-creds \ --from-literal=username=admin \ --from-literal=password=SuperSecretPass123!
    Inspect the Secret (Decoding):

    Secrets are base64 encoded, not encrypted (by default in kubectl view).

    kubectl get secret db-creds -o yaml

    Copy the base64 string for password and decode it:

    echo "U3VwZXJTZWNyZXRQYXNzMTIzIQ==" | base64 --decode
    Create a Declarative Secret Manifest:

    Often you need to commit secrets (bad idea) or manage them via GitOps. Let's see the YAML format. Create secret.yaml:

    apiVersion: v1 kind: Secret metadata: name: api-key type: Opaque data: # "my-api-key" base64 encoded api-token: bXktYXBpLWtleQ==
    kubectl apply -f secret.yaml
    Mount Secret as a Volume:

    Applications often expect secrets as files (e.g., /etc/secrets/token). Create secret-vol-pod.yaml:

    apiVersion: v1 kind: Pod metadata: name: secret-test-pod spec: containers: - name: app image: nginx:alpine volumeMounts: - name: secret-volume mountPath: "/etc/secret-volume" readOnly: true volumes: - name: secret-volume secret: secretName: db-creds
    Verify Secret Injection:
    kubectl apply -f secret-vol-pod.yaml # Execute into the pod to see the file kubectl exec -it secret-test-pod -- ls /etc/secret-volume kubectl exec -it secret-test-pod -- cat /etc/secret-volume/password
    💡 Security Note

    Notice that inside the pod, the file contains the decoded plain text. Kubernetes handles the decoding automatically when mounting.

    Lab 3: Zero-Downtime Deployments

    Objective: Perform a rolling update of a web application from version 1 to version 2 without interrupting service, then perform a rollback.

    Navigate to your lab working directory:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course/week-06-kubernetes-workloads/lab-03-rolling-deployments

    Every file you create in this lab goes into this directory. Stay here for all remaining steps unless a step explicitly tells you to change directory.

    Create the Deployment (v1):

    Create nginx-deploy.yaml using the nginx 1.19 image.

    apiVersion: apps/v1 kind: Deployment metadata: name: nginx-prod spec: replicas: 3 selector: matchLabels: app: nginx-prod template: metadata: labels: app: nginx-prod spec: containers: - name: nginx image: nginx:1.19.0 ports: - containerPort: 80
    kubectl apply -f nginx-deploy.yaml kubectl get pods -l app=nginx-prod
    Perform Rolling Update:

    Update the image to 1.21.0. We will use kubectl set image for speed, though editing YAML is better for git history.

    # Open a second terminal window to watch the update kubectl get pods -l app=nginx-prod -w # In original window, trigger update kubectl set image deployment/nginx-prod nginx=nginx:1.21.0

    Watch the "Terminating" and "ContainerCreating" dance. The Deployment ensures minimal downtime.

    Verify Rollout Status:
    kubectl rollout status deployment/nginx-prod
    Introduce a Bug & Rollback:

    Let's break it by setting a non-existent image.

    kubectl set image deployment/nginx-prod nginx=nginx:999.999 # Check status - it will hang or error kubectl get pods

    You will see ImagePullBackOff or ErrImagePull. The old pods (v1.21) are still running! The deployment stopped the rollout because the new pods failed.

    Execute Rollback:
    # View history kubectl rollout history deployment/nginx-prod # Undo the last change kubectl rollout undo deployment/nginx-prod # Verify recovery kubectl get pods

    Lab 4: Running Stateful Workloads

    Objective: Deploy a MySQL database using a StatefulSet. Understand the difference in pod naming and ordered creation compared to Deployments.

    Navigate to your lab working directory:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course/week-06-kubernetes-workloads/lab-04-statefulsets

    Every file you create in this lab goes into this directory. Stay here for all remaining steps unless a step explicitly tells you to change directory.

    Create Headless Service:

    StatefulSets require a "Headless Service" (ClusterIP: None) to control the network identity of the pods. Create mysql-svc.yaml:

    apiVersion: v1 kind: Service metadata: name: mysql labels: app: mysql spec: ports: - port: 3306 name: mysql clusterIP: None selector: app: mysql
    Create StatefulSet Manifest:

    Create mysql-statefulset.yaml.

    apiVersion: apps/v1 kind: StatefulSet metadata: name: mysql spec: selector: matchLabels: app: mysql serviceName: "mysql" replicas: 2 template: metadata: labels: app: mysql spec: containers: - name: mysql image: mysql:5.7 env: - name: MYSQL_ROOT_PASSWORD value: "secretpassword" volumeMounts: - name: mysql-store mountPath: /var/lib/mysql volumeClaimTemplates: - metadata: name: mysql-store spec: accessModes: [ "ReadWriteOnce" ] resources: requests: storage: 1Gi
    Apply and Observe Ordered Creation:
    kubectl apply -f mysql-svc.yaml kubectl apply -f mysql-statefulset.yaml # Watch the ordered creation kubectl get pods -l app=mysql -w

    Unlike Deployments which start all pods seemingly at once, you will see mysql-0 start. Only after mysql-0 is Running will mysql-1 begin initializing. This strict ordering is critical for database replication setup.

    Verify Stable Network Identity:
    # Run a temporary pod to test DNS kubectl run -it --rm --image=busybox:1.28 dns-test --restart=Never -- nslookup mysql-0.mysql

    You should see a resolved IP. The DNS name mysql-0.mysql is stable. If you delete the pod, it comes back with the same name.

    Lab 5: Batch Processing with CronJobs

    Objective: Schedule a task that runs periodically (like a database backup or report generator) using Kubernetes CronJobs.

    Navigate to your lab working directory:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course/week-06-kubernetes-workloads/lab-05-cronjobs

    Every file you create in this lab goes into this directory. Stay here for all remaining steps unless a step explicitly tells you to change directory.

    Create CronJob Manifest:

    Create periodic-job.yaml that runs every minute.

    apiVersion: batch/v1 kind: CronJob metadata: name: hello-cron spec: schedule: "*/1 * * * *" # Run every minute jobTemplate: spec: template: spec: containers: - name: hello image: busybox imagePullPolicy: IfNotPresent command: - /bin/sh - -c - date; echo "Hello from Kubernetes CronJob" restartPolicy: OnFailure
    Apply and Wait:
    kubectl apply -f periodic-job.yaml # Wait 60-90 seconds for the first run kubectl get cronjobs kubectl get jobs
    Inspect the Results:

    CronJobs create Jobs, and Jobs create Pods. You need to find the pod to see the logs.

    # Find the pod created by the job kubectl get pods # Check logs of the completed pod kubectl logs hello-cron-xxxxx-xxxxx
    Cleanup:
    # Deleting the CronJob deletes all child Jobs and Pods kubectl delete cronjob hello-cron
    7

    Kubernetes Networking & Storage

    Connecting Applications and Persisting Data at Scale

    Welcome to Week 7. In Week 6, you deployed complex applications, but they lived in somewhat isolated bubbles. You used basic Services and Volumes, but likely treated them as "magic black boxes." This week, we tear open those boxes.

    Networking in Kubernetes is notoriously complex because it imposes a unique set of constraints: every pod must be able to reach every other pod without NAT. We will dissect the Pod Network, explore how CNI (Container Network Interface) plugins make this possible, and master Services and Ingress to route external traffic to your pods. We will also secure this traffic using Network Policies—the firewall of the cloud-native world.

    Storage is equally critical. Containers are ephemeral, but data must be eternal. We will move beyond simple Docker volumes to the robust Kubernetes Storage API: PersistentVolumes (PVs), PersistentVolumeClaims (PVCs), and StorageClasses. You will learn how to dynamic provisioning allows developers to request storage on-demand without waking up storage administrators.

    Mentorship Guidance

    Mentorship students receive structured support for Kubernetes Networking & Storage: live Q&A, assignment feedback, and office hours focused on this week's goals.

    Share blockers in Google Classroom and schedule a review session once your lab and assignment work are ready.

    Learning Objectives

    By the end of this week, you will be able to demonstrate mastery across all levels of Bloom's Taxonomy:

    Remember

    Define Concepts: Define the roles of CNI (networking) and CSI (storage) plugins. List the four types of Kubernetes Services (ClusterIP, NodePort, LoadBalancer, ExternalName) and the access modes for PersistentVolumes (RWO, RWX, ROX).

    Understand

    Explain Mechanics: Explain the Kubernetes "Flat Network" model (IP-per-Pod). Describe the binding process between a PVC and a PV, and how StorageClasses automate the creation of physical disks.

    Apply

    Execute Configuration: Implement an Ingress Controller to route HTTP/HTTPS traffic based on domains. Configure Dynamic Volume Provisioning to automatically create AWS EBS or local storage volumes for a database.

    Analyze

    Diagnose Issues: Troubleshoot CoreDNS failures (Service Discovery issues). Analyze "Pending" PVCs to identify StorageClass misconfigurations or capacity constraints.

    Evaluate

    Assess Strategies: Compare different storage backends (NFS vs. Block Storage) for specific workload requirements. Evaluate the security implications of open pod-to-pod communication versus restricting traffic with Network Policies.

    Create

    Build Architecture: Design a secured multi-tier application where the frontend communicates with the backend via Service Discovery, the database uses persistent storage, and Network Policies block unauthorized access.

    Pre-Class Exercises

    Mandatory Requirement: Complete these exercises before beginning the teaching notes. Networking and Storage rely heavily on the underlying infrastructure. These exercises verify that your local cluster supports these advanced features. Budget approximately 3-4 hours.

    ⚙ Week 07 Workspace Setup — Complete This First

    Create your working directory for this week inside the course repository. Every pre-class note, lab file, screenshot, and project deliverable belongs under this folder tree — nothing goes anywhere else.

    1. Navigate to your course repository root:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course
    2. Create this week's folder and all sub-directories in one command:
    mkdir -p week-07-kubernetes-networking/{preclass-notes,lab-01-dns-discovery,lab-02-ingress,lab-03-network-policies,lab-04-manual-storage,lab-05-dynamic-provisioning,weekly-project}

    This single command creates the whole week's workspace tree. You will stay within this tree for pre-class work, every lab, and the weekly project.

    3. Verify the structure was created correctly:
    find week-07-kubernetes-networking -type d | sort

    You should see 7 directories (preclass-notes + 5 lab folders + weekly-project).

    4. Create your pre-class notes file:
    touch week-07-kubernetes-networking/preclass-notes/week-07-notes.md

    All research, written answers, and reflections for this week's pre-class exercises go into this Markdown file. Keep it open as you work through the exercises below.

    5. Commit the empty scaffold to your GitHub portfolio:
    git add week-07-kubernetes-networking/ git commit -m "chore(week-07-kubernetes-networking): scaffold week 07 working directory" git push

    Pushing the empty scaffold creates a timestamped entry on your GitHub profile showing when you started this week — visible evidence for your portfolio and mentorship reviews.

    📁 Week 07 Folder Structure
    ~/devops-engineering-course/ └── week-07-kubernetes-networking/ ├── preclass-notes/ ├── lab-01-dns-discovery/ ├── lab-02-ingress/ ├── lab-03-network-policies/ ├── lab-04-manual-storage/ ├── lab-05-dynamic-provisioning/ └── weekly-project/

    1The "Flat Network" Research

    Kubernetes Networking is different from standard Docker networking.

    Task: Research the "Kubernetes Networking Model" and answer the following:

    • Does Kubernetes use NAT (Network Address Translation) when Pod A talks to Pod B on a different node? (Hint: No)
    • What is a CNI Plugin? Name three popular ones (e.g., Calico, Flannel, Cilium).
    • If you use Minikube or Kind, which CNI are you using by default?
    💡 Why this matters

    In Docker, you had to link containers or map ports explicitly. In Kubernetes, every pod gets its own IP address and can talk to any other pod by default. This "flat network" assumption simplifies app development but complicates infrastructure setup.

    2Prepare Network Debugging Tools

    Debugging networking issues usually requires tools like dig, nslookup, or curl, which are missing from slim production images.

    Task: Pull the nicolaka/netshoot image locally. This is the "Swiss Army Knife" of container networking. We will use it extensively in the labs.

    docker pull nicolaka/netshoot

    Test it: Run a temporary pod to explore your cluster's DNS.

    kubectl run tmp-shell --rm -i --tty --image nicolaka/netshoot -- /bin/bash # Inside the pod, try resolving the internal DNS service dig kube-dns.kube-system.svc.cluster.local

    3Storage Class Verification

    Storage behavior depends entirely on where your cluster is running (AWS vs. Laptop).

    Task: Check what StorageClasses are available in your local cluster.

    kubectl get storageclass
    NAME PROVISIONER RECLAIMPOLICY VOLUMEBINDINGMODE ALLOWVOLUMEEXPANSION AGE standard (default) k8s.io/minikube-hostpath Delete Immediate false 2d

    Analysis: If you see (default) next to a name, that's the class used when you don't specify one. On Minikube, it uses hostpath (folders on your laptop). On AWS, it would be gp2 (EBS volumes).

    4Enable Ingress Addon

    We will be doing advanced Ingress routing this week. Ensure your controller is ready.

    # If using Minikube minikube addons enable ingress # If using Kind kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/kubernetes/ingress-nginx/main/deploy/static/provider/kind/deploy.yaml

    Task: Locate the Ingress Controller pod.

    kubectl get pods -n ingress-nginx # Ensure status is 'Running'

    5Reset Your Environment

    Clean up any leftover resources from Week 6 (IronVault project) to prevent conflicts.

    kubectl delete namespace ironvault-prod kubectl delete namespace week6 kubectl delete pv --all kubectl delete pvc --all

    Detailed Teaching Notes

    If Kubernetes has a "final boss" for beginners, it is Networking. In Docker, you explicitly linked containers or mapped ports. In Kubernetes, networking is designed to be "flat," meaning every Pod can talk to every other Pod without Network Address Translation (NAT). This simplifies application logic (apps just talk to IPs) but complicates the infrastructure required to make that happen.

    Similarly, Storage in Kubernetes requires a mental shift from "writing to disk" to "claiming storage resources." We abstract the physical hard drive (EBS, SSD, NFS) into logical resources that developers can request without knowing the underlying hardware.

    7.1 The "Flat Network" Assumption

    Kubernetes imposes a specific set of networking requirements on any cluster implementation. This is often referred to as the "IP-per-Pod" model.

    🌐 The 3 Networking Rules
    1. Pod-to-Pod: All Pods can communicate with all other Pods without NAT. (If Pod A at 10.1.1.1 pings Pod B at 10.1.2.1, Pod B sees the source as 10.1.1.1).
    2. Node-to-Pod: All Nodes can communicate with all Pods (and vice-versa) without NAT.
    3. IP Identity: The IP that a Pod sees itself as is the same IP that others see it as.

    How is this achieved? CNI Plugins.
    Kubernetes itself does not implement the network. It offloads this to CNI (Container Network Interface) plugins. Common plugins include:

    • Calico: Uses BGP routing for high performance and advanced network policies.
    • Flannel: A simple overlay network (VXLAN) that encapsulates packets. Great for beginners.
    • Cilium: Uses eBPF for extremely high-performance filtering and observability at the kernel level.
    • AWS VPC CNI: Assigns real AWS VPC IP addresses to Pods (allowing them to talk to RDS/EC2 natively).

    7.2 Service Networking (East-West Traffic)

    Pods are ephemeral. Their IPs change. To solve this, we use Services. But how does a Service actually work? It is not a process. It is a stable Virtual IP (VIP) and a set of packet forwarding rules.

    The Magic of Kube-Proxy

    A Service IP (e.g., 10.96.0.1) does not exist on any network interface. It is a "ghost" IP.

    1. You create a Service my-service (IP: 10.96.0.1).
    2. kube-apiserver notifies kube-proxy (running on every node) about the new Service.
    3. kube-proxy writes iptables (or IPVS) rules on the Node's kernel.
    4. The Rule: "If a packet is destined for 10.96.0.1:80, DNAT (Destination NAT) it to one of the Pod IPs (e.g., 10.1.1.5:80)."

    This creates a client-side load balancer in the kernel of every node.

    7.3 Ingress Networking (North-South Traffic)

    Services handle traffic inside the cluster (East-West). To get traffic from the internet into the cluster (North-South), we typically use an Ingress Controller.

    Unlike a Service (which is Layer 4 TCP/UDP), Ingress operates at Layer 7 (HTTP/HTTPS). It can make intelligent routing decisions:

    • Host Routing: api.example.com goes to the API service; www.example.com goes to the Web service.
    • Path Routing: example.com/v1 goes to Legacy API; example.com/v2 goes to New API.
    • TLS Termination: The Ingress Controller handles the SSL certificates, decrypting traffic before passing it to the pods (which saves CPU on the pods).

    7.4 Securing the Network

    By default, Kubernetes is a "soft shell." Any pod can talk to any other pod, even across namespaces. This is a security risk. To lock this down, we use Network Policies.

    A Network Policy is like a firewall rule for pods. It allows you to say: "Only the Frontend pods are allowed to talk to the Backend pods on port 8080. Block everything else."

    ⚠️ Requirement

    Network Policies require a CNI plugin that supports them (like Calico or Cilium). Flannel does not support Network Policies; creating them on Flannel will simply do nothing.

    7.5 Storage Architecture: The PV Subsystem

    Containers write to a writable layer that disappears when the container dies. For databases, we need data to survive pod restarts, node failures, and even cluster upgrades.

    Kubernetes decouples storage users (developers) from storage providers (admins/cloud) using three API objects:

    The Storage Trinity

    1. PersistentVolume (PV)

    The physical piece of storage in the infrastructure. It is a cluster-level resource (like a Node). It represents an actual EBS volume, NFS share, or local disk.
    Analogy: A physical USB drive plugged into the server.

    2. PersistentVolumeClaim (PVC)

    A request for storage by a user. It lives in a Namespace. A PVC claims a specific size and access mode (e.g., "I need 10GB ReadWriteOnce").
    Analogy: A voucher or ticket redeemable for storage.

    3. StorageClass (SC)

    The "Provisioner." Instead of creating PVs manually, the SC listens for PVCs and automatically calls the cloud provider API to create the volume (e.g., AWS EBS) and creates the PV object to bind to the PVC. This is called Dynamic Provisioning.

    7.6 Access Modes & Volume Types

    Not all storage is created equal. You must choose the right type for your workload.

    # Access Modes ReadWriteOnce (RWO): Mounted by a single Node (Block Storage) ReadWriteMany (RWX): Mounted by multiple Nodes simultaneously (File Storage) ReadOnlyMany (ROX): Mounted by multiple Nodes, read-only
    • Block Storage (RWO): AWS EBS, Azure Disk, GCE PD. High performance, low latency. Can only attach to ONE node at a time. Used for Databases (PostgreSQL, MySQL).
    • File Storage (RWX): AWS EFS, Azure Files, NFS. Slower, but can attach to multiple nodes. Used for shared content (CMS uploads, configuration files shared across web servers).

    7.7 The Binding Lifecycle

    Understanding how PVCs find PVs is crucial for debugging "Pending" storage.

    1. Provisioning: Administrator creates a PV (Static) or StorageClass creates one (Dynamic).
    2. Binding: User creates a PVC. Kubernetes looks for a PV that matches the request (Size, Access Mode, StorageClass). If found, they are bound together (1-to-1 mapping).
    3. Using: Pod references the PVC as a volume. Kubelet tells the Node to mount the physical device associated with the PV.
    4. Reclaiming: When user deletes PVC, the PV is either Retained (saved) or Deleted (wiped), depending on the ReclaimPolicy.
    💡 Production Tip: Retain Policy

    Default StorageClasses usually have reclaimPolicy: Delete. This means if you accidentally delete a PVC (e.g., `helm uninstall`), your database volume is deleted from the cloud provider immediately. For production databases, always set `reclaimPolicy: Retain` or use Volume Snapshots.

    Hands-On Labs

    These labs are designed to break the "magic" of Kubernetes networking and storage. You will manually trace a packet across the cluster, configure an Ingress load balancer, lock down traffic with firewalls (Network Policies), and provision persistent disk storage that survives pod destruction.

    ⚠️ Prerequisites

    Ensure your local cluster is running.
    Network Tools: We will use the nicolaka/netshoot image for debugging. It contains tools like dig, nslookup, curl, and tcpdump.
    Namespace: Create a clean namespace: kubectl create ns week7 and switch to it.

    Lab 1: Internal DNS & Service Discovery

    Objective: Prove that Kubernetes provides a stable internal DNS system. You will deploy two pods and make them communicate using hostnames, bypassing unstable IP addresses.

    Navigate to your lab working directory:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course/week-07-kubernetes-networking/lab-01-dns-discovery

    Every file you create in this lab goes into this directory. Stay here for all remaining steps unless a step explicitly tells you to change directory.

    Deploy a Backend Service:

    First, create a simple Nginx deployment and expose it as a Service.

    # Create Deployment kubectl create deployment backend --image=nginx --replicas=2 --port=80 # Expose it as a ClusterIP Service kubectl expose deployment backend --name=backend-svc --port=80 --target-port=80
    Deploy a "Netshoot" Client:

    Run an interactive pod to act as our network troubleshooter.

    kubectl run client --rm -it --image=nicolaka/netshoot -- /bin/bash

    You are now inside the container's shell.

    Test 1: DNS Resolution:

    Inside the client pod, query the internal DNS server.

    # Lookup the service by name dig backend-svc.week7.svc.cluster.local +short
    10.96.134.55 <-- This is the Service VIP (Virtual IP)
    Test 2: Connectivity via Name:

    Verify that you can reach the backend using the DNS name.

    curl http://backend-svc

    You should see the "Welcome to nginx!" HTML. This confirms Service Discovery is working. The application code doesn't need to know IPs; it just needs the name backend-svc.

    exit # Leave the pod

    Lab 2: Exposing Apps via Ingress

    Objective: Configure an Ingress Resource to route traffic based on a hostname (week7.local) to your backend service.

    Navigate to your lab working directory:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course/week-07-kubernetes-networking/lab-02-ingress

    Every file you create in this lab goes into this directory. Stay here for all remaining steps unless a step explicitly tells you to change directory.

    Verify Ingress Controller:
    # If using Minikube kubectl get pods -n ingress-nginx # If using Kind/Cloud kubectl get pods -n ingress-nginx

    Ensure the controller pod is Running. Without this, Ingress resources are just ignored text files.

    Define Ingress Resource:

    Create ingress.yaml.

    apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1 kind: Ingress metadata: name: week7-ingress annotations: nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/rewrite-target: / spec: ingressClassName: nginx rules: - host: week7.local http: paths: - path: / pathType: Prefix backend: service: name: backend-svc port: number: 80
    kubectl apply -f ingress.yaml
    Wait for Address:
    kubectl get ingress -w
    NAME CLASS HOSTS ADDRESS PORTS AGE week7-ingress nginx week7.local 192.168.49.2 80 30s

    Wait until the ADDRESS column gets an IP. If it stays empty for >2 minutes, check your Ingress Controller logs.

    Configure Local DNS (Spoofing):

    You don't own the domain week7.local, but you can trick your computer using curl headers or /etc/hosts.

    # Get the Ingress IP (Minikube users: 'minikube ip') INGRESS_IP=$(kubectl get ingress week7-ingress -o jsonpath='{.status.loadBalancer.ingress[0].ip}') # Send request with Host Header curl -H "Host: week7.local" http://$INGRESS_IP
    ✅ Success Criteria

    You should see the Nginx welcome page. This proves the Ingress Controller received the request, checked the Host header, matched it to your rule, and forwarded traffic to the backend Service.

    Lab 3: Implementing a "Default Deny" Firewall

    Objective: Lock down the namespace so pods cannot communicate unless explicitly allowed. This is a critical security practice (Zero Trust).

    ⚠️ CNI Requirement

    This lab requires a CNI that supports Network Policies (Calico, Cilium, Antrea). Minikube's default CNI (kicbase) often does NOT support this. If you are using standard Minikube, these policies might create successfully but have no effect. To verify support, start minikube with --cni=calico.

    Navigate to your lab working directory:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course/week-07-kubernetes-networking/lab-03-network-policies

    Every file you create in this lab goes into this directory. Stay here for all remaining steps unless a step explicitly tells you to change directory.

    Verify Connectivity (The "Before" State):

    Using your backend-svc from Lab 1.

    # Run a hacker pod kubectl run hacker --rm -it --image=alpine -- sh # Install curl apk add curl # Access backend (Should Succeed) curl --connect-timeout 2 http://backend-svc exit
    Apply "Default Deny" Policy:

    Block ALL ingress traffic to all pods in the namespace.

    apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1 kind: NetworkPolicy metadata: name: default-deny-all spec: podSelector: {} # Selects all pods policyTypes: - Ingress
    kubectl apply -f default-deny.yaml
    Verify Blocking:

    Try the hacker pod again.

    kubectl run hacker --rm -it --image=alpine -- sh apk add curl # Should TIMEOUT or Fail curl --connect-timeout 2 http://backend-svc
    Allow Specific Access:

    Allow only the Ingress Controller to talk to the backend.

    apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1 kind: NetworkPolicy metadata: name: allow-ingress-only spec: podSelector: matchLabels: app: backend # Only applies to backend pods ingress: - from: - namespaceSelector: matchLabels: kubernetes.io/metadata.name: ingress-nginx # Assuming controller is here

    Note: Selector labels depend on your specific Ingress Controller deployment labels.

    Lab 4: Manual Storage Provisioning

    Objective: Understand the relationship between persistent hardware (PV) and storage requests (PVC) by manually creating both.

    Navigate to your lab working directory:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course/week-07-kubernetes-networking/lab-04-manual-storage

    Every file you create in this lab goes into this directory. Stay here for all remaining steps unless a step explicitly tells you to change directory.

    Create the PersistentVolume (PV):

    This represents the physical disk. We use hostPath for simulation.

    apiVersion: v1 kind: PersistentVolume metadata: name: task-pv-volume labels: type: local spec: storageClassName: manual capacity: storage: 1Gi accessModes: - ReadWriteOnce hostPath: path: "/mnt/data"
    kubectl apply -f pv.yaml
    Create the PersistentVolumeClaim (PVC):

    This is the "ticket" requesting storage.

    apiVersion: v1 kind: PersistentVolumeClaim metadata: name: task-pv-claim spec: storageClassName: manual accessModes: - ReadWriteOnce resources: requests: storage: 500Mi
    kubectl apply -f pvc.yaml
    Verify Binding:
    kubectl get pv task-pv-volume kubectl get pvc task-pv-claim

    Status must be Bound. If it stays Available or Pending, check that storageClassName matches exactly.

    Mount to Pod:
    apiVersion: v1 kind: Pod metadata: name: task-pv-pod spec: containers: - name: task-pv-container image: nginx volumeMounts: - mountPath: "/usr/share/nginx/html" name: task-pv-storage volumes: - name: task-pv-storage persistentVolumeClaim: claimName: task-pv-claim
    kubectl apply -f pod.yaml
    Test Persistence:
    # Write data kubectl exec task-pv-pod -- sh -c "echo 'Hello Persisted World' > /usr/share/nginx/html/index.html" # Delete Pod kubectl delete pod task-pv-pod # Recreate Pod (apply yaml again) kubectl apply -f pod.yaml # Verify data exists kubectl exec task-pv-pod -- cat /usr/share/nginx/html/index.html

    Lab 5: Dynamic Provisioning (The "Easy Button")

    Objective: Use a StorageClass to automatically provision storage without manually creating PVs. This is how 99% of cloud storage is handled.

    Navigate to your lab working directory:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course/week-07-kubernetes-networking/lab-05-dynamic-provisioning

    Every file you create in this lab goes into this directory. Stay here for all remaining steps unless a step explicitly tells you to change directory.

    Check Default StorageClass:
    kubectl get sc

    Note the class marked (default) (e.g., standard, gp2, hostpath).

    Create a PVC (No PV!):

    Create dynamic-pvc.yaml. Do not specify storageClassName—it will use the default.

    apiVersion: v1 kind: PersistentVolumeClaim metadata: name: dynamic-claim spec: accessModes: - ReadWriteOnce resources: requests: storage: 1Gi
    kubectl apply -f dynamic-pvc.yaml
    Observe the Magic:
    kubectl get pvc dynamic-claim kubectl get pv
    ✨ Automation

    You should see the PVC is Bound.
    You should also see a new PV named pvc-xxxx-xxxx... that was automatically created by the Storage Provisioner. You didn't have to define the physical volume yourself!

    8

    Advanced Kubernetes Patterns

    Autoscaling, Health Probes, Scheduling, and Access Control

    Welcome to Week 8. By now, you can deploy applications, expose them to the internet, and persist their data. But is your cluster "Production Ready"? A production cluster isn't just about running containers; it's about resilience under load, self-healing capabilities, and security.

    This week, we implement the patterns that make Kubernetes truly powerful. We will configure Liveness and Readiness Probes so your applications only receive traffic when they are actually ready. We will implement Horizontal Pod Autoscaling (HPA) to automatically add more pods during traffic spikes. We will dive into Scheduling nuances (Taints, Tolerations, Affinity) to control exactly where workloads run. Finally, we will lock down the cluster using RBAC (Role-Based Access Control), moving away from the "admin-has-all-power" model to the principle of least privilege.

    Mentorship Guidance

    Mentorship students receive structured support for Advanced Kubernetes Patterns: live Q&A, assignment feedback, and office hours focused on this week's goals.

    Share blockers in Google Classroom and schedule a review session once your lab and assignment work are ready.

    Learning Objectives

    By the end of this week, you will be able to demonstrate mastery across all levels of Bloom's Taxonomy:

    Remember

    Define Mechanisms: Define the three types of probes (Liveness, Readiness, Startup), the components of RBAC (Role, RoleBinding, ServiceAccount), and the metric sources for Autoscaling.

    Understand

    Explain Logic: Explain how HPA calculates replica counts based on CPU utilization. Describe the difference between Taints (repelling pods) and Affinity (attracting pods).

    Apply

    Execute Configuration: Implement robust health checks to prevent broken deploys. Configure HPA to scale a web application from 1 to 10 pods under load. Create a ServiceAccount with restricted permissions.

    Analyze

    Diagnose Constraints: Analyze why a Pod is stuck in "Pending" due to Taints or insufficient resources. Debug 502 errors caused by failing Readiness probes.

    Evaluate

    Assess Strategies: Evaluate the trade-offs between Vertical Scaling (VPA) and Horizontal Scaling (HPA). Determine the appropriate QoS class (Guaranteed vs. BestEffort) for critical workloads.

    Create

    Build Policies: Author a complete RBAC policy that grants a specific user "read-only" access to a specific namespace while denying access to Secrets.

    Pre-Class Exercises

    Mandatory Requirement: Complete these exercises before beginning the teaching notes. Advanced features like Autoscaling rely on specific cluster addons (Metrics Server) that are not installed by default. Budget approximately 3-4 hours.

    ⚙ Week 08 Workspace Setup — Complete This First

    Create your working directory for this week inside the course repository. Every pre-class note, lab file, screenshot, and project deliverable belongs under this folder tree — nothing goes anywhere else.

    1. Navigate to your course repository root:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course
    2. Create this week's folder and all sub-directories in one command:
    mkdir -p week-08-advanced-kubernetes/{preclass-notes,lab-01-probes,lab-02-autoscaling,lab-03-node-affinity,lab-04-rbac,lab-05-resource-management,weekly-project}

    This single command creates the whole week's workspace tree. You will stay within this tree for pre-class work, every lab, and the weekly project.

    3. Verify the structure was created correctly:
    find week-08-advanced-kubernetes -type d | sort

    You should see 7 directories (preclass-notes + 5 lab folders + weekly-project).

    4. Create your pre-class notes file:
    touch week-08-advanced-kubernetes/preclass-notes/week-08-notes.md

    All research, written answers, and reflections for this week's pre-class exercises go into this Markdown file. Keep it open as you work through the exercises below.

    5. Commit the empty scaffold to your GitHub portfolio:
    git add week-08-advanced-kubernetes/ git commit -m "chore(week-08-advanced-kubernetes): scaffold week 08 working directory" git push

    Pushing the empty scaffold creates a timestamped entry on your GitHub profile showing when you started this week — visible evidence for your portfolio and mentorship reviews.

    📁 Week 08 Folder Structure
    ~/devops-engineering-course/ └── week-08-advanced-kubernetes/ ├── preclass-notes/ ├── lab-01-probes/ ├── lab-02-autoscaling/ ├── lab-03-node-affinity/ ├── lab-04-rbac/ ├── lab-05-resource-management/ └── weekly-project/

    1Install the Metrics Server

    Kubernetes cannot scale your applications if it doesn't know how much CPU/RAM they are using. The Metrics Server is the component that collects these stats. It is required for the HPA lab.

    For Minikube Users:

    minikube addons enable metrics-server

    For Kind Users:

    kubectl apply -f https://github.com/kubernetes-sigs/metrics-server/releases/latest/download/components.yaml # Patch for Kind (allow insecure TLS for local testing) kubectl patch deployment metrics-server -n kube-system --type='json' -p='[{"op": "add", "path": "/spec/template/spec/containers/0/args/-", "value": "--kubelet-insecure-tls"}]'

    Verification: Run kubectl top nodes. If you see CPU/Memory usage numbers, you are ready. If you see "metrics API not available," troubleshoot before proceeding.

    2Resource Limits Refresher

    Autoscaling and Scheduling depend entirely on Resource Requests and Limits.

    Task: Research and write a concise explanation of:

    • Request: What does the Scheduler do with this number? (Hint: Scheduling / Bin-packing).
    • Limit: What does the Container Runtime do with this number? (Hint: Throttling / OOMKilled).
    • QoS Classes: What are Guaranteed, Burstable, and BestEffort classes? Which one gets evicted first when the node runs out of RAM?
    💡 Critical Concept

    If you don't set Requests, the HPA cannot calculate utilization percentages, and Autoscaling will fail.

    3The "Thundering Herd" Problem

    Imagine you have a Java application that takes 45 seconds to start up (load classes, connect to DB).

    Thought Experiment: You deploy an update. Kubernetes kills the old pod and starts the new one immediately. The new container process starts (PID 1 exists), so Kubernetes sends traffic to it instantly.

    Question: What happens to the user requests sent during those first 45 seconds? How does a Readiness Probe solve this? Write down your hypothesis.

    4RBAC Roleplay

    Scenario: You are the Lead DevOps Engineer. You hired a "Junior Developer" named Alice.

    Task: List the permissions Alice needs. Does she need to delete Nodes? (No). Does she need to read Secrets in the production namespace? (Probably not). Does she need to view Pod logs in Dev? (Yes).

    Sketch a permission matrix:

    Action Namespace Alice's Access
    Get/List Pods All Allow
    Read Secrets Production Deny
    Delete Deployments Dev Allow

    5Load Generator Tool

    To test Autoscaling, we need to stress our cluster.

    Task: We will rely on a simple busybox loop in the labs, but for robust testing, install Hey (a modern HTTP load generator replacement for Apache Bench).

    # Mac/Linux (Homebrew) brew install hey # Or download binary from https://github.com/rakyll/hey

    Detailed Teaching Notes

    In the previous weeks, we focused on "Day 1" operations: getting applications deployed, networked, and persisted. Week 8 focuses on "Day 2" operations: Reliability, Scalability, and Security.

    A cluster isn't production-ready until it can handle traffic spikes without crashing (Autoscaling), detect and fix zombie processes (Probes), intelligently place workloads on appropriate hardware (Scheduling), and prevent unauthorized access (RBAC).

    8.1 Application Health: Probes

    By default, Kubernetes assumes an application is "healthy" as long as the container process (PID 1) is running. However, a Java app might be running but stuck in a deadlock, or a Node.js app might be running but disconnected from the database.

    Kubernetes solves this with Probes. Kubelet performs these diagnostic checks periodically.

    The Three Guardians

    1. Liveness Probe (The Defibrillator)

    Question: "Are you alive?"
    Action if Failed: Restart the container.
    Use Case: Deadlocks, infinite loops, frozen applications that cannot recover without a restart.

    2. Readiness Probe (The Traffic Cop)

    Question: "Are you ready to serve traffic?"
    Action if Failed: Remove the Pod's IP from the Service endpoints (stop sending traffic). Do not restart.
    Use Case: App is initializing, loading large cache files, or waiting for a database connection.

    3. Startup Probe (The Patient Waiter)

    Question: "Have you finished starting up?"
    Action: Disables Liveness/Readiness checks until this succeeds.
