    Use Case: Legacy monoliths that take 5 minutes to boot. Without this, the Liveness probe would kill the app before it ever finished starting.

    # Example: Java App Configuration apiVersion: v1 kind: Pod spec: containers: - name: java-app image: my-java-app:v1 # 1. Wait for slow startup startupProbe: httpGet: path: /health port: 8080 failureThreshold: 30 periodSeconds: 10 # Allow 30 * 10s = 5 mins to boot # 2. Check if ready for traffic readinessProbe: httpGet: path: /ready port: 8080 initialDelaySeconds: 5 periodSeconds: 5 # 3. Check for deadlocks livenessProbe: httpGet: path: /health port: 8080 initialDelaySeconds: 10 periodSeconds: 20
    ⚠️ The Liveness Trap

    Do not make your Liveness probe depend on external services (like the Database). If the DB goes down, the Liveness probe fails, and K8s restarts your Web App. Restarting the Web App won't fix the DB. This creates a cascading failure loop. Liveness should only check internal app state. Readiness can check external dependencies.

    8.2 Autoscaling: HPA

    Static replica counts (replicas: 3) are inefficient. Traffic fluctuates. The Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA) automatically updates a workload resource (Deployment or StatefulSet) to scale the number of Pods up or down based on observed CPU utilization or custom metrics.

    📈 The HPA Algorithm

    The HPA controller loop runs every 15 seconds (default). It queries the Metrics Server.

    DesiredReplicas = ceil[CurrentReplicas * ( CurrentMetricValue / DesiredMetricValue )]

    Example: You have 2 pods. Target CPU is 50%. Current CPU is 100%.
    2 * (100 / 50) = 4 Replicas.

    apiVersion: autoscaling/v2 kind: HorizontalPodAutoscaler metadata: name: php-apache spec: scaleTargetRef: apiVersion: apps/v1 kind: Deployment name: php-apache minReplicas: 1 maxReplicas: 10 metrics: - type: Resource resource: name: cpu target: type: Utilization averageUtilization: 50
    💡 Cluster Autoscaler Connection

    HPA scales Pods. If you scale from 10 to 100 pods, your Nodes might run out of capacity. The Cluster Autoscaler (managed by cloud providers like AWS/GCP) watches for "Pending" pods due to resource shortage and provisions new physical Nodes (EC2 instances).

    8.3 Advanced Scheduling: Placing Workloads

    The Scheduler's default behavior is "bin packing"—put the pod on the least busy node. Sometimes, you need more control.

    1. Taints and Tolerations (The Repellent)

    Taints are applied to Nodes. They repel pods.
    Tolerations are applied to Pods. They allow (but do not require) a pod to schedule onto a tainted node.

    Use Case: You have expensive GPU nodes. You Taint them gpu=true:NoSchedule so regular web apps don't waste the space. Only AI workloads with the matching Toleration can land there.

    2. Node Affinity (The Magnet)

    Affinity allows you to constrain which nodes your pod can be scheduled on based on node labels.

    • requiredDuringScheduling: Hard rule. If no node matches, Pod stays Pending. (e.g., "Must run on Intel CPU").
    • preferredDuringScheduling: Soft rule. "Try to run on Zone A, but Zone B is okay if A is full."

    3. Pod Affinity/Anti-Affinity

    Rules based on other pods running on the node.

    • Anti-Affinity: "Do not run this Web Pod on a node that already has a Web Pod." (Spreads replicas for High Availability).
    • Affinity: "Run this Cache Pod on the same node as the Web Pod." (Reduces network latency).

    8.4 Managing Resources & QoS

    When you define resources, you assign a Quality of Service (QoS) class to your pod. This determines who gets killed first when the node runs out of memory (OOM).

    QoS Classes

    Class Definition OOM Kill Priority
    Guaranteed Requests == Limits (for both CPU & RAM) Last to be killed (Gold Class)
    Burstable Requests < Limits Medium priority
    BestEffort No Requests or Limits set First to be killed (Trash Class)

    8.5 Security: Role-Based Access Control (RBAC)

    Kubernetes is an API. RBAC controls who can hit that API and what they can do. It follows a "Default Deny" model.

    🛡️ The RBAC Triad

    1. Subject (Who): User (Human), Group (Devs), or ServiceAccount (Application/Bot).
    2. Resource (What): Pods, Services, Deployments, Secrets.
    3. Verb (Action): Get, List, Watch, Create, Update, Delete.

    Binding it together:

    • Role: Defines permissions within a specific Namespace (e.g., "Can read pods in 'dev'").
    • RoleBinding: Connects a Subject to a Role (e.g., "User 'Alice' gets the 'read-pods' Role in 'dev'").
    • ClusterRole / ClusterRoleBinding: Same as above, but cluster-wide (e.g., "Can read Nodes" - nodes are not namespaced).
    # 1. Define the Role apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1 kind: Role metadata: namespace: dev name: pod-reader rules: - apiGroups: [""] resources: ["pods", "pods/log"] verbs: ["get", "watch", "list"] --- # 2. Bind the Role to a User apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1 kind: RoleBinding metadata: name: read-pods namespace: dev subjects: - kind: User name: alice apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io roleRef: kind: Role name: pod-reader apiGroup: rbac.authorization.k8s.io

    Hands-On Labs

    In these labs, you will push Kubernetes to its limits. You will intentionally deploy slow applications to test Probes, flood your cluster with traffic to trigger Autoscaling, restrict scheduling with Taints, and lock down permissions with RBAC.

    ⚠️ Prerequisites

    Metrics Server: Labs 2 and 5 require the Metrics Server to be running. Verify with kubectl top nodes.
    Namespace: Create a fresh namespace: kubectl create ns week8 and switch to it.

    Lab 1: Configuring Liveness & Readiness Probes

    Objective: Deploy an application that takes time to start up and occasionally "freezes." Configure probes to handle startup gracefully and restart the container when it freezes.

    Navigate to your lab working directory:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course/week-08-advanced-kubernetes/lab-01-probes

    Every file you create in this lab goes into this directory. Stay here for all remaining steps unless a step explicitly tells you to change directory.

    Create the Manifest:

    Create probes.yaml. We simulate a "freeze" by creating a file /tmp/healthy and deleting it after 30 seconds.

    apiVersion: v1 kind: Pod metadata: name: liveness-demo spec: containers: - name: liveness image: busybox args: - /bin/sh - -c - touch /tmp/healthy; sleep 30; rm -f /tmp/healthy; sleep 600 # 1. Liveness Probe (Checks file existence) livenessProbe: exec: command: - cat - /tmp/healthy initialDelaySeconds: 5 periodSeconds: 5 # 2. Readiness Probe (Simulates startup check) readinessProbe: exec: command: - cat - /tmp/healthy initialDelaySeconds: 2 periodSeconds: 5
    Apply and Watch:
    kubectl apply -f probes.yaml # Watch the status change kubectl get pod liveness-demo -w
    Analyze the Lifecycle:

    0-30s: The file exists. Probes succeed. Pod is Running and Ready (1/1).
    30s: The command deletes the file.
    35s+ : Liveness probe fails. Kubernetes kills the container.
    Observation: You will see RESTARTS count increment to 1.

    Verify Events:
    kubectl describe pod liveness-demo
    Warning Unhealthy ... Liveness probe failed: cat: can't open '/tmp/healthy': No such file or directory Normal Killing ... Container liveness failed liveness probe, will be restarted

    Lab 2: Autoscaling Under Load

    Objective: Deploy a CPU-intensive PHP application and configure HPA to scale it from 1 to 5 replicas when load increases.

    Navigate to your lab working directory:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course/week-08-advanced-kubernetes/lab-02-autoscaling

    Every file you create in this lab goes into this directory. Stay here for all remaining steps unless a step explicitly tells you to change directory.

    Deploy the Target App:

    We use a custom image designed to burn CPU on every request.

    apiVersion: apps/v1 kind: Deployment metadata: name: php-apache spec: selector: matchLabels: run: php-apache replicas: 1 template: metadata: labels: run: php-apache spec: containers: - name: php-apache image: registry.k8s.io/hpa-example ports: - containerPort: 80 resources: limits: cpu: 500m requests: cpu: 200m --- apiVersion: v1 kind: Service metadata: name: php-apache spec: ports: - port: 80 selector: run: php-apache
    kubectl apply -f php-apache.yaml
    Create the HPA:
    # Autoscale when CPU > 50% kubectl autoscale deployment php-apache --cpu-percent=50 --min=1 --max=5 # Verify HPA state kubectl get hpa
    NAME REFERENCE TARGETS MINPODS MAXPODS REPLICAS AGE php-apache Deployment/php-apache 0%/50% 1 5 1 15s
    Generate Load:

    Run a separate pod acting as a load generator loop.

    kubectl run -it --rm load-generator --image=busybox -- /bin/sh # Inside the pod, run this infinite loop: while true; do wget -q -O- http://php-apache; done
    Observe Scaling:

    Open a new terminal window to watch the cluster.

    kubectl get hpa -w

    Within 1-2 minutes, you will see TARGETS spike (e.g., 250%/50%) and REPLICAS increase to 5.

    Cooldown:

    Stop the load generator (Ctrl+C). Watch the HPA. After a cooldown period (default 5 minutes), it will scale back down to 1.

    Lab 3: Controlling Scheduler Placement

    Objective: Simulate a "Dedicated GPU" node by Tainting it, then deploy a Pod that tolerates that taint.

    Navigate to your lab working directory:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course/week-08-advanced-kubernetes/lab-03-node-affinity

    Every file you create in this lab goes into this directory. Stay here for all remaining steps unless a step explicitly tells you to change directory.

    Taint the Node:

    We will apply a Taint to your single Minikube/Kind node. This usually prevents new pods from scheduling unless they tolerate it.

    # Get node name kubectl get nodes # Apply taint (replace 'minikube' with your node name) kubectl taint nodes minikube specialized=true:NoSchedule
    Attempt Normal Deployment:
    kubectl run normal-pod --image=nginx # Check status kubectl get pod normal-pod
    NAME READY STATUS RESTARTS AGE normal-pod 0/1 Pending 0 10s

    The pod is Pending. kubectl describe pod normal-pod will show "1 node(s) had untolerated taint {specialized: true}".

    Deploy Tolerant Pod:

    Create tolerant.yaml with the matching toleration.

    apiVersion: v1 kind: Pod metadata: name: tolerant-pod spec: containers: - name: nginx image: nginx tolerations: - key: "specialized" operator: "Equal" value: "true" effect: "NoSchedule"
    kubectl apply -f tolerant.yaml kubectl get pod tolerant-pod

    This pod should transition to Running.

    Cleanup (Important!):

    Remove the taint so your cluster returns to normal.

    # The minus sign removes the taint kubectl taint nodes minikube specialized- # Delete the pending pod kubectl delete pod normal-pod

    Lab 4: Creating a Restricted "View-Only" User

    Objective: Create a ServiceAccount, bind it to a Role that allows viewing Pods but denies deleting them, and verify the permissions using impersonation.

    Navigate to your lab working directory:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course/week-08-advanced-kubernetes/lab-04-rbac

    Every file you create in this lab goes into this directory. Stay here for all remaining steps unless a step explicitly tells you to change directory.

    Create ServiceAccount:
    kubectl create serviceaccount intern
    Define the Role:

    Create role.yaml allowing read-only access to pods.

    apiVersion: rbac.authorization.k8s.io/v1 kind: Role metadata: namespace: week8 name: pod-viewer rules: - apiGroups: [""] resources: ["pods", "pods/log"] verbs: ["get", "watch", "list"]
    kubectl apply -f role.yaml
    Bind Role to Account:
    kubectl create rolebinding intern-binding \ --role=pod-viewer \ --serviceaccount=week8:intern
    Verify Permissions (Can-I):

    Use the auth can-i command to check permissions for the 'intern' account.

    # Can intern list pods? kubectl auth can-i list pods --as=system:serviceaccount:week8:intern # Output: yes # Can intern delete pods? kubectl auth can-i delete pods --as=system:serviceaccount:week8:intern # Output: no # Can intern read secrets? kubectl auth can-i get secrets --as=system:serviceaccount:week8:intern # Output: no
    Test Real Access:

    Try to delete the pod from Lab 3 as the intern.

    kubectl delete pod tolerant-pod --as=system:serviceaccount:week8:intern
    Error from server (Forbidden): pods "tolerant-pod" is forbidden: User ... cannot delete resource "pods" in API group "" in the namespace "week8"

    Lab 5: Managing Resources & OOMKilled

    Objective: Constrain a Pod's memory usage and intentionally trigger an Out Of Memory (OOM) Kill to understand how Kubernetes handles resource exhaustion.

    Navigate to your lab working directory:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course/week-08-advanced-kubernetes/lab-05-resource-management

    Every file you create in this lab goes into this directory. Stay here for all remaining steps unless a step explicitly tells you to change directory.

    Create a Memory-Hungry Pod:

    Create oom-pod.yaml. We will use the polinux/stress image to consume 150MB of RAM, but we will limit the container to 100MB.

    apiVersion: v1 kind: Pod metadata: name: memory-demo spec: containers: - name: stress image: polinux/stress resources: limits: memory: "100Mi" requests: memory: "50Mi" command: ["stress"] args: ["--vm", "1", "--vm-bytes", "150M", "--vm-hang", "1"]
    Apply and Monitor:
    kubectl apply -f oom-pod.yaml # Watch status kubectl get pod memory-demo -w
    Observe the Crash:

    The pod status will change from Running to OOMKilled, and then to CrashLoopBackOff.

    NAME READY STATUS RESTARTS memory-demo 0/1 OOMKilled 1 (5s ago)
    Inspect the Cause:

    Use describe to see the exact reason.

    kubectl describe pod memory-demo
    State: Waiting Reason: CrashLoopBackOff Last State: Terminated Reason: OOMKilled Exit Code: 137
    💡 Exit Code 137

    Exit code 137 = 128 + 9 (SIGKILL). This means the Linux kernel sent a SIGKILL signal to the process because it tried to exceed the cgroup memory limit set by Kubernetes.

    9

    Helm & Kubernetes Package Management

    Templating, Packaging, and Managing Complex Releases

    Welcome to Week 9. Up to this point, you have been crafting Kubernetes manifests by hand—writing static YAML files for Deployments, Services, and ConfigMaps. While this is excellent for learning the API, it creates significant friction in real-world scenarios. How do you deploy the same application to Development (1 replica), Staging (2 replicas), and Production (10 replicas) without maintaining three nearly identical files? How do you share your complex application stack with other teams?

    Enter Helm, the Package Manager for Kubernetes. Helm allows you to define, install, and upgrade even the most complex Kubernetes application. It introduces the concept of Charts (packages) and Templates (dynamic configuration). By the end of this week, you will stop copying-and-pasting YAML and start building reusable, version-controlled infrastructure packages that can be deployed anywhere with a single command.

    Mentorship Guidance

    Mentorship students receive structured support for Helm & Kubernetes Package Management: live Q&A, assignment feedback, and office hours focused on this week's goals.

    Share blockers in Google Classroom and schedule a review session once your lab and assignment work are ready.

    Learning Objectives

    By the end of this week, you will be able to demonstrate mastery across all levels of Bloom's Taxonomy:

    Remember

    Define Core Concepts: Define the three pillars of Helm: Charts (packages), Config (values), and Releases (running instances). Identify the standard directory structure of a Helm chart.

    Understand

    Explain Mechanics: Explain how Helm merges the values.yaml file with templates to generate valid Kubernetes manifests. Describe the lifecycle of a Helm Release (Install → Upgrade → Rollback).

    Apply

    Execute Operations: Install applications from public repositories (e.g., Bitnami). Perform atomic upgrades and rollbacks of releases. Use helm lint and --dry-run to validate changes.

    Analyze

    Diagnose Failures: Troubleshoot template rendering errors. Analyze failed releases using helm status and deployment history. Identify when configuration drift occurs between values and running state.

    Evaluate

    Assess Approaches: Evaluate the trade-offs between using "Off-the-shelf" charts vs. building "Library" charts. Critique the security implications of using Tiller (Helm v2) vs. the client-only architecture of Helm v3.

    Create

    Build Artifacts: Author a production-ready Helm Chart from scratch, including dynamic templates, dependency management (Subcharts), and helper functions in _helpers.tpl.

    Pre-Class Exercises

    Mandatory Requirement: Complete these exercises before beginning the teaching notes. Helm is a new CLI tool that requires specific setup and familiarity with templating logic. Budget approximately 2-3 hours.

    ⚙ Week 09 Workspace Setup — Complete This First

    Create your working directory for this week inside the course repository. Every pre-class note, lab file, screenshot, and project deliverable belongs under this folder tree — nothing goes anywhere else.

    1. Navigate to your course repository root:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course
    2. Create this week's folder and all sub-directories in one command:
    mkdir -p week-09-helm-package-management/{preclass-notes,lab-01-installing-charts,lab-02-templating,lab-03-advanced-templating,lab-04-upgrades-rollbacks,lab-05-dependencies,weekly-project}

    This single command creates the whole week's workspace tree. You will stay within this tree for pre-class work, every lab, and the weekly project.

    3. Verify the structure was created correctly:
    find week-09-helm-package-management -type d | sort

    You should see 7 directories (preclass-notes + 5 lab folders + weekly-project).

    4. Create your pre-class notes file:
    touch week-09-helm-package-management/preclass-notes/week-09-notes.md

    All research, written answers, and reflections for this week's pre-class exercises go into this Markdown file. Keep it open as you work through the exercises below.

    5. Commit the empty scaffold to your GitHub portfolio:
    git add week-09-helm-package-management/ git commit -m "chore(week-09-helm-package-management): scaffold week 09 working directory" git push

    Pushing the empty scaffold creates a timestamped entry on your GitHub profile showing when you started this week — visible evidence for your portfolio and mentorship reviews.

    📁 Week 09 Folder Structure
    ~/devops-engineering-course/ └── week-09-helm-package-management/ ├── preclass-notes/ ├── lab-01-installing-charts/ ├── lab-02-templating/ ├── lab-03-advanced-templating/ ├── lab-04-upgrades-rollbacks/ ├── lab-05-dependencies/ └── weekly-project/

    1Install Helm CLI

    Helm is a single binary executable. Install it on your workstation.

    # macOS (Homebrew) brew install helm # Windows (Chocolatey) choco install kubernetes-helm # Linux/Unix (Script) curl -fsSL -o get_helm.sh https://raw.githubusercontent.com/helm/helm/main/scripts/get-helm-3 chmod 700 get_helm.sh ./get_helm.sh

    Verification: Run helm version. Ensure you are running v3.x.x (Helm v2 is deprecated and insecure).

    2The Go Template Primer

    Helm uses the Go templating language. It looks like {{ .Values.image }}. If you haven't seen this syntax before, it can be confusing.

    Task: Research "Go Template Syntax" specifically for Helm. Write down the syntax for:

    • Inserting a value.
    • Setting a default value if one is missing.
    • Looping over a list (range).
    • Conditionals (if/else).
    💡 Why this matters

    In the labs, you will be writing logic inside YAML files. Understanding the difference between {{ .Values.foo }} and {{ .Release.Name }} is critical.

    3Explore Artifact Hub

    Artifact Hub is the central registry for public Helm charts.

    Task: Visit artifacthub.io.
    1. Search for "MySQL".
    2. Find the chart maintained by Bitnami.
    3. Click "Default Values". Scroll through the configuration. This file (`values.yaml`) is the API documentation for the chart.

    4Add Common Repositories

    Configure your local Helm client with the most popular chart repositories.

    # Bitnami (Database & Infrastructure) helm repo add bitnami https://charts.bitnami.com/bitnami # Prometheus Community (Monitoring) helm repo add prometheus-community https://prometheus-community.github.io/helm-charts # Update your local cache helm repo update

    5Cluster Prep

    Ensure your local Kubernetes cluster (Minikube/Kind) is running and healthy. Clean up any lingering resources from Week 8 to ensure a blank canvas.

    kubectl delete namespace overwatch-prod kubectl delete namespace week8 # Verify clean state kubectl get all --all-namespaces

    Detailed Teaching Notes

    If Kubernetes is the operating system of the cloud, Helm is its package manager. Just as you wouldn't compile every piece of software on your laptop from source code, you shouldn't write every Kubernetes manifest from scratch. Helm solves the problem of Complexity Management by treating a collection of Kubernetes resources as a single logical unit.

    9.1 The "Parameterization" Problem

    In Week 8, you likely copied `deployment.yaml` to create `deployment-prod.yaml`, changing only the `replicas` and `image` tag. This is the "Parameterization Problem."

    Without Helm: You maintain static files. To change the image tag across 50 microservices, you must edit 50 files.
    With Helm: You maintain one Template. You inject the image tag as a Value at runtime.

    📦 The Three Pillars of Helm

    1. The Chart (Package): A bundle of information necessary to create an instance of a Kubernetes application. It contains templates and default configuration. (Analogy: A Docker Image or an `.msi` file).

    2. The Config (Values): Configuration information that is merged into the chart to create a releasable object. This contains your specific settings like environment variables, resource limits, and secrets.

    3. The Release (Instance): A running instance of a Chart, combined with a specific Config. You can install the same Chart five times to create five different Releases (e.g., `mysql-dev`, `mysql-staging`, `mysql-prod`).

    9.2 Helm Architecture (v3 vs v2)

    It is important to understand the architecture, especially if you encounter older tutorials.

    Helm v2 (Legacy): Relied on a server-side component called Tiller running inside the cluster. Tiller had "root" access to the cluster, which was a massive security risk.
    Helm v3 (Current): Client-only architecture. The Helm binary on your laptop talks directly to the Kubernetes API server using your local `kubeconfig` credentials. It uses Kubernetes Secrets to store the state of releases.

    9.3 Anatomy of a Chart

    A Chart is a directory with a strict structure.

    Directory Structure

    wordpress/ Chart.yaml # A YAML file containing information about the chart LICENSE # OPTIONAL: A plain text file containing the license for the chart README.md # OPTIONAL: A readme file values.yaml # The default configuration values for this chart charts/ # A directory containing any charts upon which this chart depends templates/ # A directory of templates that, when combined with values, # will generate valid Kubernetes manifest files. templates/NOTES.txt # OPTIONAL: A plain text file containing short usage notes
    values.yaml (The Interface)

    This file defines the API of your chart. If a setting is in `values.yaml`, users can change it easily. If it's hardcoded in `templates/`, they cannot.

    templates/_helpers.tpl

    A place to put shared logic and partials. For example, a standard way to generate the "app name" label so it's consistent across your Deployment, Service, and Ingress.

    9.4 The Templating Engine

    Helm uses the Go Template language. It works by injecting values into placeholders wrapped in double curly braces {{ }}.

    🧩 The Dot (.) Context

    In Helm templates, you will frequently see .Values or .Release. The leading dot . represents the Root Context (the scope). It contains several top-level objects:
    .Values: Access to `values.yaml` and `--set` flags.
    .Release: Metadata about the deployment (Name, Namespace, Revision).
    .Chart: Metadata from `Chart.yaml` (Version, Maintainers).
    .Capabilities: Info about the Kubernetes cluster (Version, API availability).

    Example: Dynamic Replica Count

    Input (values.yaml):

    replicaCount: 3 image: repository: nginx tag: 1.21

    Template (deployment.yaml):

    apiVersion: apps/v1 kind: Deployment spec: replicas: {{ .Values.replicaCount }} template: spec: containers: - image: "{{ .Values.image.repository }}:{{ .Values.image.tag }}"

    Functions and Pipelines: Helm includes hundreds of functions (like `upper`, `quote`, `b64enc`) that can be chained using pipes |.

    # Example: Quoting a value to ensure it's a string key: {{ .Values.someValue | quote }} # Example: Default value if missing port: {{ .Values.service.port | default 80 }}

    9.5 Release Lifecycle Management

    Helm tracks the history of every release. This allows for transactional updates.

    The 3-Way Merge

    When you run `helm upgrade`, Helm performs a "3-Way Merge" patch to determine what to do. It compares:

    1. The Old Chart: The manifest currently installed.
    2. The New Chart: The manifest you want to install.
    3. The Live State: The actual state of the cluster (what `kubectl get` shows).

    Why Live State? If you manually scaled a deployment to 5 replicas via kubectl, but your new chart says 3, Helm needs to decide whether to respect your manual change or overwrite it. (Helm generally overwrites manual changes to match the desired state).

    💡 Secret Management

    Helm stores its own state (release history) in Kubernetes Secrets in the namespace where the release is installed. You can view them: kubectl get secrets -l owner=helm. This is how Helm knows what version 1 looked like when you ask to rollback from version 2.

    9.6 Dependency Management (Subcharts)

    Modern applications rarely stand alone. Your API might need Redis and PostgreSQL. Instead of installing three separate charts manually, you can define them as Dependencies.

    Chart.yaml:

    dependencies: - name: redis version: 17.3.x repository: https://charts.bitnami.com/bitnami condition: redis.enabled

    When you run helm dependency update, Helm downloads the Redis chart into your charts/ directory. You can then configure Redis from your parent `values.yaml` by nesting the config:

    # Parent values.yaml redis: enabled: true auth: password: secretpassword
    ⚠️ "Condition" Logic

    The condition: redis.enabled line in Chart.yaml creates a toggle. If you set redis.enabled: false in your values, Helm will completely ignore the Redis subchart during installation. This is useful for optional features.

    Hands-On Labs

    Theory is useful, but Helm is a tool you learn by doing. In these labs, you will progress from a consumer of public charts to an author of complex, multi-component packages. You will master the template syntax, manage release history, and integrate third-party dependencies.

    ⚠️ Prerequisites

    Ensure helm is installed (v3+) and your cluster is running.
    Create a clean namespace: kubectl create ns week9 and switch to it using kubens week9 (or kubectl config set-context --current --namespace=week9).

    Lab 1: Installing Public Charts

    Objective: Deploy a production-ready Nginx server using the Bitnami repository. Understand how to search, install, and override values without editing source files.

    Navigate to your lab working directory:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course/week-09-helm-package-management/lab-01-installing-charts

    Every file you create in this lab goes into this directory. Stay here for all remaining steps unless a step explicitly tells you to change directory.

    Add the Repository:

    Helm needs to know where to look for charts.

    helm repo add bitnami https://charts.bitnami.com/bitnami helm repo update
    Search and Inspect:
    helm search repo bitnami/nginx # View the default values without downloading the chart helm show values bitnami/nginx > default-values.yaml

    Open default-values.yaml in your editor. This is your documentation. Notice the vast number of configurable options (resources, ingress, autoscaling, etc.).

    Install with Overrides (The CLI Way):

    We will install the chart but force it to use 2 replicas and a NodePort service.

    helm install my-webserver bitnami/nginx \ --set replicaCount=2 \ --set service.type=NodePort
    Verify the Release:
    helm list kubectl get pods -l app.kubernetes.io/instance=my-webserver
    NAME NAMESPACE REVISION STATUS CHART APP VERSION my-webserver week9 1 deployed nginx-15.x.x 1.25.x
    Cleanup:
    helm uninstall my-webserver

    Lab 2: Scaffolding and Basic Templating

    Objective: Use the helm create scaffolder to build a custom chart structure and understand how values.yaml injects data into templates/.

    Navigate to your lab working directory:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course/week-09-helm-package-management/lab-02-templating

    Every file you create in this lab goes into this directory. Stay here for all remaining steps unless a step explicitly tells you to change directory.

    Generate the Scaffold:
    helm create mychart ls -R mychart

    Helm creates a "Best Practice" chart for Nginx. It includes Ingress, ServiceAccount, and Autoscaling templates automatically.

    Modify default values:

    Open mychart/values.yaml. Change the image repository to something lightweight for testing.

    image: repository: stefanprodan/podinfo pullPolicy: IfNotPresent # Overrides the image tag whose default is the chart appVersion. tag: "6.4.0" replicaCount: 3 service: type: ClusterIP port: 9898 # podinfo listens on 9898
    Debug with Dry-Run:

    This is the most important command in Helm. It generates the YAML without sending it to the cluster.

    helm install debug-release ./mychart --dry-run --debug

    Scroll up the output. Look for the Deployment section. Verify that replicas: 3 and image: stefanprodan/podinfo... are present. If you see them, the template engine is working.

    Install the Custom Chart:
    helm install my-custom-app ./mychart

    Lab 3: Loops, Conditionals, and Ranges

    Objective: Modify your chart templates to support dynamic environment variables using Go Template loops (`range`) and conditional logic (`if`).

    Navigate to your lab working directory:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course/week-09-helm-package-management/lab-03-advanced-templating

    Every file you create in this lab goes into this directory. Stay here for all remaining steps unless a step explicitly tells you to change directory.

    Update values.yaml:

    Add a new section to mychart/values.yaml for custom environment variables.

    # Add this to the bottom of values.yaml env: - name: APP_COLOR value: "blue" - name: APP_MODE value: "production" enableFeatureX: true
    Update Deployment Template:

    Edit mychart/templates/deployment.yaml. Find the containers: section and add the loop logic.

    containers: - name: {{ .Chart.Name }} image: "{{ .Values.image.repository }}:{{ .Values.image.tag | default .Chart.AppVersion }}" # --- ADD THIS BLOCK --- env: {{- range .Values.env }} - name: {{ .name }} value: {{ .value | quote }} {{- end }} {{- if .Values.enableFeatureX }} - name: FEATURE_X value: "enabled" {{- end }} # ----------------------
    Upgrade the Release:

    Apply the changes to your running release.

    helm upgrade my-custom-app ./mychart
    Verify Injection:
    # Get the pod name export POD_NAME=$(kubectl get pods -l app.kubernetes.io/instance=my-custom-app -o jsonpath="{.items[0].metadata.name}") # Check environment variables inside the pod kubectl exec $POD_NAME -- env | grep APP_
    APP_COLOR=blue APP_MODE=production

    Lab 4: Upgrades and Rollbacks

    Objective: Simulate a bad deployment (breaking change) and recover the application state using helm rollback.

    Navigate to your lab working directory:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course/week-09-helm-package-management/lab-04-upgrades-rollbacks

    Every file you create in this lab goes into this directory. Stay here for all remaining steps unless a step explicitly tells you to change directory.

    Check History:
    helm history my-custom-app
    REVISION STATUS DESCRIPTION 1 deployed Install complete 2 deployed Upgrade complete
    Deploy a "Broken" Version:

    Let's upgrade the chart with a non-existent image tag to cause a crash.

    helm upgrade my-custom-app ./mychart --set image.tag="99.99.99-broken"
    Verify Failure:
    kubectl get pods # Status will be ImagePullBackOff or ErrImagePull
    Perform Rollback:

    We need to go back to Revision 2 (the last working state).

    helm rollback my-custom-app 2

    Helm re-applies the manifest exactly as it was in Revision 2. The pods should recover to Running status.

    Lab 5: Managing Dependencies

    Objective: Add a Redis database to your application by defining it as a chart dependency, rather than writing Redis YAML manually.

    Navigate to your lab working directory:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course/week-09-helm-package-management/lab-05-dependencies

    Every file you create in this lab goes into this directory. Stay here for all remaining steps unless a step explicitly tells you to change directory.

    Edit Chart.yaml:

    Add the dependencies block to mychart/Chart.yaml.

    dependencies: - name: redis version: 18.x.x repository: https://charts.bitnami.com/bitnami condition: redis.enabled
    Download Dependencies:
    helm dependency update ./mychart

    You will see a .tgz file appear in the mychart/charts/ directory. This is the downloaded Redis chart.

    Configure Redis in values.yaml:

    Enable the subchart and configure it from the parent values.

    # Add to mychart/values.yaml redis: enabled: true auth: enabled: false # Disable password for lab simplicity architecture: standalone
    Upgrade to Deploy Redis:
    helm upgrade my-custom-app ./mychart
    Verify Architecture:
    kubectl get pods kubectl get svc

    You should now see your my-custom-app pods AND a my-custom-app-redis-master pod. Helm deployed both the app and the database together as a single unit.

    10

    Infrastructure as Code with Terraform

    Provisioning, Managing, and Versioning Cloud Infrastructure

    Welcome to Week 10. For the past 9 weeks, you have operated inside the cluster. You managed Pods, Services, and Ingress rules. But where did the cluster come from? Who created the Virtual Machines, the VPC networks, the Load Balancers, and the Database instances?

    Until now, you might have created these resources manually by clicking buttons in a web console (AWS, Azure, Google Cloud). This is fine for a hobby project, but in a professional environment, manual clicking is forbidden. It is slow, unrepeatable, and prone to human error.

    This week, we introduce Terraform, the industry standard for Infrastructure as Code (IaC). Terraform allows you to define your entire data center in text files (HCL). You can version control your infrastructure (Git), review changes before they happen (Plan), and deploy identical environments (Dev, Stage, Prod) with a single command. By the end of this week, you will stop being a "ClickOps" operator and become a Cloud Engineer.

    Mentorship Guidance

    Mentorship students receive structured support for Infrastructure as Code with Terraform: live Q&A, assignment feedback, and office hours focused on this week's goals.

    Share blockers in Google Classroom and schedule a review session once your lab and assignment work are ready.

    Learning Objectives

    By the end of this week, you will be able to demonstrate mastery across all levels of Bloom's Taxonomy:

    Remember

    Define Core Concepts: Define Infrastructure as Code (IaC), Immutable Infrastructure, Terraform Providers, Resources, Data Sources, and the State File.

    Understand

    Explain Mechanics: Explain the Terraform Workflow (`init` -> `plan` -> `apply`). Describe how the State file maps real-world resources to your configuration and why locking is critical.

    Apply

    Execute Provisioning: Write HCL code to provision an AWS EC2 instance (or Docker container). Use `terraform plan` to predict changes and `terraform destroy` to clean up.

    Analyze

    Diagnose Drift: Analyze the output of `terraform plan` to detect "Drift" (when manual changes in the console conflict with your code). Debug dependency errors in resource creation order.

    Evaluate

    Assess Strategies: Evaluate the benefits of using Remote State (S3 + DynamoDB) vs. Local State. Critique the security risks of committing `.tfstate` files to Git.

    Create

    Build Architecture: Architect a modular Terraform project that deploys a complete VPC network with public/private subnets and a web server, using variables for reusability.

    Pre-Class Exercises

    Mandatory Requirement: Complete these exercises before beginning the teaching notes. Terraform requires credentials to interact with cloud providers. Budget approximately 3-4 hours.

    ⚙ Week 10 Workspace Setup — Complete This First

    Create your working directory for this week inside the course repository. Every pre-class note, lab file, screenshot, and project deliverable belongs under this folder tree — nothing goes anywhere else.

    1. Navigate to your course repository root:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course
    2. Create this week's folder and all sub-directories in one command:
    mkdir -p week-10-infrastructure-as-code/{preclass-notes,lab-01-terraform-basics,lab-02-first-cloud-resource,lab-03-variables-outputs,lab-04-state-management,lab-05-web-server,weekly-project}

    This single command creates the whole week's workspace tree. You will stay within this tree for pre-class work, every lab, and the weekly project.

    3. Verify the structure was created correctly:
    find week-10-infrastructure-as-code -type d | sort

    You should see 7 directories (preclass-notes + 5 lab folders + weekly-project).

    4. Create your pre-class notes file:
    touch week-10-infrastructure-as-code/preclass-notes/week-10-notes.md

    All research, written answers, and reflections for this week's pre-class exercises go into this Markdown file. Keep it open as you work through the exercises below.

    5. Commit the empty scaffold to your GitHub portfolio:
    git add week-10-infrastructure-as-code/ git commit -m "chore(week-10-infrastructure-as-code): scaffold week 10 working directory" git push

    Pushing the empty scaffold creates a timestamped entry on your GitHub profile showing when you started this week — visible evidence for your portfolio and mentorship reviews.

    📁 Week 10 Folder Structure
    ~/devops-engineering-course/ └── week-10-infrastructure-as-code/ ├── preclass-notes/ ├── lab-01-terraform-basics/ ├── lab-02-first-cloud-resource/ ├── lab-03-variables-outputs/ ├── lab-04-state-management/ ├── lab-05-web-server/ └── weekly-project/

    1Install Terraform CLI

    Terraform is a single binary tool.

    # macOS (Homebrew) brew tap hashicorp/tap brew install hashicorp/tap/terraform # Windows (Chocolatey) choco install terraform # Linux # Follow instructions at: https://developer.hashicorp.com/terraform/downloads

    Verification: Run terraform version. Ensure you are on v1.5.0 or higher.

    2Prepare a Cloud Provider

    Terraform needs a target. Ideally, we will use AWS Free Tier. If you cannot use AWS, we will use the Docker Provider as a local fallback.

    Option A: AWS (Recommended)

    • Create an AWS Free Tier account.
    • Create an IAM User with AdministratorAccess (for learning only).
    • Generate Access Keys (Access Key ID and Secret Access Key).
    • Install AWS CLI and run aws configure to save these credentials.

    Option B: Docker (Fallback)

    • Ensure Docker Desktop is running.
    • We will use the kreuzwerker/docker provider in the labs to create containers instead of VMs.

    3Declarative vs. Imperative

    Research Task: Write a short paragraph explaining the difference between:
    1. Imperative (Ansible/Bash): "Make a sandwich. Step 1: Get bread. Step 2: Get cheese..."
    2. Declarative (Terraform/Kubernetes): "I want a cheese sandwich."

    Why is Declarative safer when you run the script a second time? (Hint: Idempotency).

    4The "State" File Mystery

    Terraform creates a file called terraform.tfstate.

    Task: Read the HashiCorp documentation on "State". Answer this: If you manually delete an EC2 instance in the AWS Console, but the terraform.tfstate file still thinks it exists, what will happen when you run terraform plan?

    5Install VS Code Extension

    Writing HCL (HashiCorp Configuration Language) is hard without syntax highlighting.

    Task: Install the official "HashiCorp Terraform" extension for VS Code. It provides autocompletion for AWS resources, which is a lifesaver.

    Detailed Teaching Notes

    Infrastructure as Code (IaC) is the practice of managing and provisioning computer data centers through machine-readable definition files, rather than physical hardware configuration or interactive configuration tools.

    Terraform by HashiCorp is the dominant tool in this space. Unlike scripts (Bash/Python) which describe how to build something ("run this command, then that command"), Terraform allows you to describe what you want ("I want three servers"). Terraform figures out the "how" for you.

    10.1 The HCL Language (HashiCorp Configuration Language)

    Terraform code is written in HCL. It is designed to be human-readable. A standard project is composed of several blocks.

    Anatomy of a Resource Block

    # Best Practice: Use a data source to get the latest AMI dynamically
    data "aws_ami" "ubuntu" {
      most_recent = true
      owners = ["099720109477"]
      filter {
        name = "name"
        values = ["ubuntu/images/hvm-ssd/ubuntu-jammy-22.04-amd64-server-*"]
      }
    }

    resource "aws_instance" "web_server" {
      ami = data.aws_ami.ubuntu.id
      instance_type = "t3.micro"
      tags = { Name = "MyFirstServer" }
    }
    • Block Type (`resource`): Tells Terraform we want to create something.
    • Provider Type (`aws_instance`): The specific resource type defined by the AWS Provider.
    • Local Name (`web_server`): How you refer to this resource inside your Terraform code (e.g., to link a load balancer to it). This name is NOT known to AWS.
    • Arguments: The configuration (AMI, Size, Tags).

    10.2 The Core Workflow

    Terraform has a strict, repeatable workflow that prevents errors. You will repeat this cycle thousands of times in your career.

    1

    terraform init

    Initialization. Terraform scans your code to see which Providers you are using (e.g., AWS, Docker). It downloads the plugin binaries into a hidden `.terraform` folder. You run this once per project.

    2

    terraform plan

    The Dry Run. Terraform compares your code (Desired State) against the Real World (Current State). It calculates the "Diff" and tells you exactly what it would do if you applied.
    Output: "Plan: 1 to add, 0 to change, 0 to destroy."

    3

    terraform apply

    Execution. Terraform calls the cloud APIs to create/update/delete resources to match the Plan. It then updates its State File.

    10.3 The State File (The "Brain")

    This is the most critical concept to understand. Terraform maintains a file called terraform.tfstate.

    The Problem: You declare `resource "aws_instance" "web"`. Terraform creates an EC2 instance with ID `i-012345abcdef`. How does Terraform remember that `web` = `i-012345abcdef` the next time you run it?
    The Solution: The State File maps your local code names to real-world cloud IDs.

    ⚠️ The Golden Rule of State

    NEVER edit the state file manually. Also, never touch resources manually in the AWS Console if they are managed by Terraform. This causes Drift.

    If you delete a server in the AWS Console, Terraform's state file still thinks it exists. When you run `terraform plan`, Terraform will see it's missing and attempt to recreate it (Healing).

    10.4 Variables and Outputs

    Hardcoding values (like AMIs or Regions) makes code brittle. We use Input Variables to make modules reusable and Output Values to display important info (like IP addresses) after deployment.

    # variables.tf variable "region" { description = "AWS Region" default = "us-east-1" type = string } # main.tf provider "aws" { region = var.region } # outputs.tf output "server_ip" { value = aws_instance.web.public_ip }

    10.5 Data Sources (Read-Only)

    Sometimes you need to configure your infrastructure based on resources that already exist (and aren't managed by you), or look up dynamic information (like the latest Ubuntu AMI ID).

    A Data Source is a read-only query to the provider.

    # Ask AWS for the default VPC ID data "aws_vpc" "default" { default = true } # Use that ID in a resource resource "aws_security_group" "sg" { name = "allow_http" vpc_id = data.aws_vpc.default.id <-- Dynamic Reference }

    10.6 Remote State & Locking

    When working alone, a local `terraform.tfstate` file is fine. In a team, it is disastrous.

    • Consistency: If Developer A applies changes, Developer B's local state file is now outdated.
    • Security: The state file stores sensitive data (like database passwords) in plain text. You should not commit this to GitHub.
    • Race Conditions: What if Alice and Bob run `terraform apply` at the exact same time?

    The Solution: Remote Backends. We configure Terraform to store the state file in a shared location (like an AWS S3 Bucket) and use a database (like DynamoDB) to create a "Lock". When Alice runs `apply`, Terraform locks the table. If Bob tries to run `apply`, Terraform says "Error: State is locked by Alice."

    Hands-On Labs

    The only way to learn Terraform is to destroy infrastructure (intentionally). In these labs, you will start by provisioning a local Docker container to master the workflow without cloud costs. Then, you will graduate to AWS to build a real web server with security groups and dynamic IP addressing. Finally, you will simulate a "disaster" and watch Terraform heal the infrastructure.

    ⚠️ Prerequisites

    1. Terraform Installed: Verify with terraform version.
    2. AWS Credentials: (For Labs 2-5) Ensure aws configure is run and you have a profile with Admin permissions.
    3. Docker Running: (For Lab 1) Ensure Docker Desktop is active.
    4. VS Code Extension: Ensure "HashiCorp Terraform" is installed for syntax highlighting.

    Lab 1: The "Hello World" of IaC

    Objective: Learn the core lifecycle (`init`, `plan`, `apply`, `destroy`) using a safe, local resource (a Docker container) before touching the cloud.

    Navigate to your lab working directory:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course/week-10-infrastructure-as-code/lab-01-terraform-basics

    Every file you create in this lab goes into this directory. Stay here for all remaining steps unless a step explicitly tells you to change directory.

    Setup Workspace:
    mkdir terraform-labs cd terraform-labs mkdir lab1-docker cd lab1-docker touch main.tf
    Write the Configuration:

    Open main.tf and define the Docker provider and an Nginx container resource.

    terraform { required_providers { docker = { source = "kreuzwerker/docker" version = "~> 3.0.1" } } } provider "docker" {} resource "docker_image" "nginx" { name = "nginx:latest" keep_locally = false } resource "docker_container" "nginx" { image = docker_image.nginx.image_id name = "tutorial" ports { internal = 80 external = 8000 } }
    Step 1: Init:

    Initialize the project. This downloads the kreuzwerker/docker plugin.

    terraform init

    Success looks like: "Terraform has been successfully initialized!"

    Step 2: Plan:
    terraform plan
    Plan: 2 to add, 0 to change, 0 to destroy.

    Terraform calculates it needs to download the Image AND start the Container.

    Step 3: Apply:
    terraform apply # Type 'yes' when prompted
    Verify:

    Open your browser to http://localhost:8000. You should see the Nginx welcome page. The container is running!

    docker ps # You will see a container named "tutorial"
    Step 4: Destroy:

    Clean up your workspace.

    terraform destroy # Type 'yes'

    Lab 2: Your First Cloud Resource

    Objective: Provision a real virtual machine on AWS. Learn how to map a local resource block to a cloud instance.

    Navigate to your lab working directory:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course/week-10-infrastructure-as-code/lab-02-first-cloud-resource

    Every file you create in this lab goes into this directory. Stay here for all remaining steps unless a step explicitly tells you to change directory.

    Setup Workspace:
    cd .. mkdir lab2-aws cd lab2-aws touch main.tf
    Write the Provider Block:

    Tell Terraform we are talking to AWS in the us-east-1 region (N. Virginia).

    provider "aws" { region = "us-east-1" }
    Define the EC2 Instance (main.tf):

    Use a data source to fetch the latest Ubuntu AMI dynamically. This is more maintainable than hardcoding AMI IDs.

    # First, define the data source
    data "aws_ami" "ubuntu" {
      most_recent = true
      owners = ["099720109477"] # Canonical
      filter {
        name = "name"
        values = ["ubuntu/images/hvm-ssd/ubuntu-jammy-22.04-amd64-server-*"]
      }
      filter {
        name = "virtualization-type"
        values = ["hvm"]
      }
    }

    # Then, create the instance using the dynamic AMI
    resource "aws_instance" "web_server" {
      ami = data.aws_ami.ubuntu.id
      instance_type = "t3.micro"
      tags = { Name = "Terraform-Lab-2" }
    }
    Apply:
    terraform init terraform apply -auto-approve
    💡 -auto-approve

    This flag skips the interactive "yes" prompt. Use carefully in production!

    Verify in Console:

    Log into the AWS Console > EC2. You should see an instance named "Terraform-Lab-2" initializing.

    Lab 3: Smarter Infrastructure

    Objective: Remove hardcoded values. Use Data Sources to dynamically find the latest AMI and Outputs to print the public IP address.

    Navigate to your lab working directory:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course/week-10-infrastructure-as-code/lab-03-variables-outputs

    Every file you create in this lab goes into this directory. Stay here for all remaining steps unless a step explicitly tells you to change directory.

    Create variables.tf:

    Define variables to make the code reusable.

    variable "instance_name" { description = "Value of the Name tag for the EC2 instance" type = string default = "My-Dynamic-Server" }
    File: variables.tf
    variable "instance_name" {
      description = "Name tag for the EC2 instance"
      type = string
      default = "My-Dynamic-Server"
    }
    File: main.tf (Use Data Source):

    Replace the hardcoded AMI with a dynamic lookup.

    provider "aws" {
      region = "us-east-1"
    }

    # Query AWS for the latest Ubuntu 22.04 AMI
    data "aws_ami" "ubuntu" {
      most_recent = true
      filter {
        name = "name"
        values = ["ubuntu/images/hvm-ssd/ubuntu-jammy-22.04-amd64-server-*"]
      }
      filter {
        name = "virtualization-type"
        values = ["hvm"]
      }
      owners = ["099720109477"] # Canonical (Ubuntu Creator)
    }

    # Create the instance using the dynamic AMI
    resource "aws_instance" "web" {
      ami = data.aws_ami.ubuntu.id
      instance_type = "t3.micro"
      tags = {
        Name = var.instance_name
      }
    }
    File: outputs.tf

    Tell Terraform to print the IP address after creation.

    output "instance_id" {
      description = "ID of the EC2 instance"
      value = aws_instance.web.id
    }

    output "public_ip" {
      description = "Public IP address of the EC2 instance"
      value = aws_instance.web.public_ip
    }
    Apply with Variable Override:
    terraform apply -var="instance_name=Production-Server"
    Apply complete! Resources: 1 added, 0 changed, 1 destroyed. Outputs: instance_id = "i-0123456789abcdef0" public_ip = "54.1.2.3"

    Terraform destroyed the old instance (because we changed the TF configuration structure significantly or renamed resources in state) and created a new one with the latest AMI.

    Lab 4: Detecting Drift & Healing

    Objective: Manually sabotage the infrastructure to see how Terraform reacts. This demonstrates the "Healing" capability of Declarative IaC.

    Navigate to your lab working directory:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course/week-10-infrastructure-as-code/lab-04-state-management

    Every file you create in this lab goes into this directory. Stay here for all remaining steps unless a step explicitly tells you to change directory.

    The Sabotage:

    1. Go to the AWS Console.
    2. Find your "Production-Server".
    3. Terminate it manually.
    4. Wait for the state to show "Terminated".

    ⚠️ The Reality

    In a script (Imperative), if you ran "create server", it would blindly create a second server. Terraform is smarter.

    Run Plan (Drift Detection):
    terraform plan
    Terraform used the selected providers to generate the following execution plan. Resource actions are indicated with the following symbols: + create Terraform will perform the following actions: # aws_instance.web will be created + resource "aws_instance" "web" { ... } Plan: 1 to add, 0 to change, 0 to destroy.

    Analysis: Terraform refreshed its state (API lookup), realized the instance ID in its state file no longer exists in AWS, and decided the only way to match your desired state (`resource "aws_instance"`) is to create a new one.

    The Healing:
    terraform apply -auto-approve

    Your infrastructure is restored to the correct state.

    Lab 5: Building a Web Server with Security Groups

    Objective: Deploy a web server that is actually accessible over the internet by configuring Security Groups (Firewalls) and User Data (Startup Scripts).

    Navigate to your lab working directory:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course/week-10-infrastructure-as-code/lab-05-web-server

    Every file you create in this lab goes into this directory. Stay here for all remaining steps unless a step explicitly tells you to change directory.

    File: security.tf

    Define a security group allowing HTTP (80) and SSH (22).

    resource "aws_security_group" "web_sg" {
      name = "allow_web_traffic"
      description = "Allow Web inbound traffic"

      ingress {
        description = "HTTP"
        from_port = 80
        to_port = 80
        protocol = "tcp"
        cidr_blocks = ["0.0.0.0/0"]
      }

      ingress {
        description = "SSH"
        from_port = 22
        to_port = 22
        protocol = "tcp"
        cidr_blocks = ["0.0.0.0/0"]
      }

      egress {
        description = "Allow all outbound"
        from_port = 0
        to_port = 0
        protocol = "-1"
        cidr_blocks = ["0.0.0.0/0"]
      }
    }
    File: main.tf (EC2 Instance with Dynamic AMI and User Data)

    Define the data source for the latest Ubuntu AMI and configure the instance.

    # Query AWS for the latest Ubuntu 22.04 LTS AMI
    data "aws_ami" "ubuntu" {
      most_recent = true
      filter {
        name = "name"
        values = ["ubuntu/images/hvm-ssd/ubuntu-jammy-22.04-amd64-server-*"]
      }
      filter {
        name = "virtualization-type"
        values = ["hvm"]
      }
      owners = ["099720109477"] # Canonical
    }

    resource "aws_instance" "web" {
      ami = data.aws_ami.ubuntu.id
      instance_type = "t3.micro"
      vpc_security_group_ids = [aws_security_group.web_sg.id]

      user_data = <<-EOF
    #!/bin/bash
    sudo apt-get update
    sudo apt-get install -y apache2
    echo "<h1>Deployed via Terraform</h1>" | sudo tee /var/www/html/index.html
    sudo systemctl start apache2
    sudo systemctl enable apache2
    EOF

      tags = {
        Name = "Terraform-Web-Server"
      }
    }
    Apply and Test:
    terraform apply -auto-approve

    Wait 2 minutes for the instance to boot and run the script.
    Copy the public_ip from the outputs.
    Paste it into your browser: http://54.x.x.x

    You should see "Deployed via Terraform".

    Final Cleanup (Crucial):

    Do not skip this. You will be billed if you leave resources running.

    terraform destroy -auto-approve
    11

    Advanced Terraform & Cloud Provisioning

    Modules, State Manipulation, and Production Architectures

    Welcome to Week 11. In Week 10, you learned the vocabulary of Terraform: Resources, Variables, and the basic Plan/Apply workflow. You built a single server. But in the real world, "Infrastructure" is rarely a single server. It is a complex web of Load Balancers, Auto Scaling Groups, Databases, and Networks, replicated across multiple environments (Dev, Staging, Production).

    This week, we move from "writing scripts" to "architecting systems." We will stop copying and pasting code and start building reusable Modules. We will tackle the hardest problems in Terraform: State Management (Remote Backends, Locking, and Importing existing resources), Control Logic (Loops, Conditionals, and Dynamic Blocks), and Refactoring live infrastructure without downtime. By the end of this week, you will be able to provision a complete, highly available 3-tier architecture with a single command.

    Mentorship Guidance

    Mentorship students receive structured support for Advanced Terraform & Cloud Provisioning: live Q&A, assignment feedback, and office hours focused on this week's goals.

    Share blockers in Google Classroom and schedule a review session once your lab and assignment work are ready.

    Learning Objectives

    By the end of this week, you will be able to demonstrate mastery across all levels of Bloom's Taxonomy:

    Remember

    Define Constructs: Define the syntax for `for_each`, `count`, and `dynamic` blocks. Identify the components required for a secure Remote Backend (State Storage + State Locking).

    Understand

    Explain Mechanics: Explain the difference between `local` values and `variables`. Describe how `terraform import` reconciles existing cloud resources into the state file.

    Apply

    Execute Refactoring: Refactor a monolithic `main.tf` into a modular directory structure. Use `for_each` to provision multiple subnets dynamically from a map variable.

    Analyze

    Diagnose State Issues: Analyze a "State Lock" error and resolve it safely. Debug a "Circular Dependency" error between two modules.

    Evaluate

    Assess Strategies: Evaluate the trade-offs between Terraform Workspaces vs. Directory-based environment separation. Critique the use of `provisioner "local-exec"` versus using `user_data`.

    Create

    Build Architecture: Architect a reusable "Web Server Cluster" module that includes an Application Load Balancer (ALB), Target Groups, and Auto Scaling Groups (ASG), parameterized for any environment.

    Pre-Class Exercises

    Mandatory Requirement: Complete these exercises before beginning the teaching notes. Advanced Terraform requires setting up cloud storage for state and understanding control flow logic. Budget approximately 2-3 hours.

    ⚙ Week 11 Workspace Setup — Complete This First

    Create your working directory for this week inside the course repository. Every pre-class note, lab file, screenshot, and project deliverable belongs under this folder tree — nothing goes anywhere else.

    1. Navigate to your course repository root:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course
    2. Create this week's folder and all sub-directories in one command:
    mkdir -p week-11-advanced-terraform/{preclass-notes,lab-01-modules,lab-02-remote-backend,lab-03-advanced-expressions,lab-04-brownfield-import,weekly-project}

    This single command creates the whole week's workspace tree. You will stay within this tree for pre-class work, every lab, and the weekly project.

    3. Verify the structure was created correctly:
    find week-11-advanced-terraform -type d | sort

    You should see 6 directories (preclass-notes + 4 lab folders + weekly-project).

    4. Create your pre-class notes file:
    touch week-11-advanced-terraform/preclass-notes/week-11-notes.md

    All research, written answers, and reflections for this week's pre-class exercises go into this Markdown file. Keep it open as you work through the exercises below.

    5. Commit the empty scaffold to your GitHub portfolio:
    git add week-11-advanced-terraform/ git commit -m "chore(week-11-advanced-terraform): scaffold week 11 working directory" git push

    Pushing the empty scaffold creates a timestamped entry on your GitHub profile showing when you started this week — visible evidence for your portfolio and mentorship reviews.

    📁 Week 11 Folder Structure
    ~/devops-engineering-course/ └── week-11-advanced-terraform/ ├── preclass-notes/ ├── lab-01-modules/ ├── lab-02-remote-backend/ ├── lab-03-advanced-expressions/ ├── lab-04-brownfield-import/ └── weekly-project/

    1The "Chicken and Egg" Backend

    To use Terraform in a team, you need a Remote Backend (S3 + DynamoDB). You cannot easily use Terraform to manage the resources that hold Terraform's state (it creates a circular dependency). You must create them manually or via script.

    Task: Run these AWS CLI commands (or perform the equivalent in the AWS Console):

    # 1. Create S3 Bucket (Must be globally unique name) aws s3api create-bucket --bucket my-advanced-tf-state-[YOURNAME] --region us-east-1 # 2. Enable Versioning (CRITICAL for state recovery) aws s3api put-bucket-versioning --bucket my-advanced-tf-state-[YOURNAME] --versioning-configuration Status=Enabled # 3. Create DynamoDB Table for Locking aws dynamodb create-table \ --table-name terraform-locks \ --attribute-definitions AttributeName=LockID,AttributeType=S \ --key-schema AttributeName=LockID,KeyType=HASH \ --provisioned-throughput ReadCapacityUnits=1,WriteCapacityUnits=1
    ⚠️ Why Versioning?

    If your state file gets corrupted, S3 Versioning allows you to "Undo" and roll back to the previous state file. Without this, a corrupted state file can require a total infrastructure rebuild.

    2The "Import" Concept

    Scenario: Your boss manually created an S3 bucket named `legacy-data-bucket` via the AWS Console 3 years ago. They now want you to manage it with Terraform.

    Task: Create a dummy S3 bucket manually in the AWS Console. Then, research the `terraform import` command. Write down the syntax required to bring this existing bucket into a Terraform configuration without deleting and recreating it.

    3Logic Primer: Count vs. For_Each

    Terraform isn't a standard programming language, but it has loops.

    Research Task: Read the HashiCorp documentation on `count` and `for_each`.
    Scenario: You have a list of subnets: `["subnet-a", "subnet-b", "subnet-c"]`.
    Question: If you use `count` to create resources, what happens if you remove `subnet-b` from the middle of the list? (Hint: The index shifts). Why is `for_each` safer for lists that might change?

    4Install `tfenv` (Recommended)

    In professional environments, different projects use different versions of Terraform. Managing binaries manually is painful.

    Task: Install a Terraform version manager.

    # MacOS brew install tfenv # Linux git clone https://github.com/tfutils/tfenv.git ~/.tfenv echo 'export PATH="$HOME/.tfenv/bin:$PATH"' >> ~/.bashrc

    Run tfenv install 1.9.0 and tfenv use 1.9.0 to standardize your version for this week.

    Detailed Teaching Notes

    Moving from basic Terraform to advanced usage requires a shift in mindset. You are no longer writing a linear script; you are building a software application where the "compiler" is the Terraform Core and the "executable" is your Cloud Infrastructure.

    This section covers the four pillars of Advanced Terraform: Modularization (encapsulation), State Management (team safety), Control Logic (dynamic resource generation), and Brownfield Adoption (importing existing resources).

    11.1 The Module Architecture

    A Module is a container for multiple resources that are used together. Modules allow you to create abstractions. Instead of defining 5 resources to build a VPC (subnet, route table, gateway, association, vpc), you can define a single `module` block that handles it all.

    📦 Anatomy of a Module

    Every directory with `.tf` files is a module.
    1. Root Module: The directory where you run `terraform apply`.
    2. Child Module: A module called by another module.

    Data Flow:
    - Inputs: Data flows down into the child module via `variables`.
    - Outputs: Data flows up to the root module via `outputs`.

    # Directory Structure
    root/
    ├── main.tf # Calls the module
    └── modules/
        └── web_server/ # The Child Module
            ├── main.tf # Defines aws_instance, aws_security_group
            ├── variables.tf # Defines input: instance_type, ami_id
            └── outputs.tf # Defines output: public_ip

    # Calling the Module (in root/main.tf)
    module "frontend" {
      source = "./modules/web_server"
      instance_type = "t3.micro"
      ami_id = data.aws_ami.ubuntu.id
    }

    # Accessing the Output
    output "app_url" {
      value = module.frontend.public_ip
    }

    11.2 Remote State & Collaboration

    In Week 10, your state file (`terraform.tfstate`) lived on your laptop. In a team, this is disastrous. If your laptop dies, the state is lost. If your colleague applies changes, your state is outdated.

    The Solution: Remote Backends. We move the state file to a shared, durable storage location (AWS S3) and use a database (DynamoDB) to manage State Locking.

    The Locking Workflow

    1. Developer A runs `terraform apply`.
    2. Terraform calculates a checksum and writes a "Lock" item to the DynamoDB table.
    3. Developer B runs `terraform apply` at the same time.
    4. Terraform checks DynamoDB, sees the Lock, and aborts with:
      Error: State blob is already locked by Developer A.
    5. Developer A finishes. Terraform removes the Lock.
    6. Developer B can now proceed.

    11.3 Control Logic: Loops & Dynamics

    Terraform is declarative, but sometimes you need procedural logic (loops).

    1. Count vs. For_Each

    Both create multiple instances of a resource, but `for_each` is safer.

    🔢

    Count

    Uses an Array Index (0, 1, 2).
    resource "aws_instance" "web" { count = 3 }
    Risk: If you delete item #1, item #2 becomes #1. Terraform will destroy and recreate the wrong servers to match the new index order.

    🔠

    For_Each

    Uses a Map Key ("web", "api", "db").
    for_each = toset(["web", "api"])
    Benefit: Resources are identified by unique string keys. Adding/removing items does not shift the others.

    2. Dynamic Blocks

    Sometimes you need to loop inside a resource (e.g., creating multiple Ingress rules in a Security Group).

    resource "aws_security_group" "sg" { name = "dynamic-sg" # Loop over a list of ports dynamic "ingress" { for_each = [80, 443, 8080] content { from_port = ingress.value to_port = ingress.value protocol = "tcp" cidr_blocks = ["0.0.0.0/0"] } } }

    11.4 Dealing with Brownfield (Importing)

    Rarely do you start with a blank slate. Usually, there is existing infrastructure created manually. Terraform allows you to adopt these resources using `terraform import`.

    ⚠️ Import is Two Steps

    Running `terraform import` only updates the state file. It does not generate the configuration code (`main.tf`). You must write the `resource` block manually to match the real-world resource, or Terraform will try to destroy it on the next run.

    11.5 Managing Environments

    How do you deploy to Dev, Staging, and Prod using the same code?

    Approach A: Workspaces (The Terraform Native Way)
    CLI commands switch the backend state file.
    terraform workspace new dev -> `s3://bucket/env:/dev/tfstate`
    Pros: Easy setup. Cons: Easy to make mistakes (apply to prod while thinking you are in dev).

    Approach B: Directory Structure (The Industry Standard)
    Separate folders for each environment, referencing the same modules.
    /envs/dev/main.tf -> `module "app" { source = "../../modules/app" }`
    /envs/prod/main.tf -> `module "app" { source = "../../modules/app" }`
    Pros: Explicit separation. Cons: More boilerplate code.

    Hands-On Labs

    In these labs, you will perform the tasks of a Senior DevOps Engineer. You will not just "create infrastructure"; you will refactor existing code, secure state files against corruption, automate repetitive tasks with loops, and reconcile legacy infrastructure that wasn't created by Terraform.

    ⚠️ Prerequisites

    1. AWS Credentials: Ensure your terminal is authenticated (`aws configure` or env vars).
    2. Pre-Class Setup: You MUST have the S3 Bucket and DynamoDB Table created from the Pre-Class exercises.
    3. Workspace: Create a new directory `week11-labs` and cd into it.

    Lab 1: The "Module Refactor"

    Objective: Take a monolithic `main.tf` and split it into a reusable `web_server` module.

    Navigate to your lab working directory:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course/week-11-advanced-terraform/lab-01-modules

    Every file you create in this lab goes into this directory. Stay here for all remaining steps unless a step explicitly tells you to change directory.

    Create Directory Structure:
    mkdir -p lab1/modules/web_server cd lab1 touch main.tf touch modules/web_server/{main.tf,variables.tf,outputs.tf}
    Step 1: Define the Child Module:

    We are creating a template for a standard web server.

    modules/web_server/variables.tf

    variable "server_name" { type = string } variable "instance_type" { type = string }

    modules/web_server/main.tf

    data "aws_ami" "amazon_linux" { most_recent = true owners = ["amazon"] filter { name = "name" values = ["al2023-ami-2023.*-x86_64"] } } resource "aws_instance" "web" { ami = data.aws_ami.amazon_linux.id instance_type = var.instance_type tags = { Name = var.server_name } }

    modules/web_server/outputs.tf

    output "public_ip" { value = aws_instance.web.public_ip }
    Step 2: Define the Root Module:

    Now, use the module to create two servers in lab1/main.tf.

    provider "aws" { region = "us-east-1" }

    module "marketing_server" {
      source = "./modules/web_server"
      server_name = "marketing-prod"
      instance_type = "t3.micro"
    }

    module "finance_server" {
      source = "./modules/web_server"
      server_name = "finance-internal"
      instance_type = "t3.micro"
    }

    output "marketing_ip" {
      value = module.marketing_server.public_ip
    }
    Step 3: Init and Apply:
    terraform init # Note: "Initializing modules..." terraform apply -auto-approve

    Terraform creates 2 instances. Notice how you didn't have to duplicate the AMI lookup logic.

    Lab 2: Migrating to Remote Backend

    Objective: Move your local state file to S3 and verify that DynamoDB locking prevents concurrent modifications.

    Navigate to your lab working directory:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course/week-11-advanced-terraform/lab-02-remote-backend

    Every file you create in this lab goes into this directory. Stay here for all remaining steps unless a step explicitly tells you to change directory.

    Configure the Backend:

    Add this block to lab1/main.tf. Replace the bucket name with the one you created in Pre-Class exercises.

    terraform { backend "s3" { bucket = "my-advanced-tf-state-[YOURNAME]" # UPDATE THIS key = "lab1/terraform.tfstate" region = "us-east-1" dynamodb_table = "terraform-locks" encrypt = true } }
    Migrate State:
    terraform init
    Do you want to copy existing state to the new backend? Enter a value: yes

    Terraform uploads your local `terraform.tfstate` to S3. You can now delete the local file.

    Verify Locking (Simulation):

    We will simulate a collision.

    # Open Terminal Tab 1: terraform apply # (Wait at the "Enter a value" prompt. Do NOT type yes.) # Open Terminal Tab 2 (same directory): terraform plan
    Error: State blob is already locked Lock Info: ID: ...-uuid-... Operation: OperationTypeApply Who: user@hostname

    Result: Terraform protected you from corrupting the state.

    Cleanup:

    Cancel the apply in Tab 1 (`Ctrl+C`). Run terraform destroy -auto-approve.

    Lab 3: Advanced Control Logic

    Objective: Use `for_each` to create multiple IAM users and `dynamic` blocks to generate security group rules programmatically.

    Navigate to your lab working directory:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course/week-11-advanced-terraform/lab-03-advanced-expressions

    Every file you create in this lab goes into this directory. Stay here for all remaining steps unless a step explicitly tells you to change directory.

    Setup:
    mkdir ../lab3 cd ../lab3 touch main.tf
    The `for_each` User Loop:

    Create main.tf. We will create 3 IAM users from a Map.

    provider "aws" { region = "us-east-1" } locals { users = { "alice" = "developer" "bob" = "manager" "charlie" = "intern" } } resource "aws_iam_user" "staff" { for_each = local.users name = each.key tags = { Role = each.value } }
    The `dynamic` Security Group:

    Append this to main.tf. We iterate over a list of ports to create ingress rules.

    variable "ingress_ports" { type = list(number) default = [80, 443, 8080, 22] } resource "aws_security_group" "web" { name = "dynamic-sg" dynamic "ingress" { for_each = var.ingress_ports content { from_port = ingress.value to_port = ingress.value protocol = "tcp" cidr_blocks = ["0.0.0.0/0"] } } }
    Apply and Inspect:
    terraform init && terraform apply -auto-approve

    Go to the AWS Console > IAM. You will see Alice, Bob, and Charlie. Go to EC2 > Security Groups. You will see "dynamic-sg" with 4 inbound rules.

    Cleanup:
    terraform destroy -auto-approve

    Lab 4: Importing "Brownfield" Infrastructure

    Objective: Simulate a "Legacy" resource created manually, and bring it under Terraform control without deleting/recreating it.

    Navigate to your lab working directory:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course/week-11-advanced-terraform/lab-04-brownfield-import

    Every file you create in this lab goes into this directory. Stay here for all remaining steps unless a step explicitly tells you to change directory.

    Create a "Legacy" Resource:

    Use the CLI to create a Security Group manually (simulating a ClickOps engineer).

    aws ec2 create-security-group \ --group-name legacy-sg \ --description "Created manually" \ --region us-east-1
    { "GroupId": "sg-0123456789abcdef0" }

    Copy that GroupId. You will need it.

    Write the Terraform Config:

    Create `../lab4/main.tf`. We must define a resource block that matches the existing resource.

    # ../lab4/main.tf provider "aws" { region = "us-east-1" } resource "aws_security_group" "legacy" { name = "legacy-sg" description = "Created manually" # Note: We haven't imported it yet, so Terraform thinks this is new. }
    The Import Command:
    cd ../lab4 terraform init # Syntax: terraform import
    terraform import aws_security_group.legacy sg-0123456789abcdef0
    Import successful!
    Verify Sync (Plan):
    terraform plan
    No changes. Your infrastructure matches the configuration.
    💡 What just happened?

    Terraform pulled the real-world configuration of `sg-xxxx` and wrote it into `terraform.tfstate`. It then compared `main.tf` to the state. Since the names matched, it reported "No changes". You have successfully adopted the resource.

    Cleanup:
    terraform destroy -auto-approve
    13

    Monitoring, Logging & Observability

    Moving from "It runs" to "It works"

    Welcome to the Advanced Track. In the previous 12 weeks, your focus was on Day 0 (Design) and Day 1 (Deployment). Now we enter Day 2: Operations.

    Deployment is easy; keeping things running is hard. When a user reports "The site is slow," how do you know if it's the database, the network, or a code bug? If a pod crashes at 3 AM, do you have the logs?

    This week, we implement the "Three Pillars of Observability": Metrics, Logs, and Traces. We will deploy the industry-standard stack (Prometheus and Grafana) to visualize cluster health, and we will learn the difference between "Monitoring" (telling you the system is down) and "Observability" (telling you why).

    Mentorship Guidance

    Mentorship students receive structured support for Monitoring, Logging & Observability: live Q&A, assignment feedback, and office hours focused on this week's goals.

    Share blockers in Google Classroom and schedule a review session once your lab and assignment work are ready.

    Learning Objectives

    By the end of this week, you will be able to demonstrate mastery across all levels of Bloom's Taxonomy:

    Remember

    Define the Pillars: Define Metrics (aggregatable numbers), Logs (discrete events), and Traces (request lifecycle). Recall the "Four Golden Signals" (Latency, Traffic, Errors, Saturation).

    Understand

    Explain Architecture: Explain the Pull-based architecture of Prometheus (scraping targets) versus Push-based systems. Describe how AlertManager groups and routes alerts.

    Apply

    Execute Installation: Deploy the kube-prometheus-stack using Helm. Configure a Grafana dashboard to visualize Pod memory usage.

    Analyze

    Diagnose Performance: Analyze a spike in 99th percentile latency on a Grafana graph and correlate it with CPU throttling metrics.

    Evaluate

    Assess Tooling: Evaluate the trade-offs between self-hosted logging (ELK/Loki) vs. SaaS (Datadog/Splunk) regarding cost, maintenance, and data sovereignty.

    Create

    Build Alerts: Construct a Prometheus alerting rule that triggers PagerDuty only when error rates exceed 5% for more than 5 minutes (preventing flapping).

    Pre-Class Exercises

    Mandatory Requirement: Complete these exercises before beginning the teaching notes. This week requires a Kubernetes cluster with significant resources (Observability stacks are heavy). Budget approximately 1 hour.

    ⚙ Week 13 Workspace Setup — Complete This First

    Create your working directory for this week inside the course repository. Every pre-class note, lab file, screenshot, and project deliverable belongs under this folder tree — nothing goes anywhere else.

    1. Navigate to your course repository root:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course
    2. Create this week's folder and all sub-directories in one command:
    mkdir -p week-13-monitoring-logging/{preclass-notes,lab-01-observability-stack,lab-02-grafana-dashboards,lab-03-promql,lab-04-alerting,weekly-project}

    This single command creates the whole week's workspace tree. You will stay within this tree for pre-class work, every lab, and the weekly project.

    3. Verify the structure was created correctly:
    find week-13-monitoring-logging -type d | sort

    You should see 6 directories (preclass-notes + 4 lab folders + weekly-project).

    4. Create your pre-class notes file:
    touch week-13-monitoring-logging/preclass-notes/week-13-notes.md
