    All research, written answers, and reflections for this week's pre-class exercises go into this Markdown file. Keep it open as you work through the exercises below.

    5. Commit the empty scaffold to your GitHub portfolio:
    git add week-13-monitoring-logging/ git commit -m "chore(week-13-monitoring-logging): scaffold week 13 working directory" git push

    Pushing the empty scaffold creates a timestamped entry on your GitHub profile showing when you started this week — visible evidence for your portfolio and mentorship reviews.

    📁 Week 13 Folder Structure
    ~/devops-engineering-course/ └── week-13-monitoring-logging/ ├── preclass-notes/ ├── lab-01-observability-stack/ ├── lab-02-grafana-dashboards/ ├── lab-03-promql/ ├── lab-04-alerting/ └── weekly-project/

    1Cluster Sizing

    Prometheus and Grafana require more memory than a standard Nginx pod.

    Task: Ensure your local cluster (Minikube/Docker) has at least 2 CPUs and 4GB RAM.

    # Minikube users: minikube delete minikube start --cpus 2 --memory 4096

    2Install Helm (Refresher)

    We will use the community-maintained `kube-prometheus-stack` Helm chart. It is too complex to install via raw YAML.

    helm version # If not installed, please review Week 9. # Add the Prometheus Repo helm repo add prometheus-community https://prometheus-community.github.io/helm-charts helm repo update

    3Research: The 4 Golden Signals

    Task: Read the Google SRE Book chapter on "Monitoring Distributed Systems" (available online for free).
    Write down: What are the 4 Golden Signals? Why is "Saturation" different from "Traffic"?

    4Port Forwarding Primer

    Grafana runs inside the cluster. To view the dashboard, we will need to forward ports.

    Command Refresher: kubectl port-forward svc/service-name local_port:container_port.

    Detailed Teaching Notes

    In the modern cloud-native ecosystem, systems are ephemeral. Pods die, nodes autoscale, and IPs change. Old-school monitoring (checking if a server responds to ping) is dead. Instead, we need Observability: the ability to understand the internal state of a system based purely on its external outputs.

    13.1 Monitoring vs. Observability

    These terms are often used interchangeably, but they are distinct concepts in Site Reliability Engineering (SRE).

    📟

    Monitoring

    "Is the system healthy?"
    Monitoring tells you when something is wrong based on known failure modes. It is dashboard-centric.
    Example: "CPU usage is at 95%." "The API is returning 500 errors."

    🔬

    Observability

    "Why is the system behaving this way?"
    Observability allows you to ask arbitrary questions about unknown failure modes. It is data-centric.
    Example: "Show me all requests from User ID 123 that took longer than 2s and hit the checkout service."

    13.2 The Three Pillars of Observability

    To achieve observability, we need three distinct types of data.

    📊 1. Metrics (Aggregatable)

    Metrics are numerical data measured over time. They are cheap to store and fast to query.
    Use Case: "How much traffic are we getting?" "What is the average latency?"
    Tool: Prometheus.
    Structure: http_requests_total{status="200", method="POST"} = 450

    📝 2. Logs (Discrete Events)

    Logs are text records of specific events. They provide high-fidelity context but are expensive to store at scale.
    Use Case: "Why did this specific transaction fail?" "What was the stack trace?"
    Tool: Loki, ELK Stack (Elasticsearch, Logstash, Kibana), Fluentd.
    Structure: [ERROR] Database connection failed: Timeout at 12:00:01

    🕸️ 3. Traces (Request Lifecycle)

    Traces track a single request as it hops between microservices.
    Use Case: "The checkout is slow. Is it the frontend, the payment gateway, or the database lock?"
    Tool: Jaeger, Tempo, OpenTelemetry.
    Structure: Span A (50ms) -> Span B (200ms) -> Span C (Error).

    13.3 Deep Dive: Prometheus Architecture

    Prometheus is the de facto standard for Kubernetes monitoring. Its architecture is unique because it is Pull-based.

    • Scraping: Instead of apps sending data to Prometheus (Push), Prometheus visits the app's /metrics endpoint every 15 seconds to "scrape" data.
    • Time Series Database (TSDB): Prometheus stores data locally on disk in a highly optimized custom format. It does not use SQL.
    • Service Discovery: In Kubernetes, pods change IPs constantly. Prometheus asks the Kubernetes API server: "Where are all the pods?" and updates its scrape targets automatically.
    • PromQL: A powerful query language. Example: rate(http_requests_total[5m]) calculates the per-second request rate averaged over 5 minutes.

    13.4 The 4 Golden Signals of SRE

    When building dashboards, don't just graph random things. Google SRE recommends tracking these four signals for every user-facing system:

    The Signals

    1. Latency: Time taken to serve a request. (Distinguish between successful requests and errors—an error might be very fast!).
    2. Traffic: Demand placed on the system (e.g., HTTP requests/sec or I/O bytes/sec).
    3. Errors: The rate of requests that fail (HTTP 500s, non-zero exit codes).
    4. Saturation: How "full" is the system? (e.g., Memory usage, Thread pool capacity, CPU quotas). Saturation predicts imminent failure.

    Hands-On Labs

    Theory is useless without visibility. In these labs, you will deploy the industry-standard "Kube-Prometheus-Stack" (formerly Prometheus Operator). You will go from a blind cluster to a fully instrumented environment where you can visualize CPU throttling, memory leaks, and disk saturation.

    ⚠️ Resource Warning

    The full observability stack is heavy. It spins up Prometheus, AlertManager, Grafana, Node-Exporter (DaemonSet), and Kube-State-Metrics.
    Requirement: Ensure your cluster has at least 4GB of RAM allocated. If using Minikube: minikube start --memory 4096 --cpus 2.

    Lab 1: The "One-Click" Observability Suite

    Objective: Deploy Prometheus, Grafana, and AlertManager using Helm. Understand what components are installed.

    Navigate to your lab working directory:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course/week-13-monitoring-logging/lab-01-observability-stack

    Every file you create in this lab goes into this directory. Stay here for all remaining steps unless a step explicitly tells you to change directory.

    Create Namespace:

    Isolate monitoring tools from your application workloads.

    kubectl create namespace monitoring
    Install via Helm:

    We use the community stack, which includes pre-configured dashboards for Kubernetes.

    helm repo add prometheus-community https://prometheus-community.github.io/helm-charts helm repo update helm install monitoring prometheus-community/kube-prometheus-stack \ --namespace monitoring \ --set grafana.adminPassword="admin"

    Note: We set a fixed password for simplicity in this lab.

    Verify Installation:
    kubectl get pods -n monitoring

    You should see several pods:
    • alertmanager-... (Handles alerts)
    • grafana-... (The UI)
    • prometheus-... ( The Metric Collector)
    • kube-state-metrics-... (Converts K8s objects to metrics)
    • node-exporter-... (One per node, reads hardware stats)

    Lab 2: Visualizing Cluster Health

    Objective: Access the Grafana dashboard and analyze the "Cluster Compute Resources" to understand CPU/Memory pressure.

    Navigate to your lab working directory:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course/week-13-monitoring-logging/lab-02-grafana-dashboards

    Every file you create in this lab goes into this directory. Stay here for all remaining steps unless a step explicitly tells you to change directory.

    Port Forwarding:

    Expose Grafana to your localhost.

    kubectl port-forward svc/monitoring-grafana 8080:80 -n monitoring

    Keep this terminal open. Open a new terminal for other commands.

    Login:

    1. Open browser to http://localhost:8080.
    2. Username: admin
    3. Password: admin (Set in Lab 1).

    Explore Default Dashboards:

    1. Click the Dashboards icon (four squares) in the left sidebar.
    2. Go to Browse -> General or Kubernetes folder.
    3. Open the dashboard named "Kubernetes / Compute Resources / Namespace (Pods)".

    Analyze Data:

    Select datasource: Prometheus and namespace: kube-system.
    Look at the "CPU Usage" graph. This is real-time data scraped from your actual cluster components (CoreDNS, Etcd, API Server).

    Lab 3: Writing PromQL Queries

    Objective: Don't rely just on pre-made dashboards. Learn to ask Prometheus specific questions using PromQL.

    Navigate to your lab working directory:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course/week-13-monitoring-logging/lab-03-promql

    Every file you create in this lab goes into this directory. Stay here for all remaining steps unless a step explicitly tells you to change directory.

    Open Explore Mode:

    In Grafana, click the Compass Icon (Explore) on the left sidebar.

    Query 1: Instant Vector:

    Type this query and hit Shift + Enter:

    kube_pod_status_phase{phase="Running"}

    This returns a list of all running pods with a value of 1. It tells you what is running right now.

    Query 2: Rate (The most important function):

    Raw counters (like "total bytes sent") are useless because they always go up. We need the rate (bytes per second).

    rate(container_network_receive_bytes_total[5m])

    This calculates the per-second rate of network traffic received by every container, averaged over a 5-minute window.

    Query 3: Aggregation (Sum):

    The previous query returned 50+ lines (one for every container). Let's sum it up to see total cluster traffic.

    sum(rate(container_network_receive_bytes_total[5m]))

    Now you have a single line graph showing total cluster network load.

    Lab 4: Creating a PrometheusRule

    Objective: Define an alert using Kubernetes Custom Resources (CRDs). We will trigger an alert if a specific pod is missing.

    Navigate to your lab working directory:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course/week-13-monitoring-logging/lab-04-alerting

    Every file you create in this lab goes into this directory. Stay here for all remaining steps unless a step explicitly tells you to change directory.

    Deploy a Dummy App:
    kubectl create deployment web-app --image=nginx kubectl expose deployment web-app --port=80
    Define the Alert:

    Create a file named alert-rule.yaml. This tells Prometheus to fire if web-app has 0 replicas for 1 minute.

    apiVersion: monitoring.coreos.com/v1 kind: PrometheusRule metadata: name: web-app-down namespace: monitoring labels: release: monitoring # Critical: Matches the Prometheus Selector spec: groups: - name: web-app.rules rules: - alert: WebAppDown expr: kube_deployment_status_replicas_available{deployment="web-app"} == 0 for: 1m labels: severity: critical annotations: summary: "The Web App is down!" description: "Replicas available has been 0 for more than 1 minute."
    Apply the Alert:
    kubectl apply -f alert-rule.yaml
    Trigger the Alert:

    Scale the deployment to 0 to break the app.

    kubectl scale deployment web-app --replicas=0
    Observe in Prometheus UI:

    1. Port forward Prometheus (different port from Grafana):
    kubectl port-forward svc/monitoring-prometheus 9090:9090 -n monitoring
    2. Open http://localhost:9090.
    3. Go to Alerts.
    4. You will see WebAppDown in PENDING state (yellow) for 1 minute.
    5. After 1 minute, it turns FIRING (red).

    Cleanup:
    kubectl delete deployment web-app kubectl delete svc web-app kubectl delete prometheusrule web-app-down -n monitoring
    14

    Advanced CI/CD Pipeline Engineering

    Optimization, Templating, and Pipeline-as-Code

    Welcome to Week 14. You have already built basic pipelines that test and deploy code. That was the "Junior" phase. Now we enter the "Senior" phase.

    A pipeline that takes 45 minutes to run is a productivity killer. A pipeline that requires copying and pasting 50 lines of YAML for every new microservice is a maintenance nightmare. A pipeline that passes even when the tests fail is a liability.

    This week, we stop treating pipelines as simple scripts and start treating them as Software Engineering artifacts. We will apply software principles—DRY (Don't Repeat Yourself), Modularity, and Efficiency—to our CI/CD workflows. You will learn to optimize build times using Caching and Parallelism, and you will architect reusable Shared Libraries to standardize deployments across the entire organization.

    Mentorship Guidance

    Mentorship students receive structured support for Advanced CI/CD Pipeline Engineering: live Q&A, assignment feedback, and office hours focused on this week's goals.

    Share blockers in Google Classroom and schedule a review session once your lab and assignment work are ready.

    Learning Objectives

    By the end of this week, you will be able to demonstrate mastery across all levels of Bloom's Taxonomy:

    Remember

    Define Mechanisms: Define the difference between "Vertical Scaling" (bigger runners) and "Horizontal Scaling" (more runners) for pipelines. Identify the syntax for Matrix Builds.

    Understand

    Explain Architecture: Explain how Docker Layer Caching works inside a CI environment and why `npm ci` is preferred over `npm install` in pipelines.

    Apply

    Execute Refactoring: Refactor a monolithic 20-minute serial pipeline into a 5-minute parallel pipeline using stages and matrix strategies.

    Analyze

    Diagnose Bottlenecks: Analyze a build log to identify the "Critical Path" (the longest chain of dependent steps) and propose an optimization strategy.

    Evaluate

    Assess Tools: Evaluate the trade-offs between Jenkins (Self-hosted, infinite customizability, high maintenance) vs. GitHub Actions (SaaS, fast setup, lower control).

    Create

    Build Libraries: Architect a "Composite Action" (GitHub) or "Shared Library" (Jenkins) that encapsulates complex security scanning logic into a single line of config.

    Pre-Class Exercises

    Mandatory Requirement: Complete these exercises before beginning the teaching notes. This week focuses heavily on Jenkins and GitHub Actions. Budget approximately 1.5 hours.

    ⚙ Week 14 Workspace Setup — Complete This First

    Create your working directory for this week inside the course repository. Every pre-class note, lab file, screenshot, and project deliverable belongs under this folder tree — nothing goes anywhere else.

    1. Navigate to your course repository root:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course
    2. Create this week's folder and all sub-directories in one command:
    mkdir -p week-14-advanced-cicd-pipelines/{preclass-notes,lab-01-build-caching,lab-02-matrix-testing,lab-03-reusable-workflows,lab-04-docker-buildx,weekly-project}

    This single command creates the whole week's workspace tree. You will stay within this tree for pre-class work, every lab, and the weekly project.

    3. Verify the structure was created correctly:
    find week-14-advanced-cicd-pipelines -type d | sort

    You should see 6 directories (preclass-notes + 4 lab folders + weekly-project).

    4. Create your pre-class notes file:
    touch week-14-advanced-cicd-pipelines/preclass-notes/week-14-notes.md

    All research, written answers, and reflections for this week's pre-class exercises go into this Markdown file. Keep it open as you work through the exercises below.

    5. Commit the empty scaffold to your GitHub portfolio:
    git add week-14-advanced-cicd-pipelines/ git commit -m "chore(week-14-advanced-cicd-pipelines): scaffold week 14 working directory" git push

    Pushing the empty scaffold creates a timestamped entry on your GitHub profile showing when you started this week — visible evidence for your portfolio and mentorship reviews.

    📁 Week 14 Folder Structure
    ~/devops-engineering-course/ └── week-14-advanced-cicd-pipelines/ ├── preclass-notes/ ├── lab-01-build-caching/ ├── lab-02-matrix-testing/ ├── lab-03-reusable-workflows/ ├── lab-04-docker-buildx/ └── weekly-project/

    1Local Jenkins Environment

    To practice Jenkins pipelines without provisioning a massive server, we will run Jenkins in Docker.

    Task: Run the official Jenkins LTS image ("Docker in Docker" enabled is complex, so we will focus on the Controller logic).

    docker run -p 8080:8080 -p 50000:50000 --restart=on-failure \ -v jenkins_home:/var/jenkins_home \ jenkins/jenkins:lts-jdk11

    Action: Open `http://localhost:8080`, unlock using the initial password printed in the logs, and install the "Suggested Plugins."

    2Dependency Theory: `npm ci`

    Context: A common mistake in pipelines is using commands designed for humans (`npm install`) instead of commands designed for robots (`npm ci`).

    Task: Research the difference between `package.json` and `package-lock.json`.
    Question: Why does `npm install` sometimes result in different production builds even if the code hasn't changed? Why does `npm ci` fix this?

    3GitHub Actions Self-Hosted Runner (Optional but Recommended)

    Sometimes the free GitHub runners are too slow or need access to private VPC resources.

    Task: Go to your GitHub Repo > Settings > Actions > Runners. Follow the instructions to download and run the runner agent on your local machine (Linux/Mac/WSL). Verify that you can trigger a "Hello World" job on your own laptop from the cloud.

    4The Concept of "Artifacts"

    Research: In a multi-stage pipeline, Stage 1 (Build) runs on Server A, and Stage 2 (Test) might run on Server B.
    Problem: Server B does not have the binary Server A built.
    Solution: Look up "Pipeline Artifacts" or "Stashing". How do we pass files between stages?

    Detailed Teaching Notes

    In the early days of CI/CD, engineers clicked buttons in Jenkins UIs to configure jobs. This was fragile; if the server died, the configuration was lost. Today, we treat pipelines as Production Code. They are versioned, code-reviewed, and tested just like the application they deploy.

    This section covers the architecture of high-performance pipelines: defining them in code, optimizing their execution speed, and modularizing them for reuse across hundreds of microservices.

    14.1 Pipeline-as-Code Architectures

    The industry has converged on two dominant patterns: Jenkins (Groovy) and GitHub Actions/GitLab CI (YAML). While the syntax differs, the concepts of Stages, Steps, and Agents are universal.

    ☕

    Jenkins (Declarative)

    Language: Groovy (JVM-based).
    Strengths: Infinite extensibility. Massive plugin ecosystem. Capable of complex logic (loops, try/catch) inside the pipeline.
    Weaknesses: Heavyweight. Requires maintaining a master server. "Plugin Hell" (updates breaking pipelines).

    🐙

    GitHub Actions (YAML)

    Language: YAML (Declarative).
    Strengths: Zero maintenance (SaaS). Deep integration with Git events (PRs, Issues). Fast startup.
    Weaknesses: Harder to implement complex conditional logic. Reuse requires "Actions" rather than simple functions.

    # Jenkinsfile (Declarative Syntax) pipeline { agent any stages { stage('Build') { steps { sh 'npm ci' sh 'npm run build' } } stage('Test') { steps { sh 'npm test' } } } }

    14.2 Optimization: The Need for Speed

    A 20-minute pipeline allows a developer to deploy 3 times an hour. A 2-minute pipeline allows 30 times. Speed is a competitive advantage.

    Strategy A: Parallelism

    If you have 3 distinct test suites (Unit, Lint, Security), running them sequentially (A -> B -> C) takes Sum(A+B+C) time. Running them in parallel takes Max(A, B, C) time.

    Strategy B: Matrix Builds

    You need to verify your library works on Node 14, 16, and 18. Instead of writing 3 jobs, you define a Matrix. The CI system automatically spawns 3 containers.

    # GitHub Actions Matrix Example jobs: test: runs-on: ubuntu-latest strategy: matrix: node-version: [14.x, 16.x, 18.x] steps: - uses: actions/checkout@v2 - name: Use Node.js ${{ matrix.node-version }} uses: actions/setup-node@v1 with: node-version: ${{ matrix.node-version }} - run: npm ci - run: npm test

    14.3 Caching & Artifacts

    Pipelines are usually ephemeral (containers start fresh every time). This means they re-download the internet (npm packages, apt packages) on every run.

    📦 Dependency Caching

    The Trick: Hash your lockfile (`package-lock.json`) to create a unique cache key.
    1. Pre-Run: Check if Cache Key `os-node-hash(package-lock.json)` exists.
    2. Hit: Download node_modules folder from S3/Cache (2 seconds).
    3. Miss: Run npm ci (2 minutes). Upload result to Cache.

    💾 Pipeline Artifacts

    Do not rebuild your Docker image in the "Deploy" stage if you already built it in the "Build" stage.
    Pattern: Build Once, Deploy Anywhere.
    Pass the binary or the Docker Image Digest between stages using Artifacts (or a registry).

    14.4 Reusability: Don't Repeat Yourself (DRY)

    If you have 50 microservices, you do not want 50 copies of the same Jenkinsfile. If you need to update the security scanner version, you'd have to edit 50 repos.

    Jenkins Shared Libraries:
    You write a global function standardPipeline() in a separate git repo. Your microservices call this one line.

    GitHub Composite Actions:
    You wrap a sequence of steps (Checkout -> Setup Node -> Install -> Build) into a single Action my-org/build-node-app@v1.

    ⚠️ The "BOM" (Bill of Materials) Problem

    When using shared libraries or actions, always pin versions (e.g., uses: my-action@v1.2.0). If you point to @main or @latest, a breaking change in the shared library will instantly break every pipeline in your company simultaneously. This is known as a "Blast Radius" event.

    Hands-On Labs

    In these labs, we will move away from simple "Hello World" builds and tackle real-world engineering challenges. You will optimize a slow pipeline using caching, parallelize testing using matrix builds, and refactor repetitive code into reusable modules.

    ⚠️ Prerequisites

    1. GitHub Account: We will focus on GitHub Actions as it is the most accessible platform for modern CI.
    2. Fork: You will need a repo to work on. Fork this sample repo: https://github.com/actions/starter-workflows or create a fresh repo with a basic package.json.

    Lab 1: Speeding Up Builds with Caching

    Objective: Reduce the installation time of NPM dependencies from minutes to seconds.

    Navigate to your lab working directory:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course/week-14-advanced-cicd-pipelines/lab-01-build-caching

    Every file you create in this lab goes into this directory. Stay here for all remaining steps unless a step explicitly tells you to change directory.

    Create the Baseline Workflow:

    Create .github/workflows/ci.yml. This is the "slow" version.

    name: Slow CI on: [push] jobs: build: runs-on: ubuntu-latest steps: - uses: actions/checkout@v3 - name: Use Node.js uses: actions/setup-node@v3 with: node-version: 16 - name: Install Dependencies run: npm ci # This downloads hundreds of MBs every time - name: Test run: npm test
    Run and Measure:

    Commit this file. Go to the "Actions" tab in GitHub. Note the duration of the "Install Dependencies" step (e.g., 45s).

    Implement Caching:

    Modify the workflow to check for a cache before installing.

    - name: Cache node modules uses: actions/cache@v3 id: npm-cache with: path: ~/.npm key: ${{ runner.os }}-node-${{ hashFiles('**/package-lock.json') }} restore-keys: | ${{ runner.os }}-node- - name: Install Dependencies run: npm ci
    Verify Acceleration:

    1. Trigger the workflow again (Push a dummy change).
    2. The first run will still be slow (Cache Miss - creates the cache).
    3. Trigger it a third time.
    4. Check the logs. You should see "Cache restored from ..." and the install time drop drastically (e.g., 2s).

    Lab 2: Parallel Testing (The Matrix)

    Objective: Ensure your application works on Node 14, 16, and 18 simultaneously without writing three separate jobs.

    Navigate to your lab working directory:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course/week-14-advanced-cicd-pipelines/lab-02-matrix-testing

    Every file you create in this lab goes into this directory. Stay here for all remaining steps unless a step explicitly tells you to change directory.

    Define the Strategy:

    Modify your ci.yml to include a strategy block.

    jobs: test: runs-on: ubuntu-latest strategy: matrix: node-version: [14.x, 16.x, 18.x] steps: - uses: actions/checkout@v3 - name: Use Node.js ${{ matrix.node-version }} uses: actions/setup-node@v3 with: node-version: ${{ matrix.node-version }} - run: npm ci - run: npm test
    Observe Parallelism:

    Push the change. Go to the Actions tab.
    You will see the test job split into 3 sub-jobs: test (14.x), test (16.x), test (18.x).
    They run at the same time. The total workflow time is determined by the slowest job, not the sum of all jobs.

    Lab 3: Creating a Reusable Workflow

    Objective: Abstract the "Build and Test" logic into a separate file so 10 different repos can use it.

    Navigate to your lab working directory:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course/week-14-advanced-cicd-pipelines/lab-03-reusable-workflows

    Every file you create in this lab goes into this directory. Stay here for all remaining steps unless a step explicitly tells you to change directory.

    Create the Template:

    Create .github/workflows/reusable-build.yml.

    name: Reusable Build on: workflow_call: inputs: node-version: required: true type: string jobs: build: runs-on: ubuntu-latest steps: - uses: actions/checkout@v3 - uses: actions/setup-node@v3 with: node-version: ${{ inputs.node-version }} - run: npm ci - run: npm test
    Call the Template:

    Modify your main ci.yml to "call" the reusable workflow instead of defining steps.

    name: Main CI on: [push] jobs: call-workflow-1: uses: ./.github/workflows/reusable-build.yml with: node-version: '16.x' call-workflow-2: uses: ./.github/workflows/reusable-build.yml with: node-version: '18.x'
    Analyze the Benefit:

    If you need to add a "Security Scan" step later, you only add it to reusable-build.yml. Both call-workflow-1 and call-workflow-2 (and any other repos referencing it) get the update automatically.

    Lab 4: Accelerating Docker Builds

    Objective: Docker builds in CI are notoriously slow because they start from scratch. We will use the GitHub Actions Cache to store Docker layers.

    Navigate to your lab working directory:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course/week-14-advanced-cicd-pipelines/lab-04-docker-buildx

    Every file you create in this lab goes into this directory. Stay here for all remaining steps unless a step explicitly tells you to change directory.

    Setup Buildx:

    Standard `docker build` cannot easily export cache. We need `docker buildx`.

    - name: Set up Docker Buildx uses: docker/setup-buildx-action@v2
    Configure Build with Cache:
    - name: Build and push uses: docker/build-push-action@v4 with: context: . push: false tags: user/app:latest # The Magic Part: cache-from: type=gha cache-to: type=gha,mode=max
    Verify:

    Run the build twice.
    Run 1: "Exporting cache" (Slow).
    Run 2: "Importing cache manifest" (Fast). You will see steps marked as CACHED in the logs, skipping the heavy lifting (like npm install inside the Dockerfile).

    15

    Advanced Deployment Strategies

    Blue-Green, Canary, and Progressive Delivery

    Welcome to Week 15. Up until now, we have relied on Kubernetes' standard Rolling Update strategy. While better than downtime, Rolling Updates have a fatal flaw: once the new pods are running, 100% of users are exposed to the new code immediately. If you have a bug that only affects checkout, you just broke checkout for everyone.

    This week, we learn how to deploy code like Netflix, Uber, and Facebook. We will decouple "Deployment" (installing the code) from "Release" (exposing it to traffic). We will implement Blue-Green Deployments for instant rollbacks and Canary Releases to test new features on just 1% of your users before rolling out to the rest. Finally, we will automate this entire process using Flagger for "Progressive Delivery."

    Mentorship Guidance

    Mentorship students receive structured support for Advanced Deployment Strategies: live Q&A, assignment feedback, and office hours focused on this week's goals.

    Share blockers in Google Classroom and schedule a review session once your lab and assignment work are ready.

    Learning Objectives

    By the end of this week, you will be able to demonstrate mastery across all levels of Bloom's Taxonomy:

    Remember

    Define Strategies: Define the difference between Blue-Green (Traffic Switching), Canary (Traffic Splitting), and A/B Testing (User Segmentation).

    Understand

    Explain Mechanics: Explain how an Ingress Controller or Service Mesh weights traffic between two different Kubernetes Services.

    Apply

    Execute Traffic Shift: Use Argo Rollouts or Flagger to automatically shift traffic from 0% to 50% to 100% based on success metrics.

    Analyze

    Diagnose Failures: Analyze a failed Canary deployment where error rates spiked at 10% traffic, and determine why the automated rollback triggered.

    Evaluate

    Assess Cost vs. Risk: Evaluate the infrastructure cost of Blue-Green (requiring 2x capacity) versus the risk mitigation it provides.

    Create

    Build Strategy: Architect a "Progressive Delivery" pipeline that promotes code from Canary to Primary only if Prometheus latency metrics stay below 200ms.

    Pre-Class Exercises

    Mandatory Requirement: Complete these exercises before beginning the teaching notes. Advanced deployments rely heavily on networking layers (Ingress/Mesh) to split traffic. Budget approximately 1.5 hours.

    ⚙ Week 15 Workspace Setup — Complete This First

    Create your working directory for this week inside the course repository. Every pre-class note, lab file, screenshot, and project deliverable belongs under this folder tree — nothing goes anywhere else.

    1. Navigate to your course repository root:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course
    2. Create this week's folder and all sub-directories in one command:
    mkdir -p week-15-advanced-deployment/{preclass-notes,lab-01-nginx-canary,lab-02-argo-rollouts,lab-03-blue-green,lab-04-automated-rollback,lab-05-traffic-mirroring,weekly-project}

    This single command creates the whole week's workspace tree. You will stay within this tree for pre-class work, every lab, and the weekly project.

    3. Verify the structure was created correctly:
    find week-15-advanced-deployment -type d | sort

    You should see 7 directories (preclass-notes + 5 lab folders + weekly-project).

    4. Create your pre-class notes file:
    touch week-15-advanced-deployment/preclass-notes/week-15-notes.md

    All research, written answers, and reflections for this week's pre-class exercises go into this Markdown file. Keep it open as you work through the exercises below.

    5. Commit the empty scaffold to your GitHub portfolio:
    git add week-15-advanced-deployment/ git commit -m "chore(week-15-advanced-deployment): scaffold week 15 working directory" git push

    Pushing the empty scaffold creates a timestamped entry on your GitHub profile showing when you started this week — visible evidence for your portfolio and mentorship reviews.

    📁 Week 15 Folder Structure
    ~/devops-engineering-course/ └── week-15-advanced-deployment/ ├── preclass-notes/ ├── lab-01-nginx-canary/ ├── lab-02-argo-rollouts/ ├── lab-03-blue-green/ ├── lab-04-automated-rollback/ ├── lab-05-traffic-mirroring/ └── weekly-project/

    1Install NGINX Ingress Controller

    To perform Canary releases (splitting traffic 90/10), we need a traffic manager. The standard Kubernetes `Service` object cannot do fractional weighting; it only does Round Robin.

    # Using Helm to install NGINX Ingress helm repo add ingress-nginx https://kubernetes.github.io/ingress-nginx helm repo update helm install my-ingress ingress-nginx/ingress-nginx \ --namespace ingress-nginx --create-namespace
    ⚠️ Cloud vs. Local

    If you are on Minikube, run `minikube addons enable ingress`.
    If you are on AWS EKS, the command above will create an AWS Network Load Balancer (NLB). This costs money (~$0.025/hour).

    2Cluster Capacity Check

    Context: Blue-Green deployment requires running two complete copies of your application simultaneously (the old version and the new version).
    Task: Check your cluster limits. If your app takes 2GB RAM, do you have 4GB available?

    # Check allocatable resources on nodes kubectl describe nodes | grep Allocatable -A 5

    3Install Argo Rollouts CLI

    We will use Argo Rollouts as our deployment controller. It extends Kubernetes with advanced deployment capabilities.

    # MacOS brew install argoproj/tap/kubectl-argo-rollouts # Linux curl -LO https://github.com/argoproj/argo-rollouts/releases/latest/download/kubectl-argo-rollouts-linux-amd64 chmod +x ./kubectl-argo-rollouts-linux-amd64 sudo mv ./kubectl-argo-rollouts-linux-amd64 /usr/local/bin/kubectl-argo-rollouts

    4Research: The "Blast Radius"

    Thought Experiment: You push a code change that causes a memory leak.
    Scenario A: You deploy to all 100 servers at once.
    Scenario B: You deploy to 1 server ("Canary"), watch it for 10 minutes, then deploy to the rest.
    Task: Read about the "Blast Radius" concept in SRE. How does Canary deployment reduce blast radius?

    Detailed Teaching Notes

    In traditional software delivery, "Deploying" and "Releasing" were the same event. You copied the files to the server, restarted Apache, and the users saw the new version immediately. In modern DevOps, we separate these concerns.

    Deployment: Installing the new version of the application onto the infrastructure (e.g., creating the Pods).
    Release: Switching production traffic to the new version.

    By separating them, we gain the ability to test the deployment in production before users see it (Dark Launching), or to expose it to only a tiny fraction of users (Canary).

    15.1 The Deployment Strategy Spectrum

    There are four main ways to update an application in Kubernetes. Each trades off between downtime, cost, and risk.

    ♻️

    Recreate

    Mechanism: Kill Version A. Then start Version B.
    Pros: Simple. No extra cost. No version incompatibility issues.
    Cons: Downtime (The system is down while B boots up).

    🔄

    Rolling Update (Default)

    Mechanism: Replace A with B one by one.
    Pros: Zero downtime. Minimal extra cost (usually +1 pod).
    Cons: No control. Blast Radius is 100% (once rolled out, everyone sees the bug). Hard to verify.

    🔵🟢

    Blue-Green

    Mechanism: Spin up a full cluster of B (Green) alongside A (Blue). Switch the Load Balancer instantly.
    Pros: Instant rollback. Testing on production infrastructure before switch.
    Cons: Expensive (Requires 2x capacity).

    🐤

    Canary

    Mechanism: Send 1% of traffic to B. If healthy, increase to 10%, 50%, 100%.
    Pros: Lowest risk. Errors only affect a small user subset.
    Cons: Complex. Requires advanced traffic routing (Ingress/Mesh) and automated analysis.

    15.2 Traffic Management: The "Router"

    Standard Kubernetes Services cannot do weighted traffic splitting (e.g., "90% here, 10% there"). They only do Round Robin (random). To implement Canary, we need a smarter router.

    🚦 The Ingress Controller Approach

    Advanced Ingress Controllers (like NGINX or ALB) support Annotations for splitting.
    Example Annotation: nginx.ingress.kubernetes.io/canary-weight: "10"
    This tells NGINX: "For every 100 requests hitting `example.com`, send 10 to the Service marked as Canary and 90 to the Primary."

    15.3 Automated Progressive Delivery (Argo Rollouts)

    Doing Canary manually is tedious (editing YAML to change weights from 10 to 20 to 50). Argo Rollouts is a Kubernetes controller that automates this. It introduces a new CRD: Rollout.

    apiVersion: argoproj.io/v1alpha1 kind: Rollout metadata: name: my-app spec: replicas: 5 strategy: canary: steps: - setWeight: 20 - pause: {duration: 1h} # Wait 1 hour to see if errors spike - setWeight: 50 - pause: {duration: 10m} - setWeight: 100 template: # (Standard Pod Template here)

    The "Analysis" Phase:
    The true power of Argo Rollouts is not just waiting for time (pause: 1h), but querying Prometheus.
    "Check Prometheus every minute. Is the HTTP 500 rate on the Canary pods > 1%? If yes, ABORT and rollback immediately."

    15.4 Feature Flags (Application-Level Deployment)

    Infrastructure deployment (Canary) controls traffic. Feature Flags control logic.

    Even if you deploy the code to 100% of servers, you can wrap the new feature in an if statement:

    if (LaunchDarkly.client.variation("new-checkout-flow", user_id) == true) { return renderNewCheckout(); } else { return renderOldCheckout(); }

    This decouples release from deployment entirely. You can turn features on/off instantly without touching Kubernetes or waiting for a pipeline.

    ⚠️ Technical Debt Warning

    Feature Flags are powerful but dangerous. If you don't clean them up, your code becomes a graveyard of dead if/else logic. A mature team has a process to remove flags once a feature is fully launched.

    Hands-On Labs

    In these labs, we will move beyond the standard kubectl apply. We will manually orchestrate a Canary release using NGINX Ingress to understand the networking mechanics, and then we will automate the process using Argo Rollouts, the industry-standard controller for progressive delivery in Kubernetes.

    ⚠️ Prerequisites

    1. Ingress Controller: You MUST have NGINX Ingress installed (from Pre-Class exercises).
    2. Argo Rollouts: You need the Controller installed in the cluster and the plugin installed in `kubectl`.
    3. Hosts File: We will use the domain `canary.local`. Add 127.0.0.1 canary.local (or your Minikube IP) to your /etc/hosts file.

    Lab 1: The "Poor Man's Canary" (NGINX Annotations)

    Objective: Use NGINX Ingress annotations to manually split traffic 90/10 between two Services without using any external deployment tools.

    Navigate to your lab working directory:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course/week-15-advanced-deployment/lab-01-nginx-canary

    Every file you create in this lab goes into this directory. Stay here for all remaining steps unless a step explicitly tells you to change directory.

    Deploy V1 (Primary):

    Create a namespace `demo`. Deploy "Blue" (V1) application and Service.

    kubectl create ns demo # V1 Deployment & Service kubectl apply -f - <
    Deploy V1 Ingress:

    Create the main Ingress rule pointing to V1.

    kubectl apply -f - <
    Deploy V2 (Canary):

    Deploy "Green" (V2) application and Service.

    # Similar to V1, but different text and name kubectl create deployment app-v2 --image=hashicorp/http-echo --replicas=1 --port=5678 -n demo -- -text="VERSION_2_GREEN" kubectl expose deployment app-v2 --name=service-v2 --port=80 --target-port=5678 -n demo
    Create the Canary Ingress:

    Here is the magic. We create a second Ingress resource with the same host but specific annotations.

    kubectl apply -f - <
    Verify Traffic Split:

    Run a loop to hit the endpoint 10 times. You should see roughly 8 BLUEs and 2 GREENs.

    for i in {1..10}; do curl -s canary.local; echo; done
    VERSION_1_BLUE VERSION_1_BLUE VERSION_2_GREEN <-- There it is! VERSION_1_BLUE ...
    Cleanup Lab 1:
    kubectl delete ns demo

    Lab 2: The `Rollout` CRD

    Objective: Automate the deployment process. Instead of managing Deployments and Ingresses manually, we will define a `Rollout` resource that handles traffic splitting and pauses automatically between steps.

    Navigate to your lab working directory:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course/week-15-advanced-deployment/lab-02-argo-rollouts

    Every file you create in this lab goes into this directory. Stay here for all remaining steps unless a step explicitly tells you to change directory.

    Install Argo Rollouts Controller:

    We need to install the controller and the CRDs into the cluster.

    kubectl create namespace argo-rollouts kubectl apply -n argo-rollouts -f https://github.com/argoproj/argo-rollouts/releases/latest/download/install.yaml
    Deploy a Basic Rollout:

    Create a file named `basic-rollout.yaml`. Notice `kind: Rollout` (not Deployment).

    apiVersion: argoproj.io/v1alpha1 kind: Rollout metadata: name: rollout-demo spec: replicas: 5 strategy: canary: steps: - setWeight: 20 - pause: {} # Pauses indefinitely until manual approval - setWeight: 50 - pause: { duration: 10s } # Auto-promotes after 10s - setWeight: 100 revisionHistoryLimit: 2 selector: matchLabels: app: rollout-demo template: metadata: labels: app: rollout-demo spec: containers: - name: rollouts-demo image: argoproj/rollouts-demo:blue ports: - name: http containerPort: 8080 --- apiVersion: v1 kind: Service metadata: name: rollout-demo spec: ports: - port: 80 targetPort: 8080 protocol: TCP name: http selector: app: rollout-demo
    kubectl apply -f basic-rollout.yaml
    Visualize (The CLI Dashboard):

    Argo provides a terminal UI to watch the pods and the traffic split in real-time.

    kubectl argo rollouts get rollout rollout-demo --watch
    Trigger an Update:

    Update the image to "Yellow" to start the canary process.

    kubectl argo rollouts set image rollout-demo rollouts-demo=argoproj/rollouts-demo:yellow

    Observe in the Dashboard:
    1. Argo creates 1 new pod (Yellow) and keeps 4 old pods (Blue) -> roughly 20% weight.
    2. It hits the `pause: {}` step and waits. Status becomes "Paused".

    Promote:

    Since the rollout is paused indefinitely, you must manually approve it to proceed to the next step.

    kubectl argo rollouts promote rollout-demo

    Observe:
    1. It scales Yellow to 50% (3 pods) and Blue to 50% (3 pods).
    2. It waits 10 seconds (as defined in `duration: 10s`).
    3. It automatically scales Yellow to 100% and terminates the Blue replica set.

    Cleanup Lab 2:
    kubectl delete rollout rollout-demo kubectl delete svc rollout-demo

    Lab 3: Blue-Green with Preview Service

    Objective: Configure a Blue-Green strategy where we can test the new version on a private "Preview" service before switching the public "Active" service. Unlike Canary (which is gradual), this switch is atomic.

    Navigate to your lab working directory:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course/week-15-advanced-deployment/lab-03-blue-green

    Every file you create in this lab goes into this directory. Stay here for all remaining steps unless a step explicitly tells you to change directory.

    Define Services:

    We need two services: one for the public (Active) and one for QA (Preview). Both initially point to the same selector, but Argo will manage them.

    kubectl apply -f - <
    Define Blue-Green Rollout:

    Create the Rollout manifest specifying the `blueGreen` strategy.

    kubectl apply -f - <
    Trigger Update:

    Update the image to "Green".

    kubectl argo rollouts set image rollout-bluegreen rollouts-demo=argoproj/rollouts-demo:green

    What happens now?
    1. Argo spins up a new ReplicaSet (Green).
    2. It waits for the Green pods to become healthy.
    3. It updates the rollout-bluegreen-preview service to point to Green.
    4. The rollout-bluegreen-active service still points to Blue.

    Verify Preview:

    You can port-forward the preview service to test the new version without affecting users hitting the active service.

    # In Terminal A: kubectl port-forward svc/rollout-bluegreen-preview 9090:80 # In Terminal B: curl localhost:9090 # Output should show the "Green" version (different color/text)
    The Switch (Promotion):

    Once you are happy with the Preview, flip the switch.

    kubectl argo rollouts promote rollout-bluegreen

    Mechanism: Argo instantly updates the activeService selector to point to the Green ReplicaSet. Users are switched instantly. After a delay (default 30s), the Blue ReplicaSet is scaled down.

    Cleanup Lab 3:
    kubectl delete rollout rollout-bluegreen kubectl delete svc rollout-bluegreen-active rollout-bluegreen-preview

    Lab 4: Automated Rollback with Analysis

    Objective: The holy grail of progressive delivery. If the error rate increases during a canary step, the system should automatically abort and rollback without human intervention.

    Navigate to your lab working directory:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course/week-15-advanced-deployment/lab-04-automated-rollback

    Every file you create in this lab goes into this directory. Stay here for all remaining steps unless a step explicitly tells you to change directory.

    Context:

    Normally, you would query Prometheus. Since that requires a complex setup from Week 13, we will use a Job-based Analysis for this lab. This runs a Kubernetes Job; if the job exits with code 0, it passes. If it exits with 1, it fails.

    Create AnalysisTemplate:

    This template defines "What is a success?". Here, we simulate a test that fails randomly.

    kubectl apply -f - <
    Update Rollout to use Analysis:

    Modify your Canary Rollout (from Lab 2) to trigger this analysis during the steps. Update `basic-rollout.yaml` or patch it live.

    kubectl patch rollout rollout-demo --type='merge' -p '{"spec": {"strategy": {"canary": {"steps": [{"setWeight": 20}, {"pause": {"duration": "5s"}}, {"analysis": {"templates": [{"templateName": "success-rate"}], "args": [{"name": "service-name", "value": "rollout-demo"}]}}]}}}}'

    (Note: The patch adds an `analysis` step after the 20% weight step).

    Trigger a "Bad" Update:

    Change the image to trigger the rollout.

    kubectl argo rollouts set image rollout-demo rollouts-demo=argoproj/rollouts-demo:red
    Watch it Fail:

    Observation Sequence:
    1. Argo scales Canary to 20%.
    2. It runs the Analysis Job.
    3. The Job exits with 1 (Fail).
    4. Argo marks the analysis as Failed.
    5. Result: Argo automatically aborts the rollout. The status becomes Degraded. It scales the Canary back to 0 immediately to save users.

    Cleanup Lab 4:
    kubectl delete rollout rollout-demo kubectl delete analysistemplate success-rate

    Lab 5: Traffic Mirroring (The "Dark Launch")

    Objective: Test a new microservice version with real production traffic without the users knowing. The traffic is duplicated (mirrored) to the new service "out-of-band." The response from the mirrored service is discarded, so if it crashes, the user is unaffected.

    Navigate to your lab working directory:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course/week-15-advanced-deployment/lab-05-traffic-mirroring

    Every file you create in this lab goes into this directory. Stay here for all remaining steps unless a step explicitly tells you to change directory.

    Concept:

    We will use NGINX Ingress to send 100% of traffic to V1 (User Facing) and a copy of 100% of traffic to V2 (Shadow).

    Deploy Shadow Service (V2):

    Reuse the `demo` namespace from Lab 1. If you deleted it, recreate the V1/V2 deployments and services quickly.

    # Ensure V1 (Main) exists kubectl create deployment app-v1 --image=hashicorp/http-echo --replicas=1 --port=5678 -n demo -- -text="MAIN_V1" kubectl expose deployment app-v1 --name=service-v1 --port=80 --target-port=5678 -n demo # Ensure V2 (Shadow) exists kubectl create deployment app-v2 --image=hashicorp/http-echo --replicas=1 --port=5678 -n demo -- -text="SHADOW_V2" kubectl expose deployment app-v2 --name=service-v2 --port=80 --target-port=5678 -n demo
    Configure Mirroring Ingress:

    Apply the mirror annotation to the main Ingress.

    kubectl apply -f - <
    Generate Traffic:
    curl canary.local

    Output: You will ONLY see "MAIN_V1". The shadow response is discarded by NGINX.

    Verify the Shadow Received Traffic:

    Check the logs of the V2 pod. It should show it received the request.

    kubectl logs -l app=app-v2 -n demo
    "GET / HTTP/1.1" 200 ... "curl/7.64.1"

    This proves V2 processed the request. This strategy is perfect for testing performance regressions or database load issues without risking user downtime.

    Cleanup Lab 5:
    kubectl delete ns demo

    🎉 Part 3 Conclusion

    You have now successfully orchestrated the four major patterns of modern deployment:

    • Canary (Manual): Using Ingress weights to verify networking logic.
    • Canary (Automated): Using Argo Rollouts to pause and promote based on time.
    • Blue-Green: Using atomic service switching for zero-downtime cutovers.
    • Traffic Mirroring: Using dark launching to test code safely.

    In Part 4, we will review the solutions and troubleshooting steps for when these rollouts get stuck (e.g., "Why is my rollout paused forever?").

    17

    Advanced Observability & SRE Practices

    From "Monitoring" to "Observability" and Site Reliability Engineering

    Welcome to Week 17. You have built robust systems, but they are black boxes. When a user reports "it's slow," how do you know where? Is it the frontend, the API, the database, or the network mesh?

    This week, we transcend basic "up/down" monitoring. We enter the world of Observability—the ability to understand the internal state of a system based purely on its outputs (Logs, Metrics, and Traces). We will adopt the Site Reliability Engineering (SRE) culture pioneered by Google, shifting from "zero defects" to "managed risk" using Error Budgets and SLOs. We will also implement Distributed Tracing to visualize the lifecycle of a request across 50+ microservices.

    Mentorship Guidance

    Mentorship students receive structured support for Advanced Observability & SRE Practices: live Q&A, assignment feedback, and office hours focused on this week's goals.

    Share blockers in Google Classroom and schedule a review session once your lab and assignment work are ready.

    Learning Objectives

    By the end of this week, you will be able to demonstrate mastery across all levels of Bloom's Taxonomy:

    Remember

    Define Terms: Define the precise difference between an SLI (Indicator), SLO (Objective), and SLA (Agreement).

    Understand

    Explain Context Propagation: Explain how a Trace ID is passed from the Load Balancer -> Frontend -> Backend -> Database to stitch together a distributed trace.

    Apply

    Execute Tracing: Instrument a Kubernetes application with Jaeger sidecars or OpenTelemetry agents to generate waterfall trace diagrams.

    Analyze

    Diagnose Bottlenecks: Analyze a Jaeger trace to identify a "N+1 Query" problem where a service makes 50 serial database calls instead of 1 batch call.

    Evaluate

    Assess Burn Rates: Evaluate an Alerting Policy based on "Burn Rate" (how fast the Error Budget is being consumed) rather than simple thresholds.

    Create

    Design SRE Dashboard: Build a Grafana dashboard that explicitly tracks the "Four Golden Signals": Latency, Traffic, Errors, and Saturation.

    Pre-Class Exercises

    Mandatory Requirement: Complete these exercises before beginning the teaching notes. Observability requires heavy data processing. Budget approximately 1.5 hours.

    ⚙ Week 17 Workspace Setup — Complete This First

    Create your working directory for this week inside the course repository. Every pre-class note, lab file, screenshot, and project deliverable belongs under this folder tree — nothing goes anywhere else.

    1. Navigate to your course repository root:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course
    2. Create this week's folder and all sub-directories in one command:
    mkdir -p week-17-advanced-observability/{preclass-notes,lab-01-golden-signals,lab-02-distributed-tracing,lab-03-log-trace-correlation,lab-04-slo-burn-rate,weekly-project}

    This single command creates the whole week's workspace tree. You will stay within this tree for pre-class work, every lab, and the weekly project.

    3. Verify the structure was created correctly:
    find week-17-advanced-observability -type d | sort

    You should see 6 directories (preclass-notes + 4 lab folders + weekly-project).

    4. Create your pre-class notes file:
    touch week-17-advanced-observability/preclass-notes/week-17-notes.md

    All research, written answers, and reflections for this week's pre-class exercises go into this Markdown file. Keep it open as you work through the exercises below.

    5. Commit the empty scaffold to your GitHub portfolio:
    git add week-17-advanced-observability/ git commit -m "chore(week-17-advanced-observability): scaffold week 17 working directory" git push

    Pushing the empty scaffold creates a timestamped entry on your GitHub profile showing when you started this week — visible evidence for your portfolio and mentorship reviews.

    📁 Week 17 Folder Structure
    ~/devops-engineering-course/ └── week-17-advanced-observability/ ├── preclass-notes/ ├── lab-01-golden-signals/ ├── lab-02-distributed-tracing/ ├── lab-03-log-trace-correlation/ ├── lab-04-slo-burn-rate/ └── weekly-project/

    1Install Jaeger Operator

    We will use Jaeger for distributed tracing. Install the operator to manage the complexity of the collector, query, and agent components.

    # Create observability namespace kubectl create namespace observability # Install cert-manager (prerequisite) kubectl apply -f https://github.com/cert-manager/cert-manager/releases/download/v1.13.0/cert-manager.yaml # Install Jaeger Operator kubectl create -n observability -f https://github.com/jaegertracing/jaeger-operator/releases/download/v1.51.0/jaeger-operator.yaml

    2PromQL Refresher

    Context: To define SLOs (e.g., "99% of requests must be < 200ms"), you need advanced Prometheus queries.
    Task: Research the `histogram_quantile` function.
    Question: What does `histogram_quantile(0.99, sum(rate(http_request_duration_seconds_bucket[5m])) by (le))` actually calculate?

    3The Four Golden Signals

    Reading: Read the "Monitoring Distributed Systems" chapter of the Google SRE Book (available free online).
    Task: Write down definitions for:
    1. Latency (Time taken)
    2. Traffic (Demand placed on system)
    3. Errors (Rate of failure)
    4. Saturation (How "full" the system is)

    4Install Loki (Optional but Recommended)

    We will use the PLG Stack (Prometheus, Loki, Grafana). You already have P and G.
    Task: Add the Grafana repo and install Loki (Log aggregation) via Helm.

    helm repo add grafana https://grafana.github.io/helm-charts helm install loki grafana/loki-stack -n observability

    Detailed Teaching Notes

    In the "old days" of monoliths, debugging was simple: you SSH'd into the server and checked /var/log/syslog. In a Kubernetes cluster with 50 microservices and 200 pods, SSH is impossible. Logs are ephemeral (they vanish when pods die). And a single user request might touch 15 different services before returning.

    Observability is not just "more monitoring." Monitoring tells you when something is wrong (The "What"). Observability allows you to ask arbitrary questions about your system to figure out why it is wrong (The "Why").

    17.1 The Three Pillars of Observability

    To understand a complex distributed system, we need three distinct types of data.

    📈

    1. Metrics (The "Is it healthy?")

    Definition: Aggregatable numerical data (counters, gauges, histograms).
    Use Case: "What is my error rate right now?" "How much CPU is used?"
    Pros: Extremely cheap to store. Fast to query.
    Cons: Lacks context. Tells you "Error rate spiked" but not "Which user caused it?"

    📜

    2. Logs (The "What happened?")

    Definition: Discrete, timestamped text records of events.
    Use Case: "Database connection failed: Timeout."
    Pros: High detail. Perfect for root cause analysis of specific errors.
    Cons: Expensive. Hard to aggregate ("How many timeouts?" requires scanning gigabytes of text).

    🔗

    3. Traces (The "Where did it go?")

    Definition: The lifecycle of a request as it traverses multiple services.
    Use Case: "Why did the login take 5 seconds?" (Trace shows: 4.8s spent waiting for the Legacy User DB).
    Pros: Essential for microservices latency debugging.

    17.2 SRE Fundamentals: SLIs, SLOs, and Error Budgets

    Google's Site Reliability Engineering (SRE) philosophy states: "100% reliability is the wrong target."
    If you aim for 100%, you cannot release new features because every change carries risk. You should aim for "Reliable Enough."

    📐 The SRE Acronyms

    SLI (Service Level Indicator): The metric you measure.
    Example: HTTP Status Code 200 vs 500.

    SLO (Service Level Objective): The target you aspire to.
    Example: "99.9% of requests in 30 days should be 200 OK."

    SLA (Service Level Agreement): The legal contract with consequences.
    Example: "If we drop below 99.5%, we will refund 10% of your subscription." (Engineers care about SLOs; Lawyers care about SLAs).

    💰 The Error Budget

    If your SLO is 99.9%, your Error Budget is 0.1%.
    $$ \text{Total Requests} \times 0.001 = \text{Allowed Failures} $$
    The Golden Rule: As long as you have Error Budget remaining, developers can push features fast. If you exhaust the budget (too many outages), Feature Freezes are enforced until the system stabilizes. Reliability becomes a shared responsibility.

    17.3 Distributed Tracing Anatomy

    How does Jaeger construct a waterfall graph from 50 isolated services?

    Context Propagation

    When the Load Balancer receives a request, it generates a unique Trace ID (e.g., `abc-123`).
    1. LB calls Service A. It injects HTTP header `x-b3-traceid: abc-123`.
    2. Service A does work (creates a Span).
    3. Service A calls Service B. It must forward the header `x-b3-traceid: abc-123`.
    4. If Service A forgets to forward the header, the trace breaks. This is called "Broken Context."

    The Jaeger Collector receives these spans asynchronously and stitches them together based on IDs.

    17.4 The PLG Stack (Prometheus, Loki, Grafana)

    The industry is moving away from ELK (Elasticsearch, Logstash, Kibana) for Kubernetes logs. Why?

    Elasticsearch: Indexes every word in every log line. Huge memory/disk usage. Great for text search, expensive for ops logs.
    Loki: Indexes only the metadata (labels like `app=frontend`, `pod=frontend-xyz`). It treats logs like Prometheus metrics.

    Result: Loki is 90% cheaper than ELK. You search using "LogQL", which looks just like PromQL.

    ⚠️ Cardinality Explosion

    When designing metrics/logs, avoid High Cardinality labels.
    Good Label: `status_code=500` (Cardinality: ~5 values).
    Bad Label: `user_id=102938` (Cardinality: Millions).
    If you put User IDs or Email Addresses into Prometheus labels, you will crash the server. Prometheus creates a new time series for every unique combination of labels.

    Hands-On Labs

    In these labs, we will move beyond simple "is it running?" checks. We will build a full Observability Stack (PLG + Jaeger). You will instrument a microservices application to generate traces, query logs using LogQL, and define mathematical Service Level Objectives (SLOs) that alert you only when users are truly impacted.

    ⚠️ Prerequisites

    1. Cluster Specs: Ensure your cluster has sufficient memory (6GB+). Running Prometheus, Grafana, Loki, and Jaeger simultaneously is resource-intensive.
    2. Jaeger Operator: Must be installed (from Pre-Class exercises).
    3. Sample App: We will use the "HotROD" (Rides on Demand) app from Jaeger, as it is designed to showcase tracing capabilities.

    Lab 1: Building the Golden Signals

    Objective: Construct a Grafana dashboard that visualizes the "Four Golden Signals" for a deployed service.

    Navigate to your lab working directory:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course/week-17-advanced-observability/lab-01-golden-signals

    Every file you create in this lab goes into this directory. Stay here for all remaining steps unless a step explicitly tells you to change directory.

    Deploy Metrics Generator:

    We need an app that generates consistent metrics. We will use a standard demo app.

    kubectl create ns monitoring-demo kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/istio/istio/master/samples/httpbin/httpbin.yaml -n monitoring-demo # Generate load (run in a separate terminal) kubectl run load-generator --image=curlimages/curl -n monitoring-demo --restart=Never -- /bin/sh -c "while true; do curl -s http://httpbin:8000/status/200; curl -s http://httpbin:8000/status/500; sleep 0.5; done"
    Open Grafana:

    Port-forward your Grafana service (from previous weeks or installed via kube-prometheus-stack).
    Create a New Dashboard -> Add Panel.

    Signal 1: Traffic (Request Rate):

    How many requests per second?

    sum(rate(http_requests_total{namespace="monitoring-demo"}[2m])) by (service)
    Signal 2: Errors (Error Rate):

    What percentage of requests are 5xx?

    sum(rate(http_requests_total{status=~"5.*", namespace="monitoring-demo"}[2m])) / sum(rate(http_requests_total{namespace="monitoring-demo"}[2m]))
    Signal 3: Latency (Duration):

    How long do requests take (99th Percentile)?
    Note: Ensure your app exports a histogram metric for this to work.

    histogram_quantile(0.99, sum(rate(http_request_duration_seconds_bucket[5m])) by (le))

    Lab 2: Visualizing the Waterfall (Jaeger)

    Objective: Deploy the "HotROD" application and trace a request as it travels through Frontend -> Route Service -> Driver Service -> Database.

    Navigate to your lab working directory:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course/week-17-advanced-observability/lab-02-distributed-tracing

    Every file you create in this lab goes into this directory. Stay here for all remaining steps unless a step explicitly tells you to change directory.

    Deploy Jaeger Instance:

    Create a simple "AllInOne" Jaeger instance using the Operator.

    kubectl apply -f - <
    Deploy HotROD App:

    This app is pre-instrumented with the OpenTelemetry SDK. It automatically sends traces to the Jaeger collector URL.

    kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/jaegertracing/jaeger/master/examples/hotrod/docker/hotrod-k8s.yaml -n observability
    Generate Traces:

    Access the HotROD UI and click the buttons to order rides.

    # Port forward HotROD UI kubectl port-forward svc/hotrod 8080:8080 -n observability # Visit http://localhost:8080
    Analyze the Trace:

    1. Open Jaeger UI (`kubectl port-forward svc/my-jaeger-query 16686:16686 -n observability`).
    2. Select Service: `frontend`. Click "Find Traces".
    3. Click on a trace with many spans (e.g., 50+ spans).
    4. Identify the Bottleneck: Look for the longest horizontal bar. Expanding it usually reveals a Redis or MySQL query taking too long.

    Lab 3: Correlating Logs with Traces

    Objective: Use Loki's query language (LogQL) to filter high-volume logs and find the "needle in the haystack."

    Navigate to your lab working directory:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course/week-17-advanced-observability/lab-03-log-trace-correlation

    Every file you create in this lab goes into this directory. Stay here for all remaining steps unless a step explicitly tells you to change directory.

    Context:

    You have Grafana connected to Loki. You want to find error logs from the `hotrod` app specifically involving "driver T7382".

    Basic Selection:

    In Grafana > Explore > Loki.
    Enter the Log Stream Selector:

    {namespace="observability", app="hotrod"}
    Log Pipeline (Filtering):

    Filter for lines containing "error".

    {namespace="observability", app="hotrod"} |= "error"
    Parsing JSON Logs:

    If the logs are JSON, you can parse fields to do advanced filtering (e.g., finding latency > 500ms from logs).

    {app="hotrod"} | json | latency > 500ms

    Observation: This is the power of Loki. It turns unstructured text logs into queryable data metrics.

    Lab 4: The Burn Rate Alert

    Objective: Define a Prometheus Rule that alerts if we are consuming our Error Budget too fast.
    Scenario: SLO is 99.9%. Window is 30 days.

    Navigate to your lab working directory:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course/week-17-advanced-observability/lab-04-slo-burn-rate

    Every file you create in this lab goes into this directory. Stay here for all remaining steps unless a step explicitly tells you to change directory.

    Calculate the Threshold:

    If our error rate is 0.1% (0.001), we are burning budget at 1x speed.
    If error rate is 1% (0.01), we are burning at 10x speed. We want to alert if Burn Rate > 10.

    Create PrometheusRule:
    apiVersion: monitoring.coreos.com/v1 kind: PrometheusRule metadata: name: slo-alerts namespace: monitoring spec: groups: - name: slo.rules rules: - alert: HighErrorBudgetBurn # Alert if Error Rate > 10 * (1 - SLO) over 1 hour # 10 * (1 - 0.999) = 10 * 0.001 = 0.01 (1%) expr: | job:request_error_rate:5m > 0.01 for: 2m labels: severity: critical annotations: summary: "Burning Error Budget too fast (10x)" description: "We will exhaust our 30-day budget in 3 days at this rate."
    Verify:

    This alert doesn't fire on a single error. It fires only if the rate of errors is sustained enough to threaten the monthly goal. This significantly reduces "pager fatigue."

    18

    DevSecOps & Security Automation

    Shifting Left: Automated Compliance, Scanning, and Secrets Management

    Welcome to Week 18. Until now, you have built pipelines that deploy code fast. But speed without safety is negligence. In traditional IT, "Security" was a gatekeeper at the very end of the release process, often delaying deployments by weeks. This model is incompatible with modern DevOps.

    DevSecOps is the practice of integrating security controls into the pipeline itself. We call this "Shifting Left"—moving security checks earlier in the lifecycle. Instead of a manual audit before production, we automate vulnerability scanning (SAST/DAST) on every commit, scan container images for CVEs, and manage secrets dynamically so they never exist in plain text. This week, you stop being just an engineer and become a guardian of your infrastructure.

    Mentorship Guidance

    Mentorship students receive structured support for DevSecOps & Security Automation: live Q&A, assignment feedback, and office hours focused on this week's goals.

    Share blockers in Google Classroom and schedule a review session once your lab and assignment work are ready.

    Learning Objectives

    By the end of this week, you will be able to demonstrate mastery across all levels of Bloom's Taxonomy:

    Remember

    Define Threat Landscape: Identify the difference between Static Application Security Testing (SAST) and Dynamic Application Security Testing (DAST).

    Understand

    Explain Supply Chain Security: Explain how a "Software Supply Chain Attack" (like SolarWinds) works and why signing images (Cosign) mitigates it.

    Apply

    Execute Scanning: Integrate Trivy into a CI pipeline to block any Docker build containing "Critical" severity vulnerabilities.

    Analyze

    Diagnose Policy Violations: Use OPA (Open Policy Agent) to audit Kubernetes manifests and identify deployments running as `root` user.

    Evaluate

    Assess Secret Strategies: Evaluate the risks of Kubernetes Secrets (base64 encoded) versus using a dedicated Secrets Manager like HashiCorp Vault.

    Create

    Build Dynamic Secrets: Architect a system where database credentials are generated "Just-in-Time" (JIT) for a specific pod and revoked automatically after 1 hour.

    Pre-Class Exercises

    Mandatory Requirement: Complete these exercises before beginning the teaching notes. Security tools often require specific kernel capabilities or CLI setups. Budget approximately 1.5 hours.

    ⚙ Week 18 Workspace Setup — Complete This First

    Create your working directory for this week inside the course repository. Every pre-class note, lab file, screenshot, and project deliverable belongs under this folder tree — nothing goes anywhere else.

    1. Navigate to your course repository root:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course
    2. Create this week's folder and all sub-directories in one command:
    mkdir -p week-18-devsecops/{preclass-notes,lab-01-vulnerability-scanning,lab-02-admission-controller,lab-03-vault-secrets,lab-04-image-signing,lab-05-vault-agent-injector,weekly-project}

    This single command creates the whole week's workspace tree. You will stay within this tree for pre-class work, every lab, and the weekly project.

    3. Verify the structure was created correctly:
    find week-18-devsecops -type d | sort

    You should see 7 directories (preclass-notes + 5 lab folders + weekly-project).

    4. Create your pre-class notes file:
    touch week-18-devsecops/preclass-notes/week-18-notes.md

    All research, written answers, and reflections for this week's pre-class exercises go into this Markdown file. Keep it open as you work through the exercises below.

    5. Commit the empty scaffold to your GitHub portfolio:
    git add week-18-devsecops/ git commit -m "chore(week-18-devsecops): scaffold week 18 working directory" git push

    Pushing the empty scaffold creates a timestamped entry on your GitHub profile showing when you started this week — visible evidence for your portfolio and mentorship reviews.

    📁 Week 18 Folder Structure
    ~/devops-engineering-course/ └── week-18-devsecops/ ├── preclass-notes/ ├── lab-01-vulnerability-scanning/ ├── lab-02-admission-controller/ ├── lab-03-vault-secrets/ ├── lab-04-image-signing/ ├── lab-05-vault-agent-injector/ └── weekly-project/

    1Install Security Tooling

    We will use standard open-source tools for scanning and policy enforcement.

    # 1. Trivy (Vulnerability Scanner) curl -sfL https://raw.githubusercontent.com/aquasecurity/trivy/main/contrib/install.sh | sh -s -- -b /usr/local/bin # 2. Cosign (Image Signing) # (Check sigstore documentation for your OS) # 3. HashiCorp Vault (Secrets Management) # Install CLI tool brew install vault # OR apt/yum equivalent

    2The Vulnerable Image

    Task: Pull an intentionally vulnerable image to test your scanners later.
    docker pull dvwa/dvwa (Damn Vulnerable Web App)
    Question: Run docker history dvwa/dvwa. Can you see any plain text credentials or weird environment variables?

    3Research: The SolarWinds Hack

    Reading: Spend 15 minutes reading about the SolarWinds supply chain attack.
    Key Concept: The attackers didn't hack the production servers directly; they hacked the build system and injected code before it was signed. This highlights why we need to secure the pipeline (CI/CD), not just the server.

    4Kubernetes Secrets Audit

    Task: Run the following command on your cluster:
    kubectl get secret -n default -o jsonpath="{.items[0].data.password}" | base64 --decode
    Observation: Realize that Kubernetes Secrets are merely Base64 encoded, not encrypted by default. Anyone with `kubectl` access can read them. This is why we need Vault.

    Detailed Teaching Notes

    DevSecOps is not a tool you buy; it is a methodology. It acknowledges that in a world where we deploy 50 times a day, a manual security review process is a bottleneck that will be bypassed. To maintain security at velocity, we must automate it.

    The Core Philosophy: "Security is everyone's responsibility." Developers must fix vulnerabilities before code leaves their laptop (or at least before it merges), rather than Security Engineers finding them 3 weeks later in production.

    18.1 The "Shift Left" Paradigm

    Imagine a timeline of software delivery from Left (Design/Code) to Right (Production).
    Traditional Security: Testing happens at the far Right. Fixing a bug here is 100x more expensive because it involves rollbacks, hotfixes, and customer PR damage.
    Shifting Left: Moving security controls to the earliest possible point.

    💻

    IDE / Pre-Commit

    Tools: SonarLint, Talisman.
    Goal: Block hardcoded secrets (AWS keys) from even being committed to Git. Real-time feedback to the developer.

    🏗️

    CI Pipeline (Build)

    Tools: Trivy, Snyk, SonarQube.
    Goal: Scan the source code (SAST) and dependencies (SCA) for known CVEs. If a critical vuln is found, fail the build.

    📦

    Artifact Registry

    Tools: Cosign, Harbor.
    Goal: Sign the Docker image cryptographically. "I certify this image was built by our trusted CI, not a hacker."

    🛡️

    Admission Controller

    Tools: OPA Gatekeeper, Kyverno.
    Goal: Prevent deployment. "If the image isn't signed or runs as root, reject the `kubectl apply`."

    18.2 Layers of Vulnerability Scanning

    Scanning isn't just one thing. We must scan three distinct layers.

    1. SAST (Static Application Security Testing)

    What it scans: Your raw source code (Python, Go, JS).
    What it finds: SQL Injection risks, buffer overflows, bad coding patterns.
    Tool: SonarQube, CodeQL.

    2. SCA (Software Composition Analysis)

    What it scans: Your libraries (`package.json`, `requirements.txt`).
    What it finds: "You are using `log4j v2.14` which has a Critical CVE."
    Tool: Snyk, Trivy (`trivy fs .`).

    3. Container Scanning

    What it scans: The OS layer (Alpine/Debian) inside your Docker image.
    What it finds: "The `openssl` binary installed in this container is outdated."
    Tool: Trivy (`trivy image my-app:latest`).

    18.3 Policy as Code (OPA)

    In the past, security policies were written in PDF documents ("Do not run containers as root"). Nobody read them.
    Open Policy Agent (OPA) allows us to write these policies as code (Rego language) and enforce them automatically in the Kubernetes API.

    The Flow:
    1. Developer runs `kubectl apply`.
    2. API Server sends request to OPA Gatekeeper (Validating Webhook).
    3. OPA checks: input.request.object.spec.securityContext.runAsNonRoot == true?
    4. If false, OPA rejects the request with a message: "Policy Violation: Containers must not run as root."

    18.4 Secrets Management: The "Secret Zero" Problem

    The Problem: Kubernetes Secrets are weak (base64). Environment variables are leaky.
    The Solution: HashiCorp Vault.
    Vault centralizes secret storage. But how does an app verify its identity to Vault to get the secret? That is the "Secret Zero" problem.

    🔑 Dynamic Secrets (Just-In-Time)

    Instead of giving an app a permanent database password (which can be stolen), Vault generates a Dynamic Secret.
    1. App authenticates to Vault (using Kubernetes Service Account JWT).
    2. App asks for DB access.
    3. Vault calls the Database and creates a temporary user `user-abc-123` valid for 1 hour.
    4. Vault returns credentials to the App.
    5. After 1 hour, Vault automatically deletes the user from the DB.
    If a hacker steals this credential, it is useless after 60 minutes.

    18.5 Supply Chain Security (Signing)

    If a hacker compromises your Docker Hub account, they can push a malicious image named `my-app:v1`. Your cluster will pull it and run it.
    To prevent this, we use Image Signing (Cosign).
    1. Sign: The CI pipeline signs the image with a private key after building.
    2. Verify: The Kubernetes cluster (via OPA/Kyverno) verifies the signature using the public key before pulling. If the signature is missing or invalid, the deployment is blocked.

    Hands-On Labs

    In these labs, we will implement the "Shift Left" philosophy. You will act as the Security Engineer. Your goal is to break the build when vulnerabilities are found, prevent insecure pods from deploying, and secure secrets using an external vault.

    ⚠️ Prerequisites

    1. Tooling: Ensure `trivy`, `helm`, and `kubectl` are installed.
    2. Cluster: A fresh Minikube or Cloud cluster (some OPA policies conflict with existing messy namespaces).
    3. Vault CLI: Install the Vault client (`brew install vault` or Linux equivalent).

    Lab 1: Breaking the Build (Vulnerability Scanning)

    Objective: Integrate a security scanner into your workflow. We will scan an intentionally vulnerable image, see the report, and then emulate a CI/CD pipeline failure.

    Navigate to your lab working directory:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course/week-18-devsecops/lab-01-vulnerability-scanning

    Every file you create in this lab goes into this directory. Stay here for all remaining steps unless a step explicitly tells you to change directory.

    The "Ignorant" Pull:

    Pull a known vulnerable image (Damn Vulnerable Web App).

    docker pull vulnerables/web-dvwa
    The Scan:

    Run Trivy against the image. Prepare to be shocked by the output.

    trivy image vulnerables/web-dvwa

    Observation: You will see a table with hundreds of "LOW", "MEDIUM", "HIGH", and "CRITICAL" vulnerabilities. This image is a security nightmare.

    The CI Gate (Fail on Critical):

    In a CI pipeline (Jenkins/GitHub Actions), we don't look at the screen. We check the exit code. If exit code != 0, the build stops.
    Run Trivy in "CI Mode":

    trivy image --exit-code 1 --severity CRITICAL vulnerables/web-dvwa
    ... Total: 58 (CRITICAL: 58) ... # Check the exit code echo $? # Output should be 1 (Failure)
    Fixing it (The Alpine Way):

    Let's compare this to a minimal, secure image.

    docker pull alpine:3.18 trivy image --exit-code 1 --severity CRITICAL alpine:3.18 echo $? # Output should be 0 (Success)

    Lesson: Using minimal base images (Alpine/Distroless) is the easiest way to pass security scans.

    Lab 2: The Admission Controller

    Objective: Prevent developers from deploying containers that run as `root`. We will install OPA Gatekeeper and apply a "Constraint".

    Navigate to your lab working directory:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course/week-18-devsecops/lab-02-admission-controller

    Every file you create in this lab goes into this directory. Stay here for all remaining steps unless a step explicitly tells you to change directory.

    Install Gatekeeper:

    Deploy the Gatekeeper controller to your cluster.

    kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/open-policy-agent/gatekeeper/release-3.13/deploy/gatekeeper.yaml

    Wait for the pods in `gatekeeper-system` to be Ready.

    Define the Template (The Logic):

    Create a `ConstraintTemplate` that describes how to check for root users.

    kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/open-policy-agent/gatekeeper-library/master/library/pod-security-policy/users/template.yaml
    Enforce the Constraint (The Rule):

    Now we tell Gatekeeper: "Apply this logic to ALL namespaces except kube-system."

    kubectl apply -f - <
    The Blocked Deployment:

    Try to run a standard NGINX pod (which defaults to root).

    kubectl run root-nginx --image=nginx --restart=Never
    Error from server (Forbidden): admission webhook "validation.gatekeeper.sh" denied the request: [must-run-as-non-root] Container root-nginx is attempting to run as root

    Success: The API Server rejected the request. It never even reached the scheduler.

    The Allowed Deployment:

    Run a pod with specific user ID 1000.

    kubectl run secure-nginx --image=nginx --restart=Never --overrides='{"spec": {"securityContext": {"runAsUser": 1000, "runAsGroup": 3000}}}' # This should succeed (pod created)
    Cleanup Lab 2:
