    kubectl delete K8sPSPAllowedUsers must-run-as-non-root kubectl delete pod secure-nginx

    Lab 3: Introduction to Vault

    Objective: Deploy HashiCorp Vault in Dev Mode and practice reading/writing secrets via the CLI. This simulates replacing K8s secrets with a centralized store.

    Navigate to your lab working directory:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course/week-18-devsecops/lab-03-vault-secrets

    Every file you create in this lab goes into this directory. Stay here for all remaining steps unless a step explicitly tells you to change directory.

    Install Vault (Dev Mode):

    We will use the Helm chart to install a Vault server in development mode (in-memory, no unseal keys needed).

    helm repo add hashicorp https://helm.releases.hashicorp.com helm install vault hashicorp/vault --set "server.dev.enabled=true"
    Connect to Vault:

    We need to enter the Vault pod to run commands.

    kubectl exec -it vault-0 -- sh
    Write a Secret:

    Store a database credential in the secret engine path `secret/db`.

    # In the vault pod vault kv put secret/db username=admin password=SuperSecretPassword!
    Success! Data written to: secret/db
    Read a Secret:

    Retrieve the secret.

    vault kv get secret/db
    ====== Data ====== Key Value --- ----- password SuperSecretPassword! username admin
    Version Control (The Vault Advantage):

    Update the password. Vault keeps the history.

    vault kv put secret/db username=admin password=NewPassword123 vault kv get secret/db # Notice "version 2" # Rollback to version 1 vault kv get -version=1 secret/db

    Observation: Kubernetes Secrets overwrite data instantly. Vault maintains versions, allowing instant rollback if a wrong secret is deployed.

    Exit Vault:
    exit

    We continue our journey into DevSecOps. Having secured the "Build" (Trivy), the "Deploy" (OPA), and the "Store" (Vault CLI), we now tackle the Supply Chain (Image Signing) and the Runtime (Secret Injection).

    Lab 4: Signing Images with Cosign

    Objective: mitigate "Supply Chain Attacks" by cryptographically signing a container image. This proves that the image running in your cluster was built by you and hasn't been tampered with by a man-in-the-middle.

    Navigate to your lab working directory:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course/week-18-devsecops/lab-04-image-signing

    Every file you create in this lab goes into this directory. Stay here for all remaining steps unless a step explicitly tells you to change directory.

    Prerequisites:

    You need `cosign` installed and a container registry you can push to (e.g., Docker Hub or TTL.sh for ephemeral testing). We will use `ttl.sh` for this lab as it requires no auth.

    Generate Keypair:

    Generate a private key (for signing) and a public key (for verification).

    cosign generate-key-pair # Enter a password (e.g., "secure123") # Output: cosign.key (private) and cosign.pub (public)
    Push an Image:

    Tag and push a simple alpine image to an ephemeral registry.

    export IMAGE=ttl.sh/my-secure-app-$(uuidgen):1h docker pull alpine:3.18 docker tag alpine:3.18 $IMAGE docker push $IMAGE
    Sign the Image:

    Use your private key to attach a signature to the image manifest in the registry.

    cosign sign --key cosign.key $IMAGE # Enter password

    Observation: Cosign pushes a new artifact (layer) to the registry containing the signature.

    Verify the Signature:

    Now, act as the Kubernetes Admission Controller. Verify the image using only the Public Key.

    cosign verify --key cosign.pub $IMAGE
    Verification for ttl.sh/my-secure-app... -- The following checks were performed on each of these signatures: - The cosign claims were validated - The signatures were verified against the specified public key

    Success: If a hacker modified the image content (layers), the cryptographic hash would change, and verification would fail.

    Lab 5: The "Secret Zero" Solution

    Objective: In Lab 3, we manually read secrets. That doesn't scale. Here, we automate it using the Vault Agent Injector. The Pod will start up, authenticate itself to Vault, fetch the secret, and write it to a shared file—application code sees a file, not the network.

    Navigate to your lab working directory:
    cd ~/devops-engineering-course/week-18-devsecops/lab-05-vault-agent-injector

    Every file you create in this lab goes into this directory. Stay here for all remaining steps unless a step explicitly tells you to change directory.

    Enable Kubernetes Auth in Vault:

    We need to tell Vault to trust our Kubernetes cluster's Service Account tokens.

    # In vault pod kubectl exec -it vault-0 -- sh # Enable auth method vault auth enable kubernetes # Configure Vault to talk to K8s API vault write auth/kubernetes/config \ kubernetes_host="https://$KUBERNETES_PORT_443_TCP_ADDR:443" # Create a role mapping "demo-role" to the "default" service account vault write auth/kubernetes/role/demo-role \ bound_service_account_names=default \ bound_service_account_namespaces=default \ policies=default \ ttl=1h exit
    Deploy an Injected Pod:

    We use Annotations to request secrets. The Vault Sidecar Injector (installed with the Helm chart) detects these and modifies the pod.

    kubectl apply -f - <
    Verify Injection:
    kubectl get pod vault-agent-demo
    NAME READY STATUS RESTARTS vault-agent-demo 2/2 Running 0

    Note: `2/2` means the App container + the Vault Agent sidecar.

    Read the Decrypted Secret:

    Check the filesystem of the application container.

    kubectl exec vault-agent-demo -c app -- cat /vault/secrets/database-config
    data: map[password:NewPassword123 username:admin] metadata: map[... version:2]

    The Magic: The app never talked to Vault. It never handled a token. It just read a file. If we rotate the password in Vault, the Agent updates the file instantly (and can even send a SIGHUP signal to reload the app).

    Cleanup Lab 5:
    kubectl delete pod vault-agent-demo helm uninstall vault
    20

    Week 20: Capstone Delivery, Executive Handover & Production Defense

    End-to-End Cloud Native Platform Engineering and Interview-Ready Delivery

    Welcome to the endgame. For the past 19 weeks, you have built individual components: a Docker container here, a Terraform script there, a Jenkins pipeline over there. Today, those training wheels come off.

    The Objective: You will architect, provision, and deploy a production-grade Internal Developer Platform (IDP) from scratch. You are no longer following a tutorial; you are building a company's infrastructure. You will combine Infrastructure as Code, GitOps, Observability, and Zero Trust Security into a single, cohesive system.

    This is not just an assignment. It is your portfolio piece, your executive handover package, and your technical defense rehearsal. It is the proof that you are ready for a Senior DevOps role.

    Mentorship Guidance

    Mentorship students receive structured support for Week 20: Capstone Delivery, Executive Handover & Production Defense: live Q&A, assignment feedback, architecture review, and office hours focused on this week's goals.

    Share blockers in Google Classroom and schedule your final defense review once your handover package is ready.

    Week 20 Capstone Workspace Setup

    Treat Week 20 as the final portfolio release. Create and maintain this capstone hierarchy as the single source of truth for mentorship grading and showcase review.

    cd ~/devops-engineering-course mkdir -p week-20-capstone-project/{preclass-notes,architecture,infrastructure,platform-services,security,observability,cicd,evidence,weekly-project} touch week-20-capstone-project/weekly-project/README.md

    Submit Google Classroom artefacts from week-20-capstone-project/weekly-project/, and keep cross-links to architecture docs and implementation folders.

    Final Learning Outcomes

    By the end of this week, you will demonstrate the ability to:

    Synthesize

    Full Stack Integration: Integrate 7+ distinct technologies (Terraform, K8s, Helm, ArgoCD, Vault, Prometheus, Jenkins) into a unified workflow.

    Architect

    Resilient Design: Design a high-availability architecture that survives node failures, availability zone outages, and traffic spikes.

    Secure

    DevSecOps Pipeline: Implement a "Supply Chain Security" pipeline that rejects unsigned or vulnerable artifacts before they reach production.

    Document

    Professional Handoff: Produce "Runbooks" and "Architecture Decision Records" (ADRs) suitable for a CTO review.

    The Scenario: "HexaBank" Digital Transformation

    🏦

    The Client: HexaBank

    Global FinTech Startup

    HexaBank is launching a new microservices-based trading platform. They have hired you as the Lead Platform Engineer. Their previous launch failed due to manual configuration drift and security breaches.

    👮 The CTO's Mandate

    • ✅ No Manual Ops: Everything must be IaC (Terraform).
    • ✅ Zero Trust: No hardcoded secrets. Use Vault.
    • ✅ GitOps: Developers push to Git; ArgoCD handles the rest.
    • ✅ Visibility: "If it isn't monitored, it doesn't exist."

    The Architecture Blueprint

    You are required to implement the following reference architecture. Study it carefully.

    🏗️

    Layer 1: Infrastructure

    Tool: Terraform.
    Scope: VPC, Subnets, EKS/AKS Cluster, Node Groups (Spot/On-Demand), RDS Database, IAM Roles.

    🔄

    Layer 2: CI/CD

    Tools: GitHub Actions (CI) + ArgoCD (CD).
    Scope: Build Docker images, Scan (Trivy), Sign (Cosign), Push to Registry. ArgoCD syncs Helm charts to EKS.

    🛡️

    Layer 3: Security

    Tools: Vault + Gatekeeper.
    Scope: Dynamic Secret Injection for DB creds. Policy enforcement (block unsigned images).

    👁️

    Layer 4: Observability

    Tools: Prometheus + Grafana + Loki.
    Scope: Centralized Logging, Golden Signals Dashboard, Alerts for Error Budget burn.

    Phase 0: Environment Prep

    Before writing code, you must prepare your "Control Plane".

    1. Select Your Cloud:

    Choose AWS or Azure.
    Constraint: You must stick to one provider for the infrastructure layer.

    2. Clean Slate:

    Nuke any existing resources from previous weeks (terraform destroy). You need a blank canvas to ensure no IP/CIDR conflicts.
    Budget Check: Ensure you have at least $10-$20 credit available for load balancers and managed Kubernetes costs.

    3. Tooling Check:
    # Verify your toolbelt terraform version # >= 1.5 kubectl version # >= 1.27 helm version # >= 3.12 argocd version # Client installed vault version # Client installed cosign version # Installed
    4. Fork the App:

    You need source code to deploy. Fork the Google Microservices Demo (Online Boutique) or continue using the `HexaShop` repository from previous weeks.

    Detailed Implementation Guide

    This is it. The following phases break down the monolithic task of "Building a Platform" into 4 distinct, manageable sprints. You must execute them sequentially. Do not proceed to Phase 2 until Phase 1 is verified operational.

    1
    Phase 1: The Foundation (Terraform)
    📂 Phase Directory

    Navigate to: ~/devops-engineering-course/week-20-capstone-project/phase-01/

    Objective: Provision the Cloud Infrastructure

    Write a modular Terraform configuration to provision the networking, compute, and IAM layers.

    Required Resources:
    • 🌐 Network: VPC (10.0.0.0/16), 3 Public Subnets, 3 Private Subnets, NAT Gateway.
    • ⚙️ Cluster: EKS/AKS Cluster (Version 1.27+).
    • 💻 Compute: Two Managed Node Groups:
        • System Pool: 2x t3.medium (On-Demand) for CoreDNS, ArgoCD, Vault.
        • App Pool: Autoscaling (0-5) t3.small (Spot Instances) for application workloads.
    • 🗄️ Database: RDS Postgres (Free Tier) or Azure SQL inside the Private Subnets.

    💡 Pro Tip: Remote State

    Do not store `terraform.tfstate` locally. Configure an S3 Backend (AWS) or Storage Account (Azure) with DynamoDB locking. This mimics a real team environment where multiple engineers deploy concurrently.

    # Deliverable Check: terraform output cluster_endpoint # Should return https://... aws eks update-kubeconfig --name hexabank-cluster kubectl get nodes # Should show "Ready" nodes with labels like 'lifecycle=spot'
    2
    Phase 2: The Engine (ArgoCD & Charts)
    📂 Phase Directory

    Navigate to: ~/devops-engineering-course/week-20-capstone-project/phase-02/

    Objective: Establish the Deployment Pipeline

    Install ArgoCD and create the "App of Apps" pattern to bootstrap the cluster.

    Tasks:
    1. Install ArgoCD: Use Helm. Expose it via LoadBalancer or Port-Forward.
    2. Create Helm Charts: Refactor the HexaBank application manifests into a reusable Helm Chart.
      Must include: `values.yaml` for environment variables, HPA configuration, and ServiceAccount logic.
    3. The App of Apps: Create a single Argo Application that points to a `gitops-repo`. This repo contains the definitions for:
      • The HexaBank App
      • Prometheus Stack
      • Vault
      • Gatekeeper
    # Deliverable Check: kubectl get applications -n argocd # Should show "hexabank", "prometheus", "vault" all 'Synced' and 'Healthy'.
    3
    Phase 3: The Fortress (DevSecOps)
    📂 Phase Directory

    Navigate to: ~/devops-engineering-course/week-20-capstone-project/phase-03/

    Objective: Secure the Runtime

    Implement the security controls designed in Week 18.

    CI Pipeline (GitHub Actions)

    Write a workflow `.github/workflows/deploy.yaml` that:
    1. Lint Helm Charts.
    2. Build Docker Image.
    3. Scan: Trivy (Fail on Critical).
    4. Sign: Cosign (Keyless or Key-based).
    5. Push to Docker Hub/ECR.
    6. Update the GitOps repo with new Image Tag.

    Admission Control (Gatekeeper)

    Apply a strict Constraint:
    • Reject images from `docker.io` unless they match your org.
    • Reject images without a valid signature.
    • Reject pods running as `root`.

    # Deliverable Check: kubectl run hack --image=alpine --restart=Never # Should fail with "Admission Webhook Denied"
    4
    Phase 4: The Panopticon (Observability)
    📂 Phase Directory

    Navigate to: ~/devops-engineering-course/week-20-capstone-project/phase-04/

    Objective: Day 2 Operations

    Ensure you can debug the system without SSH access.

    Tasks:
    • 📊 Dashboard: Import a Grafana dashboard showing CPU/RAM usage per Namespace and HTTP Error Rates.
    • 🪵 Logging: Ensure application logs (`stdout`) are visible in Loki/Grafana Explorer.
    • 🚨 Alerting: Create a Prometheus Alert that fires if `AvailableReplicas < 1` for 5 minutes.
    # Deliverable Check: # Port-forward Grafana kubectl port-forward svc/kube-prometheus-stack-grafana 3000:80 # Login (admin/prom-operator) and view the "Kubernetes / Compute Resources / Namespace" dashboard.

    Final Deliverables, Executive Handover & Defense

    An engineer's job isn't done until the documentation is written. In the real world, you can't just hand over a repo; you must hand over knowledge. Your submission will be graded not just on "Does it work?", but on "Is it maintainable?"

    A
    The Codebase (Git Structure)

    Clean Architecture Requirement

    Your repository must follow a Monorepo or Polyrepo structure that clearly separates Infrastructure from Application logic.

    hexabank-platform/ ├── .github/workflows/ # CI Pipelines ├── infrastructure/ │ ├── modules/ # Reusable Terraform Modules (VPC, EKS, RDS) │ ├── envs/ │ │ ├── dev/ # Dev Environment (tfvars) │ │ └── prod/ # Prod Environment (tfvars) ├── kubernetes/ │ ├── charts/ # Custom Helm Charts │ └── argocd/ # App-of-Apps Definitions ├── src/ # (Optional) Application Source Code └── docs/ # Architecture Decision Records (ADRs) & Runbooks

    ⛔ Hard Fail Criteria

    • Committing `.tfstate` files.
    • Committing hardcoded AWS_ACCESS_KEYs or DB passwords.
    • Committing the `.terraform/` directory.
    B
    The "Manual" (Runbooks & ADRs)

    Evidence of Thought

    You must submit two types of documents in your `docs/` folder:

    1. Architecture Decision Records (ADR)

    Explain why you made specific technical choices.
    Example: ADR-001: Secret Management
    • Decision: Use HashiCorp Vault instead of Sealed Secrets.
    • Context: We need dynamic secret injection for the database to meet compliance reqs.
    • Consequence: Increased complexity in cluster bootstrapping.

    2. Operational Runbooks

    "What do I do when it breaks?"
    Required Runbook: Disaster Recovery
    • How to restore the Terraform state if the lock file breaks.
    • How to unseal Vault manually if the auto-unseal fails.
    • How to rollback a bad deployment in ArgoCD.

    C
    The Video Defense

    Show, Don't Just Tell

    Record a 5-10 minute video demonstrating your platform. We do not want to see 10 minutes of you staring at a terminal waiting for a build. Edit the video or pause during long waits.

    Demo Script:
    1. The Commit: Change the background color of the frontend app and push to Git.
    2. The Pipeline: Show GitHub Actions running the build, scan (Trivy), and sign (Cosign).
    3. The Sync: Show ArgoCD detecting the new image tag and syncing the cluster.
    4. The Secret: Exec into the pod and `cat` the injected database credential to prove Vault is working.
    5. The Observability: Generate a 404 error and show it appearing on the Grafana dashboard.

    Career Launchpad: From Student to Senior

    You have the skills. Now you need the job. The DevOps market is competitive, but skilled engineers who can articulate "Business Value" are rare.

    🚀
    Resume Engineering

    Translating Labs to Experience

    Do not list "Completed a course." Frame your Capstone as a Project Implementation.

    ❌ Weak Bullet Point

    "Used Terraform to build AWS EKS and installed ArgoCD for deployments."

    ✅ Strong Bullet Point

    "Architected a Self-Healing Internal Developer Platform using Terraform and EKS, reducing deployment time by 90% via ArgoCD GitOps pipelines."

    Keywords to Include:

    Infrastructure as Code, Immutable Infrastructure, GitOps, CI/CD, Zero Trust Security, Secret Management, Observability, Service Mesh, Cost Optimization, FinOps, SRE, Incident Response.

    🎤
    The Technical Interview

    Be Prepared for These Questions

    "What happens when you type google.com into your browser?" (DevOps Edition)
    "How do you handle configuration drift?"
    "We have a legacy monolith. How do we move to K8s?"
    🎓 Congratulations, Engineer.

    You have completed the DevOps Engineering & Cloud Infrastructure Core Track.

    You started by running a single Docker container. You are ending by deploying a secure, observable, high-availability platform that powers modern businesses.

    The technology will change. Kubernetes might be replaced. Terraform might evolve. But the principles you learned—Automation, Visibility, Resilience, and Security—are timeless. Go build the future.

    Mentorship Review for Week 20

    Mentorship students: your Week 20 work (Week 20: Capstone Delivery, Executive Handover & Production Defense) receives expert feedback on:

    • Core concepts: accuracy and depth of the week's technical objectives
    • Implementation quality: correctness, reliability, and operational readiness
    • Security and risk: safeguards, least privilege, and failure handling
    • Architecture decisions: tradeoffs, scalability, and maintainability
    • Documentation: clarity, reproducibility, and deployment notes

    Submit via Google Classroom and schedule a review call for feedback and portfolio guidance.

    🧪 Phase IV Online Assessment

    Phase IV is complete (Weeks 16-20). Take the production excellence and DevSecOps assessment.

    Assessment URL: /courses/devops-engineering-cloud-infrastructure-core/assessments/phase-4-production-security

    🏁 Final Certification Assessment

    After clearing all phase assessments, complete the final certification exam to validate end-to-end mastery.

    Certification URL: /courses/devops-engineering-cloud-infrastructure-core/assessments/final-certification

    Mentorship Program FAQ

    Everything You Need to Know About Learning with Mentorship

    Who are the mentors?

    Our mentors are experienced DevOps engineers with 7+ years of production experience at companies like AWS, Google Cloud, and enterprise Fortune 500 organizations. They've built and scaled containerization strategies, Kubernetes clusters, CI/CD pipelines, and Infrastructure-as-Code systems that handle millions of requests daily. Each mentor specializes in different DevOps domains (cloud infrastructure, containerization, automation, security) and brings real-world insights from their careers.

    What's the time commitment for mentorship?

    Mentorship adds approximately 4-6 hours per week on top of the self-paced curriculum:

    • 1-2 hours of mentorship review sessions
    • 1 hour of personalized feedback review
    • 1-2 hours of live Q&A or office hours
    • 1 hour for portfolio and career guidance

    How does assignment feedback work?

    You submit weekly assignments through Google Classroom. Your mentor reviews your work within 48 hours and provides detailed feedback on architecture decisions, code quality, security considerations, and production readiness. Feedback includes specific improvement suggestions and optional extension challenges for advanced learners.

    Do I get 1-on-1 support?

    Yes. Each student gets scheduled 1-on-1 check-ins every 2-3 weeks, plus the ability to book additional sessions when needed. We also have daily async support through Slack and Google Classroom for quick questions.

    What if I'm stuck on a lab?

    Post your blockers in Google Classroom or Slack. Mentors respond within 24 hours with hints, debugging steps, and suggested fixes. We don't just give solutions; we guide you to understand and solve problems independently.

    Is mentorship included for the full 20 weeks?

    Yes. Mentorship is available for all 20 weeks. Each week includes assignment reviews, live Q&A, and milestone check-ins. For the capstone, you'll get extended review sessions and portfolio coaching to help you present your final project professionally.

    Can I pause or extend the mentorship?

    Yes. You can pause for up to 2 weeks if needed and resume without penalty. For longer extensions, we can add mentorship weeks at a discounted rate.

    Do I receive a certificate?

    Yes. Upon completing all assignments and the capstone project, you'll receive a certificate of completion and a detailed performance report from your mentor.

    Does mentorship help with job placement?

    Mentors provide resume feedback, mock interviews, and referrals to our hiring partner network. While we don't guarantee jobs, we do provide structured career support and interview preparation to help you succeed.

    Do you offer guarantees or money-back policies?

    We guarantee that if you complete all assignments with mentorship support and actively engage in feedback, you'll be job-ready for junior to mid-level DevOps roles. If after completing the course you don't feel prepared, reach out within 30 days and we'll provide additional support at no extra cost. We're confident in our curriculum because our mentors have trained hundreds of engineers who are now working at top tech companies.

    Questions not answered here?

    Email us at mentorship@hexadigitall.com or join our community Slack. Our team responds within 24 hours.

    Your Mentorship Learning Path

    With mentorship, each week follows a structured learning progression designed to build mastery at your pace:

    Week Start

    Pre-Class Bootcamp

    Complete pre-class exercises to activate prior knowledge. Your mentor checks your understanding and clarifies foundational concepts before the deeper content.

    Days 1-2

    Lecture Deep Dive

    Study detailed teaching notes at your pace. Attend or watch recorded lecture reviews where your mentor walks through key concepts and answers questions.

    Days 3-5

    Hands-On Labs

    Build real projects following lab instructions. Post questions in Google Classroom. Your mentor responds within 24 hours with guidance, not solutions.

    Days 6-7

    Mentorship Review

    Submit your assignment. Attend live mentorship review session for code walkthrough and feedback. Schedule 1-on-1 call if you need extra support.

    What You Get At Each Stage

    • Personalized Feedback: Not generic comments. Detailed analysis of your specific work with actionable improvement suggestions
    • Interview Preparation: Your mentor helps you articulate why you made design decisions and prepares you for technical discussions
    • Mentorship Checkpoint: A recorded checkpoint conversation (5 minutes) showing your mentor's assessment of your mastery level
    • Portfolio Guidance: Recommendations on how to present your work on GitHub and in your resume
    • Career Direction: Regular discussions about which areas align with your career goals. Cloud infra, platform engineering, SRE, etc.

    By Week 20, You'll Have:

    • ✅ 20 polished projects in your portfolio
    • ✅ Detailed feedback from industry experts on every assignment
    • ✅ Job-ready resume with mentorship guidance
    • ✅ Interview preparation sessions with your mentor
    • ✅ Confidence to discuss DevOps at a senior level
    • ✅ Connections with hiring partners in our ecosystem

    Ready to accelerate your DevOps career?

    Start Your Mentorship Journey
    📗

    The DevOps Interview Handbook

    Part 1: Psychology, Strategy, and The "Tell Me About Yourself" Trap

    Welcome to the final phase of your transformation. You have the technical skills—you can build clusters, write pipelines, and secure infrastructure. But getting hired isn't just about what you know; it's about how you communicate what you know.

    The technical interview is a performance. It requires a script, rehearsal, and the ability to improvise. In this first section, we decode the psychology of the interviewer, break down the standard interview lifecycle, and construct the perfect opening pitch.

    Preparation Objectives

    By the end of this module, you will be able to:

    Analyze

    Decode Intent: Understand exactly what hiring managers are looking for beyond technical correctness (Culture, Grit, Communication).

    Create

    The "Elevator Pitch": Craft a 2-minute professional summary that perfectly bridges your past experience with your new DevOps skills.

    Evaluate

    Red Flags: Identify behavior that instantly disqualifies candidates, regardless of their coding ability.

    1. The Mindset Game

    Most candidates fail because they treat the interview as an interrogation. They sit passively, waiting for questions to be fired at them. To succeed, you must treat the interview as a consultation. You are an engineer discussing a potential partnership.

    "A Senior Engineer doesn't just answer the question. They question the question. They ask for constraints. They discuss trade-offs. They treat the interviewer as a peer, not a professor."

    Decoding the Interviewer

    Let's look inside the brain of the person across the Zoom call. Understanding their hidden fears and motivations is your cheat code.

    • ❓ "Can they actually code?" or did they just memorize definitions from a tutorial?
    • ❓ "Are they safe?" If production goes down at 3 AM, will they panic and destroy data, or will they follow procedure?
    • ❓ "Are they arrogant?" Will I enjoy sitting next to this person for 40 hours a week, or will they argue about every code review?
    • ❓ "Do they understand 'Why'?" Do they know why we use Kubernetes, or just how to run `kubectl apply`?
    • ✅ Show, Don't Just Tell: Reference specific projects from this course (e.g., "In my Capstone, I faced this exact issue with ArgoCD...").
    • ✅ Admit Unknowns: "I haven't used Tool X specifically, but I know it's similar to Tool Y which I used for..."
    • ✅ Be Collaborative: Use "We" language. Ask for feedback during the solution process.
    • ✅ Focus on Value: Connect tools to business outcomes (e.g., "We used Terraform to reduce deployment time by 50%").

    2. The Anatomy of a Tech Interview

    Every standard DevOps interview follows a predictable structure. Knowing where you are in the timeline helps you control the pacing.

    Stage 1: The Ice Breaker (5 Minutes)

    Small talk. "How is your day?" "Tell me about yourself."
    Goal: Establish rapport. Show you are a normal, pleasant human being.

    Stage 2: The Resume Deep Dive (10-15 Minutes)

    "I see you listed Kubernetes here. Tell me about a complex deployment you managed."
    Goal: Verify that you actually did the work listed on your CV.

    Stage 3: The Technical Assessment (20-30 Minutes)

    System Design, Whiteboarding, or Live Debugging. "Design a CI/CD pipeline for a microservices app."
    Goal: Test problem-solving skills and depth of knowledge.

    Stage 4: Reverse Interview (5-10 Minutes)

    "Do you have any questions for us?"
    Goal: Judge your curiosity and interest in the company.

    3. Mastering "Tell Me About Yourself"

    This is the most important question. It sets the tone for the entire hour. If you ramble for 10 minutes about your childhood, you lose. If you are too brief, you seem inexperienced.

    The "Past-Present-Future" Framework

    Construct your answer using this 3-part structure. Keep it strictly under 2 minutes.

    1. The Past (The Hook): "I have a background in [Previous Field], where I developed strong skills in [Transferable Skill like logic, troubleshooting, or automation]."
    2. The Present (The Pivot): "I realized my passion was in Cloud Infrastructure, so I transitioned into DevOps. For the last 6 months, I've been architecting cloud-native solutions using Docker, Kubernetes, and Terraform. I recently completed an intensive platform engineering track where I built..."
    3. The Future (The Value): "I'm looking for a role where I can apply these skills to automate complex workflows, which is why I'm excited about [Company Name]'s work in [Their Industry]."

    📝 Exercise: Write Your Pitch

    Scenario: You are interviewing for a Junior DevOps role at a Fintech company.

    ❌ Bad Answer: "Um, well, I used to work in sales. Then I took a course. I know Linux and Docker. I'm looking for a job." (Too passive, low energy).

    ✅ Good Answer Template:
    "I started my career in [Sales/Admin/Support], where I learned the importance of [Process/Efficiency]. I found myself constantly trying to automate manual tasks, which led me to DevOps.

    Recently, I've been focused on building end-to-end cloud platforms. For example, I recently architected a microservices environment on AWS using EKS and Terraform, implementing a full GitOps pipeline with ArgoCD. I ensured it was secure by integrating Vault for dynamic secrets.

    I love the challenge of making infrastructure invisible to developers, and I know your team is scaling its Kubernetes footprint, so I'm eager to bring my automation experience to help stabilize that growth."

    4. Interview Etiquette: The Hard Rules

    Technical skills get you the interview. Soft skills get you the job.

    ✅ DO THIS

    • Clarify the Question: "Before I answer, are we optimizing for cost or performance? Is this a high-traffic system?"
    • Think Out Loud: Silence is deadly. Narrate your thought process. "I'm considering a Load Balancer here because..."
    • Admit Ignorance Gracefully: "I don't know the exact flag for that command off the top of my head, but I would check the `man` pages or docs."
    • Test Your Code: If you write a script, manually walk through it with a test case before saying "I'm done."

    ❌ DON'T DO THIS

    • Fake It: Never lie about your skills. Good interviewers will drill down until you break. It ruins your credibility.
    • Blame Tools/People: "My last boss was terrible" or "Windows sucks." Stay positive and professional.
    • Give One-Word Answers: Elaborate. Tell a story. Provide context.
    • Give Up: If you get stuck, say "I'm stuck on this logic. Can we step back? Here is what I am trying to achieve."
    ⚠️ The "Seniority" Trap

    Juniors try to prove they know how to use a tool.
    Seniors discuss when to use a tool (and when NOT to).

    To sound more senior, discuss trade-offs. "We could use Kubernetes here, but since it's a simple static site, S3 and CloudFront is cheaper and easier to maintain."

    ✅ Part 1 Checklist: Are You Ready?

    • Pitch Perfected: I have written and practiced my "Tell Me About Yourself" pitch (recorded and timed < 2 mins).
    • Environment Check: My webcam is at eye level, lighting is good, and the background is clean/professional.
    • Research: I have looked up the company's tech stack on StackShare or their engineering blog.
    • Mindset: I am ready to treat this as a peer-to-peer consultation, not an interrogation.

    Part 2: Behavioral Mastery & The STAR Method

    You can be a Kubernetes genius, but if you cannot communicate effectively or demonstrate emotional intelligence, you will not be hired.

    Behavioral interviews are designed to answer one question: "How does this person handle pressure?" In DevOps, where production outages cost millions, panic is expensive. This section teaches you how to structure your stories so you sound like a seasoned engineer, not a junior developer making excuses.

    Preparation Objectives

    Structure

    Master STAR: Learn the 4-step framework (Situation, Task, Action, Result) to prevent rambling during storytelling.

    Strategize

    The "Failure" Question: Transform your biggest mistakes into your strongest selling points by focusing on the "Post-Mortem."

    Persuade

    Data-Driven Results: Replace vague statements ("it was faster") with concrete metrics ("deployment time reduced by 40%").

    1. The STAR Method Blueprint

    When an interviewer asks "Tell me about a time you...", do not freestyle. Use the STAR framework. It provides a beginning, middle, and end to your story.

    S
    10% of Time

    Situation

    Set the scene briefly. What was the context?

    "We were migrating a legacy monolith to AWS, and our deployment process was taking 45 minutes manually."

    T
    10% of Time

    Task

    What was the specific challenge or goal?

    "My goal was to automate this pipeline to reduce errors and cut deployment time to under 10 minutes."

    A
    60% of Time

    Action

    The most important part. What did YOU do? Use "I", not "We".

    "I containerized the app using Docker, wrote a Terraform script to provision EKS, and built a Jenkins pipeline..."

    R
    20% of Time

    Result

    The happy ending. Use numbers.

    "This reduced deployment time by 78%, eliminated manual errors, and allowed the team to deploy 5x a day."

    2. Navigating the "Trap" Questions

    Interviewers ask specific questions to see if you blame others, hide mistakes, or lack self-awareness. Here is how to answer the most dangerous ones.

    Scenario A: "Tell me about a time you failed."

    • ❌ Do they hide their mistakes? (Honesty Check)
    • ❌ Do they blame the tool or the team? (Ownership Check)
    • ❌ Did they learn from it? (Growth Mindset)
    • ✅ Pick a real mistake (not "I worked too hard").
    • ✅ Focus on the Post-Mortem. How did you fix the process?
    • ✅ Show the system fix you implemented so it never happened again.
    ❌ Weak Answer

    "I once deployed a bug to production because the QA team didn't test it properly. It wasn't really my fault, but I helped them fix it."

    Analysis: Blaming the QA team is an instant fail. It shows a lack of ownership and teamwork.
    ✅ Senior Answer

    "In a previous project, I manually updated a security group rule and accidentally locked our team out of the database for 20 minutes.

    I immediately contacted the senior engineer to help restore access. Afterward, I realized that manual updates were the root cause. I implemented a Terraform pipeline to manage security groups via code, ensuring all changes were peer-reviewed before application. We never had that issue again."

    Analysis: Admits the mistake, fixes the immediate issue, and implements a systemic solution (IaC).

    Scenario B: "Tell me about a conflict with a coworker."

    "Never make the conflict about personality. Make it about technical approach."
    ✅ Senior Answer

    "My teammate wanted to use Jenkins for our new pipeline because he was familiar with it, but I advocated for GitHub Actions because our code was already in GitHub and it offered better concurrency.

    Instead of arguing opinions, I built a small Proof of Concept (PoC) in GitHub Actions to demonstrate the speed difference. Once he saw the data—that builds were 30% faster—he agreed to the switch. We paired on the migration so he could learn the new tool."

    Analysis: Data-driven persuasion + Empathy/Collaboration.

    3. Questions YOU Should Ask

    At the end, they will ask, "Do you have questions for us?" Saying "No" signals a lack of interest. Asking smart questions signals seniority.

    ✅ ASK THESE

    • "How do you handle incident response? Do you have a blameless post-mortem culture?"
    • "What is the ratio of operational work (toil) vs. new project work for this team?"
    • "How often do you deploy to production, and what is the failure rate?"
    • "What does success look like in the first 90 days of this role?"

    ❌ AVOID THESE

    • "What does your company do?" (You should already know).
    • "How soon can I take a vacation?" (Save for HR).
    • "Do I have to be on-call?" (Assume yes in DevOps; ask how it's managed instead).
    • "How much is the salary?" (Wait for the offer stage).

    ✅ Part 2 Checklist: Story Bank

    Prepare 3 distinct stories from your coursework/projects that fit these themes:

    • The Technical Challenge: A time I solved a hard bug (e.g., OOMKilled in Kubernetes).
    • The Mistake: A time I broke something and fixed it systemically.
    • The Efficiency Win: A time I automated a manual task (e.g., Terraform vs Manual ClickOps).
    Part 3: System Design & The "Safety Architecture"

    In Stage 3 of the interview, the interviewer will say: "Design a highly available web application on AWS." Then, they will hand you a marker (or a Google Drawing link) and stare at you.

    Do not panic.

    You do not need to invent a new cloud provider. You just need to implement the standard, boring, reliable architecture you learned in this course. This section gives you a "Cheat Sheet" architecture that solves 80% of system design questions.

    Preparation Objectives

    Apply

    The "Safety" Pattern: Memorize the standard 3-Tier VPC architecture (Public/Private subnets, ALB, ASG/EKS, RDS) so you can draw it in your sleep.

    Analyze

    Requirement Gathering: Learn the 3 questions you MUST ask before you draw a single line (Traffic, Storage, Availability).

    Evaluate

    Trade-offs: Explain why you chose a specific database or load balancer strategy (Consistency vs. Availability).

    1. The "Pause and Pivot" Strategy

    The biggest mistake candidates make is drawing immediately. If you start drawing a MySQL database before asking how much data they have, you fail.

    • ❓ "Do they jump to conclusions?" Are they assuming we need a massive cluster for a tiny app?
    • ❓ "Do they know the limits?" Do they know when a relational DB will crash?
    • ❓ "Are they security-conscious?" Did they put the database in a public subnet? (Instant fail).
    • ✅ Pause: "Before I design this, I have a few clarifying questions."
    • ✅ Pivot: "Based on that traffic volume, I'm going to suggest a horizontal scaling approach."
    • ✅ Security First: "I'm drawing the VPC boundary first because isolation is our priority."

    2. The "Safety Architecture"

    Memorize this pattern. Unless the interviewer explicitly asks for "Serverless" or "Edge Computing," this is your default answer. It works for 99% of "Design a Web App" questions.

    🌐

    Layer 1: Entry

    • DNS: Route53 (Global).
    • CDN: CloudFront (Cache static assets).
    • WAF: Web App Firewall (Block DDoS/SQLi).
    • ALB: Application Load Balancer (Public Subnet).
    ⚙️

    Layer 2: Compute

    • VPC: The boundary.
    • Private Subnets: Where apps live.
    • EKS / ASG: Autoscaling Group or Kubernetes.
    • Multi-AZ: Spread across AZ-A and AZ-B for HA.
    💾

    Layer 3: Data

    • RDS / Aurora: Primary DB (Private Subnet).
    • Standby: Read Replica/Failover in AZ-B.
    • Cache: Redis/ElastiCache (for speed).
    • S3: Object storage for files.

    3. Simulation: "Design Instagram"

    Let's walk through a real interview scenario. Observe the dialogue carefully.

    🗣️ INTERVIEWER

    "I want you to design a photo-sharing application like Instagram. It needs to handle image uploads and a news feed."

    ❌ Junior Response

    "Okay! I'll put a React app on S3, and then I'll use Python for the backend, and maybe MongoDB for the images. Oh, and Kubernetes."

    Critique: You assumed the stack without asking about scale. Storing images in MongoDB is usually a bad idea (use Object Storage/S3). You didn't mention networking or security.
    ✅ Senior Response

    "Interesting problem. Before I choose the stack, I need to understand the constraints:
    1. Scale: Are we designing for 100 users or 100 million?
    2. Read/Write Ratio: Is this read-heavy (viewing photos) or write-heavy (uploading)?
    3. Availability: Can we tolerate downtime, or do we need 99.99%?"

    Critique: This shows you think about Architecture, not just Tools.

    4. Drawing the Solution

    Once you have the requirements (e.g., "High Scale, Read Heavy"), you start drawing. Narrate as you draw.

    > DRAWING: High-Scale Web Architecture
    Step 1: The Boundary "I'm starting with a VPC in a specific Region. I'll define 3 Availability Zones (AZs) for High Availability."
    Step 2: The Network Tier "I'm placing Public Subnets here for the Load Balancer and NAT Gateway. The NAT Gateway allows our private instances to patch without being exposed to the internet."
    Step 3: The Compute Tier "In the Private Subnets, I'm placing our EKS Worker Nodes. I'll use a Horizontal Pod Autoscaler (HPA) to scale based on CPU usage."
    Step 4: The Data Tier (The Critical Part) "For the metadata (users, comments), I'll use RDS Postgres with a Multi-AZ Standby for disaster recovery. For the actual images, we NEVER store files in the DB. I'll use S3 with CloudFront to cache them at the edge."
    "If you get stuck, default to Decoupling. If the app is slow, suggest adding a Queue (SQS/Kafka) between the web server and the worker."

    ✅ Part 3 Checklist: System Design

    Can you draw these 3 patterns from memory?

    • The Standard Web App: ALB -> ASG/EKS -> RDS (Multi-AZ).
    • The CI/CD Pipeline: Git -> Jenkins/Actions -> Docker Build -> Scan -> Deploy.
    • The Decoupled Worker: API -> SQS Queue -> Worker (Lambda/Pod) -> DB.

    Part 4: Live Coding, Scripting, and the "Broken Server"

    Stage 4 is where the theory ends and the keyboard begins. The interviewer will share their screen (or ask you to share yours) and present a broken system or a blank text editor.

    The Goal: They don't just want the right answer; they want to see your investigative process. Do you check logs before restarting? Do you Google error messages efficiently? Do you write readable code or spaghetti scripts? This section trains you to think like a debugger.

    Preparation Objectives

    Apply

    The USE Method: Master the standard troubleshooting framework (Utilization, Saturation, Errors) to debug any system, even one you've never seen before.

    Create

    Practical Scripting: Write a Python/Bash script in under 10 minutes to solve a real-world task (e.g., "Parse this log file and count 500 errors").

    Analyze

    Linux Internals: Diagnose "Slow" servers using `top`, `iostat`, `netstat`, and `ps`.

    1. The "502 Bad Gateway" Challenge

    The Prompt: "Users are reporting a 502 error on our Nginx load balancer. You have SSH access. Go."
    Most candidates immediately type `restart nginx`. This is a fail. You destroyed the evidence (logs) and didn't find the root cause.

    • ❓ "Do they panic?" Do they start typing random commands (`reboot`, `killall`) without looking?
    • ❓ "Do they verify?" Do they confirm the error exists before trying to fix it?
    • ❓ "Do they know the layers?" Do they check the Network, then the OS, then the App?
    • ✅ Verify: `curl -v localhost` to confirm the 502.
    • ✅ Logs: `tail -f /var/log/nginx/error.log` to see why.
    • ✅ Upstream: 502 usually means the backend is down, not Nginx itself. Check the backend service.
    The Troubleshooting Checklist (Memorize This)
    1. Is it up? (`ping`, `curl`)
    2. Is it listening? (`netstat -tulpn`, `ss -lntp`)
    3. Is it blocked? (`iptables -L`, Security Groups)
    4. Is it overloaded? (`top`, `htop`, `df -h` for disk space)
    5. What do logs say? (`journalctl -u service-name`, `/var/log/...`)

    2. The Scripting Challenge

    DevOps engineers write glue code. You will likely be asked to write a script in Python or Bash.
    Common Task: "Here is a log file. Count the number of requests per IP address."

    Option A: The Bash Way

    Fast, one-liner, great for text processing.

    cat access.log | \ awk '{print $1}' | \ sort | \ uniq -c | \ sort -nr | \ head -n 10

    Explanation: Extract column 1 (IP), sort them (required for uniq), count unique occurrences, sort numerically reverse, show top 10.

    Option B: The Python Way

    Readable, logic-heavy, better for APIs/JSON.

    counts = {} with open('access.log') as f: for line in f: ip = line.split()[0] counts[ip] = counts.get(ip, 0) + 1 # Sort and print sorted_ips = sorted(counts.items(), key=lambda x: x[1], reverse=True) print(sorted_ips[:10])
    "It doesn't matter which language you choose. What matters is that you handle Edge Cases. What if the file doesn't exist? What if the line is empty? Mentioning these makes you look Senior."

    3. The "CrashLoopBackOff" Scenario

    The Prompt: "I deployed a pod, but it keeps crashing. Fix it."

    Step 1: Check Status `kubectl get pods` -> See "CrashLoopBackOff".
    Step 2: Describe (The Config) `kubectl describe pod ` -> Look at "Events" at the bottom.
    Common errors: "OOMKilled" (Memory limit too low), "ImagePullBackOff" (Typo in image name or auth error).
    Step 3: Logs (The App) `kubectl logs --previous` -> Why did it die last time?
    Common errors: "panic: connection refused" (DB is down), "FileNotFound" (Volume not mounted).

    ✅ Part 4 Checklist: Hands-On Ready

    Before your interview, can you do these tasks without Googling?

    • Linux: Find the process using port 8080 (`netstat` or `lsof`) and kill it.
    • Text Processing: Use `grep` and `sed` to find and replace a string in a file.
    • Docker: Write a basic `Dockerfile` (FROM, RUN, CMD) for a Python app.
    • HTTP: Know your status codes (200, 301, 401, 403, 500, 502, 503, 504).

    Part 5: The Final Polish, Negotiation, and "Game Day"

    You have the technical knowledge, the behavioral stories, and the system design patterns. Now it is time to bring it all together.

    In this final section, we move from "Learning" to "Rehearsing." We will cover the final logistics of the interview day, provide a self-assessment rubric to grade your own performance, and give you the rules of engagement for the salary negotiation phase.

    Preparation Objectives

    Evaluate

    Self-Mock Interview: Run a complete 60-minute simulation and grade yourself against a strict rubric.

    Apply

    Logistical Readiness: Execute the "Game Day" checklist to ensure zero technical glitches during the call.

    Create

    Negotiation Strategy: Prepare your "Walk Away" number and understand how to anchor salary expectations.

    1. The Self-Assessment Rubric

    Record yourself answering questions. Watch it back. It will be painful, but it is the fastest way to improve. Grade yourself using this "Hiring Committee" rubric.

    Category Signals a "NO HIRE" ❌ Signals a "HIRE" ✅
    Communication Rambles; Uses filler words ("um, like"); Interrupts interviewer; Low energy. Structured answers (STAR); Pauses to think; Checks for understanding ("Did that answer your question?"); High energy.
    Technical Depth Uses buzzwords incorrectly; Cannot explain "Why"; Guesses instead of admitting ignorance. Discusses trade-offs; Explains underlying concepts (e.g., DNS, TCP); Admits what they don't know but explains how they'd find out.
    Problem Solving Gives up immediately; Blames tools; Jumps to code without a plan. Breaks problem into small steps; Verbalizes thought process; Tests the solution; Debugs systematically.
    Culture Fit Arrogant; Defensive about mistakes; "Not my job" attitude. Collaborative ("We"); Takes ownership of failures; Curious about the team; teachable.

    2. The "Game Day" Protocol

    Do not let a bad internet connection ruin 20 weeks of hard work. Treat the interview like a Production Deployment.

    🚀 T-Minus 1 Hour Checklist

    • Network Check: Run `speedtest.net`. If unstable, tether to phone hotspot immediately.
    • Audio/Video Check: Open Zoom/Teams settings. Test mic input. Ensure you are not backlit (light source should be in front of you).
    • Clean Desktop: Close all tabs except: Your Resume, The Job Description, and a Blank Text Editor. Turn off notifications (Slack/Discord).
    • Water: Have a glass of water nearby. Nervousness causes dry mouth.
    • Pen & Paper: For quick architectural sketches or noting down the interviewer's name.

    3. The Negotiation (Bonus)

    Once you pass the technical gauntlet, HR will call with an offer. This is a business transaction.

    HR's Mindset:

    • "We have a budget range ($100k - $130k)."
    • "We want to get them for the lowest fair price."
    • "We expect them to negotiate."

    Your Strategy:

    • ✅ Never give a number first. "I'm open to market rates for a role with this responsibility."
    • ✅ Anchor High. If forced, give a range where the bottom is your target.
    • ✅ Pause. When they give a number, be silent for 5 seconds. Silence makes people want to fill the gap (often with a better number).
    ✅ The "Counter-Offer" Script

    "Thank you for the offer. I'm really excited about the team and the technology.

    However, looking at the market for Senior DevOps roles and considering the scope of responsibilities here, I was expecting something closer to [Target + 10%].

    If we can get to that number, I would be ready to sign today."

    🎓 You Are Ready.

    This handbook is not just about passing a test. It is about becoming the engineer that companies fight to hire.

    You know the technology. You know the stories. You know the design patterns.
    Walk into that room with confidence. You are not asking for a job; you are offering a solution to their problems.

    Good luck. Go build the future.
